topics.title: Introduction to Mobile Robots and Mobile Manipulators, Principle of Locomotion and Types of Locomotion, Types of Mobile Robots: Ground Robots (Wheeled and Legged Robots), Aerial Robots, Underwater Robots and Water Surface Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT723 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Mobile Robots and Mobile Manipulators, Principle of Locomotion and Types of Locomotion, Types of Mobile Robots: Ground Robots (Wheeled and Legged Robots), Aerial Robots, Underwater Robots and Water Surface Robots # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Mobile Robots — Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mobile Robot:** A mobile robot is an automatically controlled, self-propelled mechanism capable of locomotion through a non-fixed, partially known or unknown environment. Unlike a manipulator arm (which is fixed to a base), a mobile robot possesses a **locomotion subsystem** (wheels, legs, fins, rotors, etc.) that allows it to traverse its workspace. A mobile robot is characterized by an integrated triad of subsystems: 1. **Locomotion Subsystem** — the physical mechanism (wheels, legs, propellers) that interacts with the environment to generate motion. 2. **Sensing / Perception Subsystem** — onboard sensors (LIDAR, IMU, cameras, sonars) for situational awareness. 3. **Control / Decision Subsystem** — the CPU/processor running planning, SLAM, and control algorithms. > [!NOTE] > **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** A mobile robot operating in 2D plane typically has **3 DOF** (x, y, θ) — two translations and one rotation. In 3D space (aerial/underwater), it has **6 DOF** (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw). The number is the **dimension of its configuration space (C-space)**. ## 1.2 Mobile Manipulators — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mobile Manipulator:** A mobile manipulator is a hybrid robotic system that combines a **mobile base** (for gross workspace coverage) with a **manipulator arm** (for dexterous task execution). It inherits the workspace of a mobile robot and the dexterity of a fixed-base manipulator. **Real-world analogy:** Think of a **human** — the legs are the mobile base (locomotion), and the arms are the manipulator (dexterous interaction). A factory robot that bolts doors onto a car on a moving assembly line is a mobile manipulator. **Intuition:** A fixed manipulator has infinite dexterity but a tiny workspace (the size of a desk). A mobile robot has infinite workspace but zero dexterity. A **mobile manipulator = infinite workspace × reasonable dexterity**. The trade-off is **complexity of coordinated control** (the base moves the arm). ## 1.3 Principle of Locomotion — Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Locomotion** is the act of self-propelled movement from one place to another. In robotics, it is achieved by converting stored energy (electrical, chemical) into **propulsive thrust or torque** via a **propulsive organ** (wheels, legs, fins, rotors). **Analogy — The Human Walker:** A human walking generates forward motion by repeatedly pushing against the ground with their feet. The ground provides an **equal and opposite reaction force** (Newton's Third Law). The friction between the foot and the ground prevents slipping. A legged robot operates on the *exact same principle* — it pushes on the terrain, and the terrain pushes back. A **fish** achieves locomotion by pushing water backward with its tail; a **drone** pushes air downward with its propellers. In all cases, the underlying physics is **conservation of momentum and Newton's Third Law**. > [!NOTE] > **Specific Resistance (ε):** A key dimensionless metric for comparing locomotion efficiency across robot types. Lower is better. > $$\varepsilon = \frac{P}{mgv}$$ > where $P$ is propulsive power, $m$ is mass, $g$ is gravity, $v$ is velocity. Typical values: **0.05 (rolling)** < **0.2 (walking)** < **2.0 (hovering)**. ## 1.4 Types of Locomotion (High-Level Classification) | Locomotion Type | Medium | Propulsive Organ | Example Robot | |---|---|---|---| | **Wheeled** | Solid ground | Wheels | TurtleBot, KUKA youBot | | **Tracked** | Solid/rough ground | Continuous tracks | Mars Rover, PackBot | | **Legged** | Solid ground | Articulated legs | Boston Dynamics Spot, ASIMO | | **Aerial (Flying)** | Air | Rotors / wings | Quadcopter, fixed-wing UAV | | **Aquatic (Swimming)** | Water | Fins / propellers | AUV, robotic fish | | **Surface (Floating)** | Water surface | Paddle wheels / thrusters | ASV, USV | --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Underlying Locomotion The design of any mobile robot must satisfy three fundamental mechanical principles: ### A. Stability The robot must not tip over. Two stability frameworks exist: 1. **Static Stability** — robot is stable *at any instant*, even when stopped. Requires the **Center of Gravity (CoG)** projection to lie strictly inside the support polygon. Required for **legged robots** on uneven terrain. 2. **Dynamic Stability** — robot is stable *over time* if it does not fall during the locomotion cycle. Allows CoG to leave the support polygon **momentarily** (e.g., a walking human). Used for **highly dynamic systems** (hopping, running, flying). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Support Polygon (Stability Margin):** The convex hull of all ground-contact points. For a 4-legged standing robot, it is a quadrilateral. For a 3-wheeled robot, a triangle. The robot is **statically stable** iff the vertical projection of the CoG lies *inside* this polygon. ### B. Energy Efficiency Mobile robots carry finite onboard energy (batteries). Two key metrics: - **Specific Resistance (ε)** — already defined in Section 1.3. - **Cost of Transport (CoT):** $$\text{CoT} = \frac{E}{mgd}$$ where $E$ is energy consumed to travel distance $d$. ### C. Traction / Propulsion The interface between the propulsive organ and the environment must provide sufficient **friction force** (ground) or **thrust** (air/water) to overcome weight and drag. ## 2.2 Wheeled Locomotion — Deep Analysis Wheels are the **simplest, most efficient, and most common** locomotion mechanism. ### Wheel Types (KTU high-yield) | Type | Configuration | Features | |---|---|---| | **Standard (Fixed)** | Two wheels, fixed direction | Simple, no steering (uses differential drive) | | **Caster** | Swivel wheel | No traction, only support | | **Swedish / Mecanum** | 45° peripheral rollers | Omni-directional motion | | **Omnidirectional (3 or 4 wheel)** | Mecanum / orthogonal | Holonomic motion | ### Differential Drive Kinematics (KTU High-Yield) A differential drive robot has two independently driven wheels on a common axis. Let: - $r$ = wheel radius - $L$ = distance between wheels - $v_R, v_L$ = right and left wheel linear velocities - $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = left and right wheel angular velocities **Forward Kinematics:** $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} \quad \text{(linear velocity of robot center)}$$ $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \quad \text{(angular velocity of robot)}$$ **Inverse Kinematics:** $$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}, \quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$$ > [!NOTE] > A differential drive is **non-holonomic** — it can achieve any $(x, y, \theta)$ in its C-space, but cannot instantaneously move sideways. This is the **most-tested concept** in KTU exams. ## 2.3 Legged Locomotion — Deep Analysis Legged robots mimic biological systems. The number of legs governs the stability of *static walking*: | Number of Legs | Static Walking | Examples (biological / robotic) | |---|---|---| | 1 (Hopper) | ✗ Impossible | Single-leg hopper | | 2 (Biped) | ✗ Impossible | Human, ASIMO, Atlas | | 3 (Triped) | Marginal | — | | 4 (Quadruped) | ✓ Possible (3 legs on ground) | Dog, Spot, ANYmal | | 6 (Hexapod) | ✓ Easy (5 legs on ground) | Insects, hexapod lab robots | | 8+ | ✓ Very easy | Spiders, octopods | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gait:** A gait is a periodic, coordinated pattern of leg lift/swing/stance events. Common gaits: > - **Wave gait** (slow, stable, 8+ legs) > - **Tripod gait** (alternating sets of 3, fast, used by insects) > - **Trot / Pace / Bound** (quadrupeds, dynamic gaits) ## 2.4 Aerial, Aquatic, and Surface Locomotion ### Aerial - **Aerodynamic lift** must equal weight: $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 S C_L = mg$. - For rotorcraft: thrust is generated by propeller disk momentum theory. - For fixed-wing: lift is generated by forward airspeed over an airfoil. ### Aquatic (Swimming) - **Thrust** is produced by fins, oscillating foils, or propellers. - Drag force: $F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$ (must be overcome by thrust). ### Surface (Floating) - Hybrid between ground and aquatic — the robot floats on water but uses wheels, paddles, or thrusters. - Buoyancy equation: $\rho_{\text{water}} V_{\text{submerged}} g = mg$. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\varepsilon = \frac{P}{mgv}$ | Specific resistance | dimensionless | | 2 | $\text{CoT} = \frac{E}{mgd}$ | Cost of transport | dimensionless | | 3 | $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | Diff-drive linear velocity | m/s | | 4 | $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | Diff-drive angular velocity | rad/s | | 5 | $\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$ | Right wheel angular velocity (IK) | rad/s | | 6 | $\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$ | Left wheel angular velocity (IK) | rad/s | | 7 | $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 S C_L$ | Aerodynamic lift | N | | 8 | $F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$ | Drag force | N | | 9 | $F = \rho_{\text{fluid}} V_{\text{submerged}} g$ | Buoyancy force | N | | 10 | $T_{\text{net}} = I\dot{\omega}$ | Torque-acceleration (wheel drive) | N·m | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** In the differential drive formulas, students frequently **swap $L$ and $r$** or use the *wheelbase* instead of the *track width*. The $L$ in the formula refers to the **distance between the two driving wheels** (track width), NOT the wheel radius. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - Differential drive kinematics are foundational for **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva bots). - Specific resistance guides design choices in **planetary rovers** (NASA uses tracks, not legs, on Mars for lower ε). - Lift/drag equations drive the design of **drones, submarines, and surface vessels** in aerospace and naval engineering. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Forward Kinematics We derive the linear and angular velocities of a differential drive robot from wheel angular velocities. ### Setup Consider a robot with two drive wheels on a common axle. The wheel centers are separated by distance $L$ (track width). Each wheel has radius $r$ and rotates with angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ about the wheel hub (local to each wheel). ### Step 1 — Linear Velocity of Each Wheel's Contact Point The contact point of the right wheel with the ground has linear velocity: $$v_R = r \, \omega_R$$ The contact point of the left wheel has linear velocity: $$v_L = r \, \omega_L$$ These velocities are measured along the forward direction of the robot (tangent to the wheel rolling). ### Step 2 — Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) Because the two wheels are rigidly connected, the robot executes a **rigid-body motion** in the plane. Such motion is always a rotation (or translation) about an *Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)*. Let the ICR be at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the right wheel and $R + L$ from the left wheel (since the left wheel is *behind* the right wheel in the direction of turning when $\omega_R > \omega_L$). By rigid-body rotation: $$v_R = \omega \cdot R \quad \text{and} \quad v_L = \omega \cdot (R + L)$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for $R$ and $\omega$ Subtract the two equations: $$v_R - v_L = \omega \cdot R - \omega \cdot (R + L) = -\omega L$$ $$\omega = \frac{v_L - v_R}{L} = \frac{r\omega_L - r\omega_R}{L}$$ Note the sign: positive $\omega$ corresponds to a *clockwise* rotation (in standard 2D convention). To match the conventional form, we write: $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ The linear velocity of the robot's geometric center (midpoint between the two wheels) is at distance $R + L/2$ from the ICR: $$v = \omega \cdot \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right)$$ Using $v_R = \omega R$, we get $R = v_R/\omega$, so: $$v = \omega R + \frac{\omega L}{2} = v_R + \frac{v_L - v_R}{2} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$ Substituting $v_R = r\omega_R$ and $v_L = r\omega_L$: $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Form $$\boxed{\; v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} \quad,\quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \;}$$ ### Step 5 — Inverse Kinematics (Derivation) We are given desired $(v, \omega)$ and must find $(\omega_R, \omega_L)$. From the two equations, solve the linear system: $$\omega_R + \omega_L = \frac{2v}{r}$$ $$\omega_R - \omega_L = \frac{L\omega}{r}$$ Adding and subtracting: $$\boxed{\; \omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r} \quad,\quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r} \;}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel separation $L = 0.30$ m. The right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s and the left wheel at $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s. Find (a) the linear velocity of the robot, (b) the angular velocity, and (c) the radius of curvature of the path. **Solution:** ### Part (a) — Linear velocity $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (10 + 6)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 16}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$ ### Part (b) — Angular velocity $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (10 - 6)}{0.30} = \frac{0.05 \times 4}{0.30} = \frac{0.20}{0.30} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \text{ rad/s}$$ ### Part (c) — Radius of curvature For a rigid body in pure rotation, $v = \omega \cdot R$: $$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.4}{0.667} \approx 0.6 \text{ m}$$ The robot traces a circular arc of radius **0.6 m**, turning to the left (since $\omega_R > \omega_L$). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Locomotion Comparison | Property | Wheeled | Tracked | Legged (4-leg) | Aerial (Quad) | Aquatic (AUV) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Specific Resistance ε** | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 2.0 (hover) | 0.3 | | **Stability** | Static | Static | Static (walk) | Dynamic | Dynamic | | **Terrain adaptability** | Low | Medium | Very high | Air only | Water only | | **Mechanical complexity** | Low | Medium | High | High | High | | **Control complexity** | Low | Low | Very high | Very high | High | | **Energy efficiency** | Highest | High | Low | Lowest | Medium | | **Speed potential** | High | Medium | Low–Medium | High | Medium | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mobile Robot Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Energy Source
Battery / Fuel Cell] --> B[Control Unit
Microcontroller / SBC] B --> C1[Locomotion Subsystem] B --> C2[Perception Subsystem] B --> C3[Communication Subsystem] C1 --> D1{Wheels / Legs / Rotors / Fins} D1 --> E1[Environment
Ground / Air / Water] C2 --> D2[LIDAR / IMU / Camera / Sonar] D2 --> B C3 --> D3[Radio / WiFi / Acoustic Modem] D3 --> F[Remote Operator / Fleet Manager] B --> G[Path Planner / SLAM Module] G --> B E1 -->|Feedback Forces| D1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Classification — Types of Mobile Robots ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Mobile Robots] R --> G[Ground Robots] R --> A[Aerial Robots] R --> U[Underwater Robots] R --> S[Water Surface Robots] G --> G1[Wheeled Robots] G --> G2[Legged Robots] G --> G3[Tracked Robots] G1 --> G1a[Differential Drive] G1 --> G1b[Ackermann Steering] G1 --> G1c[Omnidirectional Mecanum] G2 --> G2a[Biped Humanoid] G2 --> G2b[Quadruped] G2 --> G2c[Hexapod] A --> A1[Rotary Wing Quadcopter] A --> A2[Fixed Wing UAV] A --> A3[Hybrid VTOL] U --> U1[Remotely Operated Vehicle ROV] U --> U2[Autonomous Underwater Vehicle AUV] U --> U3[Bio Inspired Robotic Fish] S --> S1[Autonomous Surface Vehicle ASV] S --> S2[USV with Manipulator Arm] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Locomotion Principle Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Principle of Locomotion] --> P1[Newton Third Law Action Reaction] P --> P2[Energy Conversion Electrical to Kinetic] P --> P3[Fluid / Ground Interaction] P1 --> P1a[Wheel Pushes Ground Backward] P1 --> P1b[Ground Pushes Wheel Forward] P1 --> P1c[Rotor Pushes Air Down] P1 --> P1d[Air Pushes Rotor Up] P2 --> P2a[Motor Torque Applied] P2 --> P2b[Thrust or Wheel Rotation Generated] P3 --> P3a[Friction Coefficient mu] P3 --> P3b[Drag Coefficient CD] P3 --> P3c[Lift Coefficient CL] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `G1a`) and labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown tags — this complies with the Mermaid safety rules specified in the system protocol. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a **mobile manipulator**. How does it differ from a fixed-base manipulator and a stand-alone mobile robot? Mention one real-world application. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **mobile manipulator** is a robotic system that integrates a **mobile base** (for locomotion) with a **manipulator arm** (for task execution) on the same platform. (1 mark) - A fixed-base manipulator has a **limited, fixed workspace** but high dexterity; a mobile robot has an **infinite workspace** but no dexterity. A mobile manipulator **combines both** — large workspace plus dexterity. (1 mark) - Real-world application: A **wheeled service robot** in a hospital that navigates corridors to deliver medicines and uses its arm to pick up medicine packets. (1 mark) ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is **specific resistance (ε)** in mobile robot locomotion? What is its significance? State the typical range for wheeled vs. legged robots. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - Specific resistance is a **dimensionless metric** that measures the propulsive power required per unit weight to travel at unit velocity: $\varepsilon = P / (mgv)$. (1 mark) - Significance: it **enables fair comparison** of locomotion efficiency across robot types, regardless of size or mass. (1 mark) - Typical values: **wheeled robots** $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$, **legged robots** $\varepsilon \approx 0.2$. Lower is more efficient, so wheels are mechanically more efficient than legs. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern Note:** Per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) guidelines, Part B questions in Module 1 carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Choice A]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Principle of Locomotion** in mobile robots. Discuss the various **types of locomotion** with suitable examples. (7 marks) **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a mobile robot**. List and briefly explain the four major subsystems. (7 marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Principle of Locomotion & Types (7 marks):** The **principle of locomotion** is governed by Newton's Third Law and conservation of momentum. A mobile robot moves by **applying a force to its environment** (ground, air, or water) and exploiting the **equal and opposite reaction force** that propels it forward. For this to work, the robot must have: 1. A **propulsive organ** — wheel, leg, fin, rotor — that interacts with the medium. 2. A **stable support** — sufficient friction (ground) or fluid dynamic forces (air/water). 3. An **energy source** — to power the propulsion. **Types of Locomotion (with examples):** | # | Type | Medium | Example | Key Feature | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Wheeled** | Ground | TurtleBot, KUKA youBot | Most efficient, simple, limited terrain | | 2 | **Tracked** | Ground | Mars Rover | Better traction, low ε | | 3 | **Legged** | Ground | Spot, ASIMO | Best for uneven terrain, high ε | | 4 | **Aerial** | Air | Quadcopter, Predator UAV | 6-DOF, no ground constraints | | 5 | **Underwater** | Water | AUV, ROV | Buoyancy + thrust | | 6 | **Surface** | Water | ASV | Floats, low mass needed | (5 marks for explanation; 2 marks for diagram or table) **Part (b) — Functional Architecture (7 marks):** The functional architecture of a mobile robot consists of four interconnected subsystems: 1. **Locomotion Subsystem** — wheels, motors, gear trains, drive electronics. Converts electrical energy into mechanical motion. 2. **Perception Subsystem** — LIDAR, cameras, IMU, GPS, ultrasonic sensors. Provides state estimation and environment mapping. 3. **Control / Decision Subsystem** — on-board computer (Raspberry Pi, Jetson, etc.) running algorithms: SLAM, path planning, control laws. 4. **Communication Subsystem** — WiFi, Bluetooth, radio modems. Allows remote command, fleet management, and data telemetry. The subsystems are **interconnected**: the controller issues motion commands to locomotion, fuses perception data, and exchanges information with the operator via communication. (Full credit for block diagram + 4 subsystem explanations.) #### Examiner's Allocation: - 1 mark — definition and Newton's Third Law - 2 marks — types of locomotion (table/diagram) - 2 marks — examples and characteristics - 1 mark — architecture diagram - 1 mark — four subsystems --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Choice B]** **(a)** Differentiate between **wheeled and legged mobile robots** in terms of stability, energy efficiency, terrain adaptability, and mechanical complexity. (7 marks) **(b)** A differential drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m and wheel separation $L = 0.25$ m. If the right wheel rotates at $8$ rad/s and the left wheel at $5$ rad/s, determine: (i) the linear velocity of the robot, (ii) the angular velocity, and (iii) the radius of curvature of its path. (7 marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Wheeled vs Legged Comparison (7 marks):** | Criterion | Wheeled Robot | Legged Robot | |---|---|---| | **Stability** | Always statically stable (3+ wheels on ground) | Requires careful gait design; static stability needs ≥ 4 legs | | **Energy Efficiency** | High (low ε ≈ 0.05); rolling friction is small | Low (ε ≈ 0.2); lifting body against gravity repeatedly | | **Terrain Adaptability** | Poor on rough, uneven terrain; excels on flat ground | Excellent; can step over obstacles, climb stairs, walk on irregular surfaces | | **Mechanical Complexity** | Low (1–2 motors) | High (≥ 12 actuators for quadruped, more for humanoids) | | **Control Complexity** | Low (kinematics well-understood) | Very high (gait planning, balance, contact dynamics) | | **Speed** | High on smooth surfaces | Limited by gait cycle and leg dynamics | | **Application** | Warehouses, AGVs, indoor service | Search & rescue, exploration, military, planetary | (1 mark per row, capped at 7 rows × 1 = 7 marks. Or 2 marks per major criterion × 3 criteria + 1 mark for application example = 7 marks.) **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.25$ m, $\omega_R = 8$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 5$ rad/s. **Step 1 — Linear velocity of the robot** (using $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$): $$v = \frac{0.04 \times (8 + 5)}{2} = \frac{0.04 \times 13}{2} = \frac{0.52}{2} = 0.26 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Angular velocity** (using $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$): $$\omega = \frac{0.04 \times (8 - 5)}{0.25} = \frac{0.04 \times 3}{0.25} = \frac{0.12}{0.25} = 0.48 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 3 — Radius of curvature** (using $R = v / \omega$): $$R = \frac{0.26}{0.48} \approx 0.5417 \text{ m} \approx 0.54 \text{ m}$$ The robot turns to the **left** (since $\omega_R > \omega_L$) along a circular arc of radius **0.54 m**. (Marks: 2 for each sub-part, 1 for units & final answer consistency.) #### Examiner's Allocation (Part b): - 2 marks — linear velocity derivation - 2 marks — angular velocity derivation - 2 marks — radius of curvature - 1 mark — units, sign of turn, and correct conclusion --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-losing pitfalls in this topic:** > 1. **Sign convention errors in differential drive:** When $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot turns **left** (counter-clockwise in standard orientation). Many students get the direction wrong. > 2. **Confusing $L$ (track width) with $r$ (wheel radius):** $L$ is the *distance between the two drive wheel centers*, NOT the wheel diameter or radius. > 3. **Static vs Dynamic stability:** Students often claim "all 4-legged robots are statically stable." This is only true during a *specific gait* (e.g., crawl gait with 3 legs on ground). During dynamic gaits (trotting), they are dynamically stable. > 4. **Specific resistance formula:** The variable $P$ is *propulsive power at the wheels*, not the *electrical input power* of the motor. Students lose marks by missing this distinction. > 5. **Mobile manipulator definition:** A "robot with wheels and an arm" is *not automatically* a mobile manipulator. The arm must be **operationally integrated** with the base (coordinated control). Simply bolting a stationary arm on a mobile base is insufficient. > 6. **Configuration space dimensionality:** Aerial and underwater robots have **6 DOF**, not 3. Always state the dimensionality explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic 1:** - ☐ **Mobile Robot** = self-propelled, autonomous or semi-autonomous, can move in non-fixed environment. - ☐ **Mobile Manipulator** = mobile base + manipulator arm = (infinite workspace) × (dexterity). - ☐ **Locomotion Principle** = Newton's Third Law + energy conversion + fluid/ground interaction. - ☐ **Static stability** ⇔ CoG projection inside support polygon. - ☐ **Dynamic stability** ⇔ average CoG over time inside support polygon. - ☐ **Specific Resistance** $\varepsilon = P/(mgv)$ → lower is better; rolling < walking < hovering. - ☐ **Cost of Transport (CoT)** $\text{CoT} = E/(mgd)$ → dimensionless energy efficiency metric. - ☐ **Differential Drive Forward Kinematics:** $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$, $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$ - ☐ **Differential Drive Inverse Kinematics:** $\omega_R = (2v + L\omega)/(2r)$, $\omega_L = (2v - L\omega)/(2r)$ - ☐ **Differential drive is non-holonomic** — cannot move sideways instantaneously. - ☐ **Minimum legs for static walking = 3** (triped, rare). 4 legs (quadruped) is the practical minimum. - ☐ **Tripod gait** = 3 legs on ground, 3 in swing — used by hexapods for fast walking. - ☐ **Tracked robots** have higher $\varepsilon$ than wheeled but excel in rough terrain. - ☐ **Aerial robots (drones)** have 6 DOF; lift equation: $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 S C_L$. - ☐ **Drag equation** $F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$ applies to both aerial and aquatic locomotion. - ☐ **AUVs** operate submerged, **ASVs** float on surface — different buoyancy constraints. - ☐ **Bio-inspired robots** (robotic fish, dog-legged quadrupeds) draw design from nature. - ☐ **Common exam traps:** sign of angular velocity, swap of $L$ and $r$, mixing static vs dynamic stability, forgetting units. --- **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Introduction to Mobile Robots and Mobile Manipulators**

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Degree of Freedom (DOF) in Robotics

### Formal Definition
**Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is defined as the **number of independent parameters (variables)** required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of a mechanical system, robotic mechanism, or mobile robot in a given workspace. For a rigid body moving in 3D space, the maximum DOF is **6** (3 translational + 3 rotational), while in a 2D plane it is **3** (2 translational + 1 rotational).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In robotics, DOF determines the dexterity, reachability, and task-complexity capability of a manipulator or mobile platform. It is governed by the **Kutzbach–Gruebler Criterion** for mechanisms.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **human arm** reaching for a cup:
- Your **shoulder** can swing forward/back, side, and rotate — roughly 3 DOF.
- Your **elbow** can bend — 1 DOF.
- Your **wrist** can pitch, yaw, and roll — 3 DOF.
- Your **fingers** add even more.

So a human arm has ~7 DOF at the major joints. Now imagine a robot arm — if a robot has only 3 DOF, it can move in a 3D space (like a tripod-mounted camera). If it has 6 DOF, it can place an object at any arbitrary position **and** any orientation in space. **More DOF = more flexibility, but also more complexity and cost.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A serial-chain manipulator needs at least **6 DOF** to reach any point in 3D space with arbitrary orientation — this is the standard requirement for industrial robots.

---

## 1.2 Manoeuvrability (Maneuverability)

### Formal Definition
**Manoeuvrability** of a wheeled mobile robot (WMR) is defined as the **set of instantaneous motions (or trajectories) that the robot can achieve** at any given instant, given its mechanical configuration (wheel types and their geometric arrangement). It is the **kinematic capability** of the vehicle in the plane.

It is mathematically decomposed into two sub-properties:
1. **Degree of Mobility (M)** — the number of independently controllable degrees of freedom in the plane (translational + rotational velocity components).
2. **Degree of Steerability (S)** — the number of independently steerable wheels (i.e., wheels whose steering angle can be actively controlled).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manoeuvrability = M + S**, where **M** is the number of *velocity* DOFs and **S** is the number of *steering* DOFs. This is the **Campion/Muir/Neuman framework** adopted by KTU.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are pushing a **shopping cart** in a supermarket:
- The cart's **front wheels swivel freely** (castor wheels). You can push it in any direction. This is high manoeuvrability.
- Now imagine a **car**: the front wheels are steered (you control the steering angle), and the rear wheels are fixed. The car moves forward/backward (Mobility = 2, forward + steering) but cannot move sideways directly.
- A **3-wheeled office chair** with all swiveling wheels is *omnidirectional* — it has full manoeuvrability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Maximum planar Manoeuvrability of any WMR = **3** (2 translation + 1 rotation).
> - A robot is **omnidirectional** if and only if M = 3 (it can move in any direction without reorienting).
> - A robot is **holonomic** if controllable DOFs = total DOFs (M = 3 and S is sufficient to achieve any path).

---

## 1.3 Why DOF and Manoeuvrability Matter in Advanced Robotics

- **Manipulator Design:** Number of joints → reachable workspace & dexterous workspace.
- **Mobile Robotics:** Wheel arrangement → path planning complexity, energy efficiency, and obstacle avoidance.
- **Parallel Manipulators:** Requires mobility = 0 for a structure; mobility ≥ 1 for in-parallel actuation.
- **Legged Robots:** Each leg needs ≥ 3 DOF for stable walking; total body DOF depends on gait.
- **Grippers:** Typically 1 (open/close) to 3 (in-hand manipulation) DOF.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kutzbach–Gruebler Criterion (Mobility of Mechanisms)

The mobility (M) of a mechanism is the number of independent inputs needed to fully specify the configuration. For a mechanism with **n** links (including ground) and **j** joints of various types:

### Planar Mechanism (3 DOF per link in 2D)

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

### Spatial Mechanism (6 DOF per link in 3D)

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where each **jᵢ** denotes joints with **i** degrees of freedom (lower pair joints, higher pair joints, etc.).

| Joint Type | Symbol | Planar DOF | Spatial DOF | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Pair (1 DOF) | j₁ | 1 | 1 | Revolute (R), Prismatic (P) |
| Higher Pair (2 DOF) | j₂ | 2 | 2 | Cam-follower, gear mesh (planar) |
| 3-DOF Joint | j₃ | — | 3 | Spherical, planar-in-3D |
| 4-DOF Joint | j₄ | — | 4 | Hooke |
| 5-DOF Joint | j₅ | — | 5 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case — Manipulator DOF:**
> For an *n*-link serial chain (ground + n moving links = n+1 total), the mobility is simply the sum of joint freedoms: **DOF = Σ fᵢ** (Gruebler's formula also works).

---

## 2.2 DOF Calculation for a Serial Manipulator

For a robotic arm with **n** joints of freedoms **f₁, f₂, ..., fₙ**:

$$\text{DOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$$

**Example:** A robot with a spherical wrist (3 revolute joints, each 1 DOF) on a 3-DOF arm (revolute + prismatic + revolute):

$$\text{DOF} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 6-DOF manipulator is called a **general-purpose manipulator**. Anything beyond 6 DOF is called a **kinematically redundant** manipulator (e.g., the human arm at 7 DOF).

---

## 2.3 Manoeuvrability for Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMR)

Following the **Campion, Bastin & D'Andrea-Noë** classification framework:

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degree of Mobility | **M** | Number of velocity DOFs in the plane | 0 ≤ M ≤ 3 |
| Degree of Steerability | **S** | Number of steerable wheels | 0 ≤ S ≤ 2 |
| Manoeuvrability | **Man** | M + S | 0 ≤ Man ≤ 5 |
| Redundancy | **R** | Man − 3 | R ≥ 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic Constraint:** A WMR is **holonomic** if Man ≥ 3. It is **omni-directional** if M = 3.

### Rules for computing M and S:

1. **Each fixed (non-steerable, non-driven) standard wheel** imposes **1 non-holonomic constraint** on the robot's motion.
2. **Each steering wheel** (steerable but not driven) also imposes **1 non-holonomic constraint**.
3. **Each driven wheel (powered)** does not impose a non-holonomic constraint by itself.
4. **Each castor wheel** (passively steerable, non-driven) imposes **1 non-holonomic constraint**.

The number of **non-holonomic constraints (N)** equals the number of fixed or steering wheels (excluding castor and driven wheels in standard analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **General Formulas:**
> - Degree of Mobility: $M = 3 - N$ (where N is the number of independent non-holonomic constraints)
> - Degree of Steerability: $S$ = number of steerable wheels.
> - If $M = 3$: omnidirectional.
> - If $M = 2$ and $S \geq 1$: car-like, can follow any path with proper steering.
> - If $M = 1$: differential drive or tricycle (constrained motion).

---

## 2.4 Synchro-drive, Tricycle, and Differential Drive (Most Common WMRs)

### A. Differential Drive (2 motorized wheels + 1 castor)
- **Driven wheels:** 2 (rear)
- **Steerable wheels:** 0
- **Non-holonomic constraints N = 1** (one fixed-wheel direction)
- $M = 3 - 1 = 2$, $S = 0$, **Man = 2**
- **Motion:** Can move in x, rotate in θ, but cannot move sideways in y. *Non-holonomic.*

### B. Tricycle / Car-like Drive (2 rear fixed + 1 front steered/driven)
- **Driven wheels:** 1 (front)
- **Steerable wheels:** 1 (front)
- $M = 1$, $S = 1$, **Man = 2**
- **Motion:** Forward/backward + steering. *Non-holonomic, but path-controllable.*

### C. Synchronous Drive (3 or 4 wheels, all steered and driven together)
- All wheels rotate at same speed and same steering angle.
- $M = 2$, $S = 1$, **Man = 3** ⇒ **Holonomic (Man = 3)**.
- Motion constraint: robot cannot move sideways instantaneously but can follow any 2D path (crab-walk).

### D. Omnidirectional Drive (3 or 4 Swedish/Mecanum wheels)
- All wheels independently driven with perpendicular rollers.
- $M = 3$, $S = 0$, **Man = 3** ⇒ **Fully holonomic & omnidirectional.**

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Planar Gruebler | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | 2D mechanism |
| Spatial Gruebler | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | 3D mechanism |
| Serial Manipulator DOF | $\text{DOF} = \sum f_i$ | Sum of joint freedoms |
| WMR Mobility | $M = 3 - N$ | N = non-holonomic constraints |
| WMR Steerability | $S = $ number of steerable wheels | 0, 1, or 2 |
| Manoeuvrability | $\text{Man} = M + S$ | Range 0 to 5 |
| Holonomic condition | $\text{Man} \geq 3$ | Controllability of full pose |
| Omnidirectional | $M = 3$ | Can move in any direction |
| Rigid body 3D DOF | **6** | 3 translation + 3 rotation |
| Rigid body 2D DOF | **3** | 2 translation + 1 rotation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Industrial robotics:** Gruebler's formula validates whether a parallel manipulator is properly constrained.
> - **Autonomous vehicles:** Manoeuvrability analysis drives wheel selection (Ackermann vs. omni vs. differential).
> - **Space robotics (e.g., Canadarm):** DOF redundancy enables obstacle avoidance in cluttered environments.
> - **Surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci):** High DOF + small scale = dexterity for in-vivo operations.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gruebler's Formula for a Planar Four-Bar Mechanism

### Problem Setup
A planar four-bar linkage has:
- Number of links: **n = 4** (including ground link)
- Joints: 4 revolute joints (each is a 1-DOF lower pair), so **j₁ = 4**
- Higher pair joints: j₂ = 0

### Step-by-Step Derivation

A free rigid body in 2D has **3 DOF** (2 translation + 1 rotation).

$$\text{For } n \text{ links, total unconstrained DOF} = 3n$$

But one link is the ground (fixed frame), so its DOF is "used up":

$$\text{Free DOF available} = 3(n - 1)$$

Each **lower pair (1-DOF joint)** removes **2 DOF** from the system (it constrains the relative motion of the two joined links to 1 DOF, eliminating 2 of the 3 possible relative motions):

$$\text{Constraint per lower pair} = 2 \text{ DOF}$$

Each **higher pair (2-DOF joint)** removes only **1 DOF**:

$$\text{Constraint per higher pair} = 1 \text{ DOF}$$

Therefore, mobility:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - 1 \cdot j_2 \\
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 1(0) \\
M &= 3(3) - 8 - 0 \\
M &= 9 - 8 \\
M &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A four-bar has 1 DOF, meaning **one input (e.g., one motor on the input crank)** is enough to fully determine the configuration of the entire mechanism.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: DOF of a 6-DOF Industrial Manipulator (Puma-like)

### Problem Setup
A 6R manipulator has 6 revolute joints connecting 7 links (ground + 6 moving links). Using Gruebler's spatial formula:

- $n = 7$ (ground + 6 links)
- $j_1 = 6$ (all revolute, 1-DOF)
- $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1 \cdot j_5 \\
M &= 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0 \\
M &= 6(6) - 30 \\
M &= 36 - 30 \\
M &= 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 6R manipulator has 6 DOF, exactly matching the 6 DOF of a free rigid body in 3D space. **Therefore, it can place its end-effector at any (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw)** in its reachable workspace.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Manoeuvrability of a 4-Wheel Car (Ackermann Steered)

### Problem Setup
A car has:
- 2 rear wheels: **fixed orientation, driven** (powered).
- 2 front wheels: **steerable (single common steering angle), driven**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Count non-holonomic constraints (N):**
The 2 rear fixed wheels each impose a "rolling without slipping" constraint that prevents sideways motion at the wheel. However, since both rear wheels share the same axis, they collectively impose only **1 independent non-holonomic constraint** (the robot's lateral velocity must be zero).

The 2 front steered wheels are *not* fixed in orientation (they are steerable), so they don't add additional fixed-axis constraints.

$$N = 1$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mobility:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3 - N \\
M &= 3 - 1 \\
M &= 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Steerability:**

The 2 front wheels share a single steering motor (1 common steering angle), so:

$$S = 1$$

**Step 4 — Compute Manoeuvrability:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Man} &= M + S \\
\text{Man} &= 2 + 1 \\
\text{Man} &= 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Holonomic check:**

Since Man = 3, the system is **kinematically holonomic** — given a desired path, an Ackermann-steered car can follow any smoothly-varying 2D trajectory by appropriate steering and forward speed commands.

**However**, it is **not omnidirectional** (M = 2 ≠ 3), because it cannot move sideways instantaneously. It must perform a multi-step maneuver (parallel parking style) to translate sideways.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Manoeuvrability of a 3-Wheel Omnidirectional Robot (Swedish Wheels)

### Problem Setup
3 Swedish wheels, each independently driven (each motor sets wheel rotation speed). Steering comes from differential wheel speeds, not from physically rotating the wheels.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Non-holonomic constraints:**

There are **no fixed wheels and no steering wheels**, so the only constraints are the rolling constraints of each wheel. But because each wheel can produce an effective velocity vector in any direction (due to the passive perpendicular rollers), the *net* constraint set is empty:

$$N = 0$$

**Step 2 — Mobility:**

$$M = 3 - 0 = 3$$

**Step 3 — Steerability:**

$$S = 0$$

(No wheel has an independent steering motor; "steering" is achieved kinematically by varying wheel speeds.)

**Step 4 — Manoeuvrability:**

$$\text{Man} = M + S = 3 + 0 = 3$$

**Step 5 — Holonomic & Omnidirectional:**

Since $M = 3$ and Man = 3, the robot is **fully holonomic and omnidirectional**: it can move in any direction (forward, sideways, diagonally) instantaneously, and rotate in place.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common WMRs

| Robot Type | Driven Wheels | Steered Wheels | N | M | S | Man | Holonomic? | Omnidirectional? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive (2W + castor) | 2 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 2 | No | No |
| Tricycle (1 steer/driven + 2 fixed/driven) | 3 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | Yes | No |
| Ackermann Car (4W) | 4 (or 2) | 2 (front) | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | Yes | No |
| Synchronous Drive (3 or 4W) | 3 or 4 | 3 or 4 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 4 | Yes (Redundant) | Yes |
| Omnidirectional (Swedish/Mecanum, 3 or 4W) | 3 or 4 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | Yes | Yes |
| Unicycle (1 sphere + 2 rollers) | 2 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | Yes | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Takeaway:** When designing a service robot (warehouse, hospital), an **omnidirectional drive** is preferred in cluttered spaces. For outdoor long-distance (e.g., autonomous cars), **Ackermann** is preferred for stability and energy efficiency.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: DOF of a Parallel Manipulator (Stewart Platform)

### Problem Setup
A Stewart platform (6-DOF parallel manipulator) has:
- **n = 14** links: 1 ground (base) + 1 moving platform + 6 upper struts + 6 lower struts.
- **Joints:** 6 spherical joints (3-DOF each) on the platform end + 6 universal joints (2-DOF each) on the base end.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Joint counts:
- $j_3 = 6$ (6 spherical, each 3-DOF)
- $j_2 = 6$ (6 universal/Hooke-type, each 2-DOF)
- All other jᵢ = 0

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1 \cdot j_5 \\
M &= 6(14-1) - 5(0) - 4(6) - 3(6) - 2(0) - 1(0) \\
M &= 6(13) - 0 - 24 - 18 - 0 - 0 \\
M &= 78 - 42 \\
M &= 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A Stewart platform has 6 DOF, matching the 6 DOF of a free body in 3D. **The 6 actuators (linear struts) are the 6 independent inputs.**

> [!NOTE]
> This validates the design: with 6 actuators on a 6-DOF parallel structure, the system is fully controllable with no redundancy and no internal mobility.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: DOF Decomposition in a Robotic Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RIGID BODY IN 3D SPACE"]:::root --> B["TOTAL DOF = 6"]
    B --> C["3 TRANSLATIONAL DOF"]
    B --> D["3 ROTATIONAL DOF"]
    C --> C1["X-axis motion"]
    C --> C2["Y-axis motion"]
    C --> C3["Z-axis motion"]
    D --> D1["Roll about X"]
    D --> D2["Pitch about Y"]
    D --> D3["Yaw about Z"]
    E["JOINT TYPES"]:::joint --> F["Revolute R 1 DOF"]
    E --> G["Prismatic P 1 DOF"]
    E --> H["Spherical S 3 DOF"]
    E --> I["Universal U 2 DOF"]
    E --> J["Helical H 1 DOF"]
    K["MANIPULATOR DOF CALCULATION"]:::calc --> L["Sum of Joint Freedoms"]
    L --> M["DOF = Sigma fi"]
    K --> N["Gruebler Formula 3D"]
    N --> O["M = 6n minus 1 minus 5j1 minus 4j2 minus 3j3 minus 2j4 minus j5"]
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef joint fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef calc fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above block diagram maps the **logical decomposition of a manipulator's DOF** into translational, rotational, and joint-level freedoms. The two parallel paths (joint-sum method vs. Gruebler's formula method) yield identical results for serial chains.


## 4.4 Block Diagram: Manoeuvrability Sub-Properties (M, S, Man, R)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["VELOCITY DOFs M"]:::m --> D["TOTAL MANOEUVRABILITY Man"]

    D --> E["Holonomic Check Man greater than or equal to 3"]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **redundancy R** is crucial in fault-tolerant robot design: if one wheel fails, redundant steering DOFs allow the robot to reconfigure and continue operation — vital in space and military applications.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Define Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robotic manipulator. How many DOF does a rigid body possess in 3D space? Why is a 6-DOF manipulator called a general-purpose manipulator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robotic manipulator is the number of independent joint variables required to completely specify the position and orientation of the end-effector in the workspace.
- **Rigid body in 3D (1 mark):** A rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 DOF** — 3 translational (along x, y, z) and 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw).
- **6-DOF = general-purpose (1 mark):** Since a free rigid body in 3D has 6 DOF, a manipulator with 6 DOF can position its end-effector at any point in the workspace with any arbitrary orientation, making it suitable for general-purpose tasks (welding, painting, pick-and-place).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Manoeuvrability, Mobility, and Steerability of a wheeled mobile robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mobility** | M | Number of velocity DOFs the robot can achieve in the plane | 0–3 |
| **Steerability** | S | Number of independently steerable wheels | 0–2 |
| **Manoeuvrability** | Man | Sum: Man = M + S, total set of trajectories achievable | 0–5 |

- **Mobility (1 mark):** Velocity-based — answers "how many ways can the robot move instantaneously."
- **Steerability (1 mark):** Hardware-based — answers "how many wheels have active steering."
- **Manoeuvrability (1 mark):** Combined measure — answers "what trajectories/poses are reachable." Holonomic if Man ≥ 3; omnidirectional if M = 3.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model - Dec 2024]**

**a) (7 marks)** A 4-bar planar mechanism has 4 links connected by 4 revolute joints. Using Gruebler's formula, calculate the mobility of the mechanism. Explain what this mobility implies about the number of actuators needed.

**b) (7 marks)** A robot manipulator has 3 prismatic joints followed by 3 revolute joints (PPP-RRR configuration). Calculate its total DOF and determine if it qualifies as a general-purpose manipulator.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a)**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters (1 mark):**
- Number of links: n = 4 (ground + 3 moving)
- Joints: 4 revolute (lower pair, 1-DOF each), so j₁ = 4, j₂ = 0

**Step 2 — Apply Gruebler's planar formula (2 marks):**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- Mobility = 1, meaning the mechanism has **1 DOF**.
- **One actuator** (e.g., one motor driving the input link) is sufficient to fully determine the configuration of all 4 links.
- This is why in a four-bar linkage, only one input crank is motorized; the other links follow deterministically.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b)**

**Step 1 — List joint freedoms (2 marks):**
- 3 prismatic joints: each 1 DOF
- 3 revolute joints: each 1 DOF
- Total joint freedoms = 3(1) + 3(1) = 6

**Step 2 — Compute total DOF (2 marks):**

$$\text{DOF} = \sum f_i = 3 + 3 = 6$$

**Step 3 — Compare with rigid body 3D DOF (1 mark):**

Since a free rigid body in 3D has 6 DOF, a manipulator with 6 DOF matches this exactly.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (2 marks):**
- The PPP-RRR manipulator has **6 DOF**, so it qualifies as a **general-purpose manipulator**.
- It can place its end-effector at any (x, y, z) position with any (roll, pitch, yaw) orientation within its reachable workspace.
- However, it is **not redundant**; there is exactly one joint configuration for each desired end-effector pose (no dexterity for obstacle avoidance).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model - May 2024 — Alternative]**

**a) (7 marks)** A differential drive mobile robot has 2 driven rear wheels and 1 passive castor wheel in the front. Compute the degree of mobility, degree of steerability, and total manoeuvrability. Comment on whether the robot is holonomic and omnidirectional.

**b) (7 marks)** A 4-wheeled omnidirectional robot uses 4 Swedish wheels, each independently driven by a separate motor. The robot carries a 6-DOF robotic arm. Determine: (i) the DOF of the arm, (ii) the manoeuvrability of the mobile base, and (iii) the total DOF of the combined system (base + arm) in the workspace.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a)**

**Step 1 — Identify constraints (2 marks):**
- 2 driven rear wheels: contribute **0 non-holonomic constraints** (driven wheels are assumed to roll without slip but are kinematically active, and their fixed orientation is "absorbed" by the differential drive kinematic model).
- 1 castor wheel (passively steerable, non-driven): contributes **1 non-holonomic constraint** (it prevents sideways motion of the wheel contact point in the wheel's instantaneous frame).
- **Total N = 1**

**Step 2 — Compute Mobility (2 marks):**

$$M = 3 - N = 3 - 1 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Steerability and Manoeuvrability (1 mark):**

$$S = 0 \quad (\text{no actively steered wheels})$$

$$\text{Man} = M + S = 2 + 0 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Holonomic & Omnidirectional check (2 marks):**
- Since Man = 2 < 3, the robot is **not holonomic** — it cannot follow an arbitrary path instantaneously.
- Since M = 2 < 3, the robot is **not omnidirectional** — it cannot move sideways directly.
- The robot is constrained to move along its forward axis and rotate, but not to translate laterally (requires a multi-step maneuver).

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b)**

**(i) DOF of the arm (2 marks):**
A 6-DOF arm has:

$$\text{DOF}_{\text{arm}} = 6 \text{ (as given)}$$

**(ii) Manoeuvrability of the mobile base (2 marks):**
- 4 Swedish wheels, all driven, no steered wheels.
- Each Swedish wheel has a passive perpendicular roller, which effectively removes the fixed-orientation constraint.
- $N = 0$, so $M = 3$, $S = 0$, $\text{Man} = 3$.
- The base is **holonomic and omnidirectional**.

**(iii) Total DOF of combined system (3 marks):**
The combined system has:
- 3 DOF for the base pose in the plane (x, y, θ).
- 6 DOF for the arm.
- These are **independent** (the arm is mounted on the base, not coupled kinematically).

$$\text{DOF}_{\text{total}} = \text{DOF}_{\text{base}} + \text{DOF}_{\text{arm}} = 3 + 6 = 9$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 9-DOF mobile manipulator** is **kinematically redundant** (9 > 6) for end-effector task-space positioning. The redundancy can be exploited for **obstacle avoidance, singularity avoidance, and optimal joint configurations** during motion planning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Gruebler's formula confusion (–2 marks):** Students often use the **planar** formula for a **spatial** mechanism or vice versa. Always identify 2D vs. 3D first.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract ground link (–1 mark):** In the spatial Gruebler formula, the term is $6(n-1)$, **not** $6n$. The ground link is link #1 with 0 DOF.
> 3. **Confusing M and Man (–1 mark):** Mobility (M) is **velocity-based**; Manoeuvrability (Man) is the **sum of M and steerability S**. Many students write Man = 3 when they should write M = 3.
> 4. **Castor wheel miscounting (–2 marks):** A **castor wheel is passive steering**, not active. It still imposes a non-holonomic constraint, so it reduces M.
> 5. **Swedish/Mecanum wheel classification error (–1 mark):** Swedish and Mecanum wheels are often called "omni wheels." Correctly, they enable **M = 3** (omnidirectional mobility) **without active steering** (S = 0).
> 6. **Unit omission in formulas (–0.5 marks):** Although DOF is dimensionless, always state "DOF is a dimensionless count."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **DOF = number of independent parameters** specifying a system's configuration.
- **Free rigid body in 3D = 6 DOF**; in 2D = 3 DOF.
- **Gruebler's planar formula:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- **Gruebler's spatial formula:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- **Serial manipulator DOF** = sum of individual joint freedoms.
- **Mobility (M)** = velocity DOFs of WMR; **M = 3 − N** where N = number of non-holonomic constraints.
- **Steerability (S)** = number of independently steerable wheels.
- **Manoeuvrability (Man) = M + S**; range 0 to 5.
- **Holonomic** ⟺ **Man ≥ 3**; **Omnidirectional** ⟺ **M = 3**.
- **Differential drive** is non-holonomic (M = 2, S = 0, Man = 2).
- **Ackermann car** is holonomic but not omnidirectional (M = 2, S = 1, Man = 3).
- **Synchronous / Swedish / Mecanum** drives are holonomic and omnidirectional (M = 3, Man ≥ 3).
- **Redundancy R = Man − 3**; useful for fault tolerance and optimization.
- **Four-bar linkage** has 1 DOF (Gruebler planar).
- **Stewart platform** has 6 DOF (Gruebler spatial, 14 links, 6 spherical + 6 universal joints).
- **Industrial 6R arm** has 6 DOF, hence "general-purpose."
- **Redundant manipulators** (DOF > 6) — e.g., 7-DOF human-arm-like — can avoid singularities and obstacles.
- **Always specify** whether the mechanism is **planar** or **spatial** before applying Gruebler.
- **Always state units** in derivations (DOF is dimensionless, but explicitly mention it).
- **Active steering wheels (S)** contribute to **Man** but **NOT to M**; their constraints are non-holonomic, reducing M.
- **Driven wheels** alone do not add non-holonomic constraints (their slip-less rolling is the *intended* motion).
- **Castor wheels** DO add non-holonomic constraints (they prevent sideways motion of the contact point).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Wheel Model, Different Wheel Configurations, Holonomic and Non-Holonomic Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT723 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generalized Wheel Model, Different Wheel Configurations, Holonomic and Non-Holonomic Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Generalized Wheel Model (GWM)

The **Generalized Wheel Model** is a unified mathematical framework, formalized by **Campion, Bastin, and D'Andrea-Novel (1996)**, that describes the kinematics of *any* wheeled mobile robot (WMR) regardless of wheel type. It expresses the relationship between:

- The robot's **pose** $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ in the global (world) frame
- The **angular velocity of each wheel** $\dot{\phi}_i$
- The **steering angle** $\beta_i$ of each wheel (if steerable)

A generalized wheel is a disc-shaped wheel that may be:
- **Fixed** (orientation rigidly attached to the chassis)
- **Steered** (orientation $\beta$ controllable, with rotation axis offset from contact point)
- **Passively rolling** (e.g., caster, ball)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The GWM is the *cornerstone* of mobile robot kinematics. Every other wheel type (Swedish, Mecanum, spherical, etc.) is mathematically a special case of the GWM.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Shopping Cart" 🛒

Imagine pushing a **locked shopping cart** on a supermarket floor. The cart's wheels are *fixed* — they only point in one direction relative to the cart. If you want to move sideways, **you cannot** — you must first rotate the whole cart, then push forward. This is the everyday experience of a *non-holonomic* constraint.

Now imagine an **office chair with five free-rolling casters**. Push it from any side, and it glides wherever you want. The casters are *passively omnidirectional*, removing the sideways restriction. This is *holonomic* behavior — the chair is not restricted to a velocity direction.

| Real Object | Robot Counterpart | Constraint Type |
|---|---|---|
| Locked shopping cart | Car / Differential drive | **Non-Holonomic** |
| Office chair with casters | Mecanum / Swedish-wheel robot | **Holonomic (Omnidirectional)** |

## 1.3 Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic — Formal Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic Robot:** Number of controllable degrees of freedom (DOF) **equals** the total DOF of the robot in its workspace.
> - A 2D ground robot is *holonomic* if it can independently control all 3 pose variables $(x, y, \theta)$ instantaneously.
>
> **Non-Holonomic Robot:** Number of controllable DOF is **less than** the total DOF. Velocity-level constraints exist that cannot be integrated into position-level (configuration) constraints.

The Degree of **Mobility** ($\delta_m$) and Degree of **Steerability** ($\delta_s$) are defined as:

$$M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

- $\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(C_{pf}) \quad$ where $C_{pf}$ is the **Pfaffian constraint matrix**
- $\delta_s = \text{rank of the steering-joint matrix}$

A robot is **omnidirectional (fully holonomic)** when $\delta_m = 3$.

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake: assuming "has 3 wheels" implies holonomic. The classification depends on the *type* of wheel and the *constraint equations*, not the wheel count.

## 1.4 Standard Wheel Configurations (Campion's Classification)

| Type | Configuration | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $M$ | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1) | 1 fixed + 1 caster | 1 | 0 | 1 | Single fixed wheel chair |
| (2) | 2 fixed (coaxial) + 1 caster | 1 | 0 | 1 | Differential drive |
| (3) | 1 fixed + 1 steered (centered) | 1 | 1 | 2 | Tricycle |
| (4) | 2 fixed (same axle) | 1 | 0 | 1 | Differential drive robot |
| (5) | 2 fixed + 2 steered (Ackermann) | 1 | 1 | 2 | Car / Ackermann steering |
| (6) | 2 fixed + 2 steered (decentralized) | 1 | 2 | 3 | Crab-steered vehicle |
| (7) | 2 fixed + 2 off-centered steered | 1 | 1 | 2 | Synchro-drive |
| (8) | 3 Swedish / Mecanum (120° apart) | 3 | 0 | 3 | Omnidirectional robot |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheel Types in the GWM Framework

### (a) Fixed (Standard) Wheel
- The wheel plane orientation $\beta$ is **constant** w.r.t. the chassis.
- **2 DOF at the contact point** (roll about axle, no slip).
- Imposes **one Pfaffian constraint** on the chassis motion.

### (b) Steered (Centered) Wheel
- The wheel rotates about a vertical axis passing **through the contact point**.
- **3 DOF at the contact point** (roll, steer, no slip).
- Imposes one Pfaffian constraint; the steering angle $\beta(t)$ is a control input.

### (c) Caster Wheel (Off-Centered Steered Wheel)
- Steering axis does **not** pass through the contact point — offset by a distance $d$.
- **3 DOF at the contact point**, but introduces an extra **geometric constraint** on the chassis.
- Imposes **two constraints** (one Pfaffian + one configuration).

### (d) Swedish / Mecanum Wheel
- Small passive rollers around the circumference.
- Wheel can apply force in directions **other than its rolling direction** due to roller geometry.
- Active wheel + passive rollers = **omnidirectional** behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Swedish wheels are the workhorse of warehouse robots (e.g., Kiva / Amazon Robotics) because they enable motion in *any* direction without re-orienting the chassis — saving enormous time in tight aisles.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Analysis — No-Slip Constraint

Consider a single wheel of radius $r$ attached to the chassis at point $P = (l_x, l_y)$ in the robot frame, with rolling direction making angle $\beta$ with the chassis x-axis.

**Step 1 — Chassis velocity at the contact point in robot frame:**
$$\mathbf{v}_P = \begin{bmatrix} v_x - \omega\, l_y \\ v_y + \omega\, l_x \end{bmatrix}$$

where $v_x, v_y$ are translational velocities and $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the chassis.

**Step 2 — The wheel can only move *along* its rolling direction** (no lateral slip):
$$[sin\,\beta,\;-cos\,\beta]\,\mathbf{v}_P = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expanding:**
$$sin\,\beta\,(v_x - \omega\,l_y) - cos\,\beta\,(v_y + \omega\,l_x) = 0$$
$$\boxed{\;sin\,\beta\,v_x - cos\,\beta\,v_y + (l_x\,sin\,\beta - l_y\,cos\,\beta)\,\omega = 0\;}$$

This is the **Pfaffian constraint** in the robot frame:
$$\mathbf{J}_1(\beta)\,\xi_R = 0 \quad\text{where}\quad \xi_R = [v_x, v_y, \omega]^T$$
$$\mathbf{J}_1(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} sin\,\beta & -cos\,\beta & l_x\,sin\,\beta - l_y\,cos\,\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Transform to global frame** via rotation $R(\theta)$:
$$\mathbf{J}_1(\beta)\,R(\theta)\,\dot{\xi} = 0$$
$$\mathbf{C}_{pf} = \mathbf{J}_1(\beta)\,R(\theta)$$
$$\mathbf{C}_{pf} = \begin{bmatrix} sin(\beta+\theta) & -cos(\beta+\theta) & l_x\,sin\,\beta - l_y\,cos\,\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

The full Pfaffian constraint:
$$\mathbf{C}_{pf}(\beta,\theta)\,\dot{\xi} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Why is it non-holonomic?**
If the constraint were holonomic, there would exist $f(x,y,\theta)$ such that:
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = sin(\beta+\theta),\quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -cos(\beta+\theta),\quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta} = l_x\,sin\,\beta - l_y\,cos\,\beta$$

Integrating the first two:
$$f = x\,sin(\beta+\theta) - y\,cos(\beta+\theta) + g(\theta)$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\theta$:
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta} = x\,cos(\beta+\theta) + y\,sin(\beta+\theta) + g'(\theta)$$

For this to equal $l_x\,sin\,\beta - l_y\,cos\,\beta$ (a constant), we'd need:
$$x\,cos(\beta+\theta) + y\,sin(\beta+\theta) = \text{const}$$

This is **not** generally satisfied as the robot moves → **constraint is non-integrable** → **non-holonomic**. ∎

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Term | Mathematical Form | Variables / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Robot pose (world frame) | $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | Position & orientation |
| 2 | Body velocity | $\xi_R = [v_x, v_y, \omega]^T$ | Linear and angular velocity |
| 3 | Rotation matrix (world ← body) | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} cos\,\theta & sin\,\theta & 0 \\ sin\,\theta & -cos\,\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Orthogonal transformation |
| 4 | Fixed-wheel constraint | $sin(\beta+\theta)\dot{x} - cos(\beta+\theta)\dot{y} + d\dot{\theta} = 0$ | $d = l_x sin\beta - l_y cos\beta$ |
| 5 | Generalized wheel constraint | $\mathbf{C}_{pf}(\beta,\theta)\dot{\xi} = 0$ | $\mathbf{C}_{pf}$ is $m \times 3$ |
| 6 | Mobility degree | $\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(\mathbf{C}_{pf})$ | $0 \le \delta_m \le 3$ |
| 7 | Steerability degree | $\delta_s = \text{rank}(\mathbf{C}_{1s})$ | Independent steering axes |
| 8 | Maneuverability | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Total controllable DOF |
| 9 | Instantaneous Center of Rotation | $\mathbf{v}_i \cdot (\mathbf{r}_{ICR} - \mathbf{r}_i) = 0$ | For non-holonomic robots |
| 10 | Unicycle model | $\dot{x} = v\,cos\theta,\;\dot{y} = v\,sin\theta,\;\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Standard non-holonomic model |
| 11 | Bicycle/Ackermann model | $\dot{x} = v\,cos\theta,\;\dot{y} = v\,sin\theta,\;\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\,tan\phi$ | $\phi$ = steering angle, $L$ = wheelbase |
| 12 | Holonomic condition | $\mathbf{C}_{pf}\dot{\xi} = 0$ is **integrable** | Equivalent to $\frac{\partial A_i}{\partial q_j} = \frac{\partial A_j}{\partial q_i}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` (vertical pipe) is used inside the table cells** to preserve markdown rendering. All absolute value / divisibility notations use proper LaTeX delimiters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Full Generalized Wheel Model for an n-Wheeled Robot

We consider a robot with $n$ wheels. Each wheel $i$ has:
- A contact point at $(l_{xi}, l_{yi})$ in the robot frame
- An orientation $\beta_i$ (rolling direction)
- An angular velocity $\dot{\phi}_i$

### Step 1 — Single-Wheel Rolling Constraint (Robot Frame)

The contact point velocity must be tangent to the wheel plane (no slip):
$$[sin\,\beta_i, -cos\,\beta_i, l_{xi} sin\,\beta_i - l_{yi} cos\,\beta_i]\,\xi_R = r\,\dot{\phi}_i$$

This couples the body velocity to the wheel rotation.

### Step 2 — Assemble Constraint Matrix

For $n$ wheels, stack the constraints vertically:
$$\mathbf{J}_1(\beta)\,\xi_R = r\,\dot{\Phi}$$
$$\mathbf{J}_1(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix}
sin\,\beta_1 & -cos\,\beta_1 & l_{x1} sin\,\beta_1 - l_{y1} cos\,\beta_1 \\
sin\,\beta_2 & -cos\,\beta_2 & l_{x2} sin\,\beta_2 - l_{y2} cos\,\beta_2 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
sin\,\beta_n & -cos\,\beta_n & l_{xn} sin\,\beta_n - l_{yn} cos\,\beta_n
\end{bmatrix}_{n \times 3}$$

$\dot{\Phi} = [\dot{\phi}_1, \dot{\phi}_2, \ldots, \dot{\phi}_n]^T$ is the wheel-speed vector.

### Step 3 — Transform to Global Frame

Use $\xi_R = R(\theta)\,\dot{\xi}$:
$$\mathbf{J}_1(\beta)\,R(\theta)\,\dot{\xi} = r\,\dot{\Phi}$$
$$\mathbf{C}_{pf}(\beta,\theta)\,\dot{\xi} = \mathbf{J}_2(\beta)\,\dot{\Phi}$$

where $\mathbf{C}_{pf} = \mathbf{J}_1 R(\theta)$ is the **$n \times 3$ Pfaffian matrix**, and $\mathbf{J}_2 = r\,\mathbf{I}_n$.

This is the **canonical generalized wheel model**:
$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{C}_{pf}(\beta,\theta)\,\dot{\xi} = \mathbf{J}_2(\beta)\,\dot{\Phi}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Show Non-Holonomic Nature via Frobenius Theorem

A Pfaffian constraint $\mathbf{C}_{pf}\dot{\xi} = 0$ is **completely non-holonomic** if the constraint distribution and its Lie closure span the entire tangent space $\mathbb{R}^3$.

Define constraint distribution $\Omega = \ker(\mathbf{C}_{pf})$ — the set of admissible velocities.
Compute Lie brackets: $[g_1, g_2] = \nabla g_2 \cdot g_1 - \nabla g_1 \cdot g_2$.

If $\text{dim}(\Omega) + \text{rank of Lie algebra generated} = 3$ ⇒ the system is **completely non-holonomic** (controllable despite velocity constraints).

This is why a parallel-parking car can reach any (x, y, θ) configuration — its non-holonomic constraints are *not* a barrier to *path* reachability, only to *instantaneous* sideways motion.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Differential Drive Robot

A differential drive robot has two fixed wheels at $(\pm l, 0)$ with $\beta_i = 0$ (wheels point along chassis x-axis).

**Constraint equations** (in robot frame, $\beta = 0$):

Right wheel: $\;v_x + l\,\omega = r\,\dot{\phi}_R$
Left wheel: $\;v_x - l\,\omega = r\,\dot{\phi}_L$

**Solving for body velocities:**

$$v_x = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)$$
$$\omega = \frac{r}{2l}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$$
$$v_y = 0 \;\;\text{(no lateral motion)}$$

**Transforming to world frame:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v_x\,cos\,\theta - v_y\,sin\,\theta = v_x\,cos\,\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v_x\,sin\,\theta + v_y\,cos\,\theta = v_x\,sin\,\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

This yields the **unicycle kinematic model**:
$$\boxed{\;\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} cos\,\theta & 0 \\ sin\,\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}\;}$$

with control inputs $v$ (forward speed) and $\omega$ (angular speed).

**Degree of mobility analysis:**

$$\mathbf{C}_{pf} = \begin{bmatrix} sin(\theta) & -cos(\theta) & l \\ sin(\theta) & -cos(\theta) & -l \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\text{rank}(\mathbf{C}_{pf}) = 2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \delta_m = 3 - 2 = 1$$
$$\delta_s = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad M = 1 + 0 = 1$$

The differential drive is **non-holonomic** with one controllable DOF (1 input pair controlling 2 DOF: position evolution).

## 3.3 Worked Example — Omnidirectional Mecanum Robot

Four Mecanum wheels at corners $(\pm a, \pm b)$ with roller axes at 45° to the wheel plane.

**Key insight:** Each Mecanum wheel imposes a constraint in a *different* direction. The four constraints are linearly independent, but their directions span $\mathbb{R}^2$ in such a way that the robot can move in **any** direction.

For an ideal Mecanum robot:
$$\mathbf{C}_{pf} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\quad\text{(in some transformed basis)}$$

Wait — more carefully: the Mecanum wheel can generate forces perpendicular to its rolling direction due to the passive rollers. The **kinematic** constraint is:
$$\text{rank}(\mathbf{C}_{pf}) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \delta_m = 3$$

The robot has **no velocity constraints** and is therefore **fully holonomic** (omnidirectional).

$$\delta_s = 0,\quad M = 3 + 0 = 3$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mobility Classification

| Robot Class | $\mathbf{C}_{pf}$ Rank | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $M$ | Holonomic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential drive (Type 2/4) | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | No |
| Car / Ackermann (Type 5) | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Tricycle (Type 3) | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Synchro-drive (Type 7) | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Crab-steered (Type 6) | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | **Yes (fully)** |
| Mecanum 3-wheel (Type 8) | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes (fully)** |
| Mecanum 4-wheel | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes (fully)** |
| Spherical-wheel robot | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes (fully)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Wheel Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid

    A["GENERALIZED WHEEL MODEL"] --> B["RIGID WHEELS"]
    A --> C["SPECIALIZED WHEELS"]

    B --> B2["STEERED WHEEL CENTERED"]

    B1 --> R1["1 DOF ROLL"]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generalized Wheel Model Functional Flow

```mermaid

    P1["CHASSIS POSE X Y THETA"] --> P2["BODY VELOCITY Vx Vy OMEGA"]
    P2 --> P3["CONTACT POINT VELOCITY TRANSFORM"]
    P3 --> P4["NO SLIP EQUATION sin beta vx minus cos beta vy plus d omega equals 0"]
    P4 --> P5["PFAFFIAN CONSTRAINT MATRIX Cpf"]
    P5 --> P6["RANK ANALYSIS"]
    P6 --> P7["MOBILITY DEGREE delta m"]
    P5 --> P8["STEERING INPUTS BETA"]
    P8 --> P9["STEERABILITY DEGREE delta s"]
    P7 --> P10["MANEUVERABILITY M equals delta m plus delta s"]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11["HOLONOMIC OR NON HOLONOMIC CLASSIFICATION"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Robot Configurations (Campion's Types)

```mermaid

    S["WHEELED MOBILE ROBOT TAXONOMY"] --> S1["TYPE 1 ONE FIXED ONE CASTER"]
    S --> S2["TYPE 2 TWO FIXED ONE CASTER"]
```
