# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ and configuration space obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ for a circular mobile robot of radius $r$ in a 2-D workspace populated with polygonal obstacles. Explain why this abstraction is fundamental to motion planning.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **configuration space** $\mathcal{C}$ of a circular mobile robot of radius $r$ moving in a 2-D plane $\mathcal{W} = \mathbb{R}^2$ is the set of all possible robot placements. Since the robot's pose can be fully described by its centre coordinates $(x, y)$, we have:

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$$

The **configuration space obstacle** $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is the set of all configurations where the robot would collide with any workspace obstacle $O_i$. It is obtained by *Minkowski-summing* each obstacle with a disc of radius $r$:

$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \left(O_i \oplus B_r\right) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \text{dist}(q, O_i) \leq r \}$$

The **free configuration space** is therefore:

$$\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$$

**Why fundamental (1 mark):** It reduces the problem of a *shaped robot* navigating around *shaped obstacles* to a *point robot* navigating around *grown obstacles*. Once the planning is done in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, the result is automatically valid in the workspace. This abstraction underlies every mobilisation algorithm discussed in Module 1.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between completeness and optimality in the context of path planning algorithms. Give one example of an algorithm that is complete but not optimal, and one that is optimal but not complete.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Completeness** is the property that an algorithm is guaranteed to find a solution (path from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$) in finite time if one exists, and to report failure if no solution exists. (1 mark)
- **Optimality** is the property that, among all feasible solutions, the algorithm returns the one with minimum cost (e.g., shortest path length). (1 mark)

**Example — Complete but not optimal:** The **Bug2** algorithm is complete (always finds *a* path if one exists) but not optimal — the path it generates can be significantly longer than the true shortest path because it greedily follows obstacle boundaries.

**Example — Optimal but not complete:** The **Basic Potential Field** method, when used with a non-convex potential, is not complete because the robot can get trapped in a local minimum from which the gradient does not lead to the goal; in fact, even when it does find a path, it is generally not optimal. A better example is the **unweighted greedy best-first search**, which is optimal only when the heuristic is consistent and the cost-to-come is irrelevant — typically the standard textbook example of "optimal but not complete" is a greedy heuristic-only search that can loop indefinitely on graphs with cycles. For KTU purposes, answer **"Visibility Graph is optimal and complete; Basic Potential Field is neither optimal nor complete."** (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students incorrectly state that A* is always optimal. A* is optimal **only if the heuristic is admissible**. With an inadmissible heuristic (e.g., Manhattan on an 8-connected grid), A* may return a non-optimal path or even fail to find a path in finite time. Always qualify the answer with the heuristic assumption.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Visibility Graph** algorithm for path planning in a 2-D polygonal workspace. Derive the conditions under which two vertices are *mutually visible* and prove that the shortest path in the visibility graph is the true shortest Euclidean path in the free space.

**(b)** Apply A* search on the following 4×4 grid where **S** is the start, **G** is the goal, **#** denotes obstacles, and 8-connected motion with unit step costs is allowed. Use **Manhattan distance** as heuristic. List the order in which nodes are expanded and state the final optimal path with its cost. (7 marks)

```
S . # .
. . # .
# . . .
. . . G
```

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition and Construction (3 marks):**
Given a 2-D workspace $\mathcal{W}$ containing polygonal obstacles, the **visibility graph** $G_{vis} = (V, E)$ is constructed as follows:

1. The vertex set $V$ contains $q_{start}$, $q_{goal}$, and all vertices of all polygonal obstacles.
2. An edge $(u, v) \in E$ exists if and only if the straight line segment $\overline{uv}$ lies entirely within $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ (i.e., does not intersect the interior of any obstacle).

**Mutual Visibility Condition (2 marks):**
Two vertices $u$ and $v$ are mutually visible if and only if the line segment $\overline{uv}$ does not intersect the interior of any obstacle. Formally:

$$\forall i, \quad \overline{uv} \cap \text{int}(O_i) = \emptyset$$

If the obstacles are convex polygons, this reduces to checking the segment against each edge. For non-convex polygons, one must additionally verify that $\overline{uv}$ does not pass through any concave indentation — this is the standard $O(n^2)$ visibility computation per vertex pair.

**Proof of Optimality (2 marks):**
*Claim:* The shortest path in the visibility graph coincides with the shortest Euclidean path in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

*Proof Sketch:* By the **lower-envelope property of convex hulls**, any shortest Euclidean path between two points avoiding polygonal obstacles can be deformed to a path whose vertices are obstacle vertices (or $q_{start}$/$q_{goal}$) without increasing length. Such a path, by definition, traverses only visibility edges. Since the visibility graph contains all such edges, the shortest path in $G_{vis}$ is at most as long as the true shortest path. Conversely, every path in $G_{vis}$ is a valid path in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, so the shortest $G_{vis}$ path is at least as long as the true optimum. Hence equality holds. $\blacksquare$

**Sketch (refer to description):**

```
  V1 ━━━━━━━━ V2
     ╲         ╲
      ╲         ╲
       V3━━━━━━━━V4
       ╲         ╱
        ╲       ╱
         S━━━━G
```

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Grid coordinates (row, col), 0-indexed:
- $S = (0,0)$, $G = (3,3)$
- Obstacles: $(0,2)$, $(1,2)$, $(2,0)$

**Heuristic:** Manhattan $h(n) = \vert r_n - r_G \vert + \vert c_n - c_G \vert$

**Iteration Trace:**

| Step | Node Expanded | $g$ | $h$ | $f$ | Action |
|------|--------------|-----|-----|-----|--------|
| 1 | $S(0,0)$ | 0 | 6 | 6 | Expand |
| 2 | $(1,0)$ | 1 | 5 | 6 | Expand (tie) |
| 3 | $(0,1)$ | 1 | 5 | 6 | Expand (tie) |
| 4 | $(2,1)$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | Expand |
| 5 | $(1,1)$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | Expand |
| 6 | $(0,1)$ or $(3,1)$ depending on tie-break | 2 or 3 | 4 | 6 | Expand |
| 7 | $(3,2)$ | 4 | 1 | 5 | Expand |
| 8 | $G(3,3)$ | 5 | 0 | 5 | GOAL REACHED |

**Optimal Path:** $S(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,1) \to (3,2) \to G(3,3)$.

**Optimal Cost:** $4$ (using 8-connected unit cardinal moves; note: a diagonal step from $(1,0)$ to $(2,1)$ is also cardinal here — counted as 1 unit in 8-connected grid where all moves are unit cost).

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Clarification:** In strict 8-connected grids with cardinal cost 1 and diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}$, the cost differs. KTU examiners typically accept either convention if clearly stated.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**(a)** Describe the **Potential Field Method** for robot navigation. Derive the mathematical expressions for attractive and repulsive potentials. Discuss **two major limitations** of the basic potential field approach and explain how **harmonic potential fields** overcome these limitations.

**(b)** Compare and contrast **RRT** and **RRT*** in terms of (i) optimality guarantees, (ii) computational complexity, (iii) the rewiring step, and (iv) suitability for kinodynamic constraints. Tabulate your answer.

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Overview (1 mark):**
The Potential Field Method, introduced by **Oussama Khatib in 1986**, treats the robot as a charged particle moving under the influence of an artificial potential field. The goal exerts an attractive force, while obstacles exert repulsive forces. The robot's velocity is aligned with the negative gradient of the total potential.

**Attractive Potential (2 marks):**
For a robot at configuration $q$ with goal $q_{goal}$:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \, \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert^2$$

The corresponding attractive force is:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$$

This is a linear restoring force, analogous to Hooke's law in mechanics.

**Repulsive Potential (2 marks):**
For an obstacle whose closest point is at distance $\rho(q)$, with influence radius $\rho_0$:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2, & \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

The repulsive force:

$$F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \nabla \rho(q)$$

**Total Potential and Motion (1 mark):**
$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_{rep,i}(q), \quad \dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$$

**Limitations and Harmonic Fix (1 mark):**

*Limitation 1 — Local Minima Trap:* In narrow corridors, $\vec{F}_{att}$ and $\vec{F}_{rep}$ can cancel at a non-goal configuration, stalling the robot permanently.

*Limitation 2 — Oscillation in Narrow Passages:* The robot can oscillate between two opposing walls.

**Harmonic Potential Fields (Koditschek & Rimon, 1992):** Replace the sum of analytic potentials with the solution to Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 U = 0$ over $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, subject to boundary conditions $U = 1$ at goal, $U = 0$ at obstacles. By the maximum principle, $U$ has *no local minima except at the goal*. The negative gradient $-\nabla U$ provides a complete navigation function. Disadvantages: computationally expensive (must solve PDE numerically); sensitive to topology changes.

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | RRT | RRT* |
|--------|-----|------|
| **(i) Optimality** | Not optimal — converges to a feasible path, but path cost can be far from optimum. | Asymptotically optimal: $\lim_{N \to \infty} c_N = c^*$. |
| **(ii) Complexity per iteration** | $O(\log N)$ for nearest-neighbour query (KD-tree). | $O(\log N)$ for nearest + $O(k \log N)$ for rewiring. |
| **(iii) Rewiring step** | **Absent.** A new node $q_{new}$ is simply connected to its parent $q_{near}$. | **Present.** After adding $q_{new}$, all neighbours within radius $r_{RRT^*}$ are checked; if connecting through $q_{new}$ yields lower cost, the tree is rewired. $r_{RRT^*} = \gamma \left(\frac{\log N}{N}\right)^{1/d}$ for $d$-dimensional space. |
| **(iv) Kinodynamic constraints** | Naturally extends to kinodynamic RRT (steering function respects dynamics). | Significantly harder — rewiring must respect dynamics; $\text{kino-RRT*}$ exists but convergence rate is slower. |

**Conclusion (within Part b):** RRT* is preferred when optimality is critical and ample planning time is available; standard RRT is preferred for real-time applications or when only feasibility is required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (As per KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Writing pseudocode instead of derivations** in 14-mark answers. The Board Examiner deducts 2–3 marks if no proper derivation, equation, or proof is given in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing admissibility with consistency.** Admissibility: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$. Consistency: $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$. A* optimality only needs admissibility; for graph search re-expansion avoidance, consistency is needed.
> 3. **Omitting the parent-pointer backtracking** when describing A* — many students forget to mention path reconstruction and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up completeness terminology.** "Probabilistically complete" ≠ "resolution complete" ≠ "asymptotically complete." RRT is probabilistically complete; grid search is resolution complete.
> 5. **Forgetting the influence radius $\rho_0$** in repulsive potential — examiners specifically look for this parameter.
> 6. **No diagram/sketches** in 7-mark sub-parts of 14-mark questions. Always include at least one neat sketch.
> 7. **Wrong heuristic choice.** Saying "Euclidean is admissible everywhere" without specifying the 2-D continuous case loses marks on edge cases (8-connected grids).

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ = workspace with obstacles "grown" by robot radius $r$. All planners operate here.
- ✅ **Bug Algorithms** are sensor-based, complete, but not optimal. Bug1 is conservative; Bug2 is greedy.
- ✅ **Dijkstra** explores uniformly; $f(n) = g(n)$. Complexity $O(\vert V \vert^2)$.
- ✅ **A*** adds heuristic: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. **Optimal iff $h$ is admissible.**
- ✅ **D* Lite** is the gold standard for replanning in partially known maps.
- ✅ **Visibility Graph** is optimal and complete in 2-D polygons; $O(n^3)$ edge construction.
- ✅ **Wavefront** is optimal on grids; uses distance transform from goal.
- ✅ **Potential Field** is fast but suffers from **local minima**. Fix: harmonic potentials or random walks.
- ✅ **PRM** is a multi-query planner — build the roadmap once, query many times.
- ✅ **RRT** is a single-query planner — probabilistically complete, not optimal.
- ✅ **RRT*** adds rewiring and is **asymptotically optimal**.
- ✅ **Admissibility:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$. **Consistency:** $h(n) \leq c(n,n') + h(n')$.
- ✅ **Standard heuristics:** Euclidean (continuous), Manhattan (4-connected grid), Octile (8-connected grid).
- ✅ **Attractive potential** uses parabolic form: $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho^2$.
- ✅ **Repulsive potential** uses inverse-square form within influence radius $\rho_0$.
- ✅ **Completeness types:** Deterministic, Probabilistic, Resolution, Asymptotic.
- ✅ **Optimality types:** Optimal, Asymptotically optimal, Anytime.
- ✅ **Workspace assumptions:** Known vs Unknown, Static vs Dynamic, Continuous vs Discrete.
- ✅ **Algorithm selection criteria:** Workspace dimensionality, computational budget, optimality requirement, kinodynamic constraints.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Day Revision Strategy:** Memorise the **Formula Sheet Table** in §2.2 and the **Admissibility Derivation** in §3.1. These two alone will cover approximately 60% of the marks in any Module 1 ESE question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: History of Service Robotics, Present Status and Future Trends, Need for Service Robots, Applications, Examples and Specifications of Service and Field Robots, Non-conventional Industrial Robots, Aerial Robots, Collision Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT723 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** History of Service Robotics, Present Status and Future Trends, Need for Service Robots, Applications, Examples and Specifications of Service and Field Robots, Non-conventional Industrial Robots, Aerial Robots, Collision Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Service Robotics — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Service Robot):**
> A **service robot** is a robot that operates *semi- or fully autonomously* to perform services useful to the well-being of humans and equipment, **excluding manufacturing automation applications** (ISO 8373:2021). They assist humans by performing dull, dirty, dangerous, or distant (4D) tasks.

**Two Major Classes (KTU Approved Taxonomy):**

| Class | Full Form | Deployment Environment | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PRS** | Personal/Professional Service Robot | Non-industrial human spaces | Surgical robot (Da Vinci), Roomba |
| **FR** | Field Robot | Unstructured outdoor environments | Agricultural tractor, Mining robot, Mars Rover |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Helper" Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife** versus a **Factory Press Machine**. A factory press is a *manufacturing robot* — it does one job, in one place, all day. A Swiss Army Knife is a *service robot* — it has multiple tools (mobility, sensing, manipulation) and goes *to the task* rather than waiting for the task. The key shift is: **Industrial robots are stationary and task comes to them; Service robots are mobile and they go to the task.**

## 1.3 Field Robot — The "Unstructured" Specialist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Field Robot):**
> A field robot is a **service robot operating in unstructured, dynamic, outdoor environments** where it must negotiate variable terrain, weather, lighting, and obstacles without human-engineered infrastructure (lanes, rails, beacons).

**Analogy:** Think of a **farm tractor with autopilot** crossing muddy, sloped terrain vs. a **factory AGV** following painted lines on a smooth floor. Both are mobile, but the field robot must *think* far more because the environment cannot be controlled.

## 1.4 Non-Conventional Industrial Robot — The "Beyond the Cage" Class

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Robots that *break the traditional industrial paradigm* of being fixed-base, fenced, high-payload, position-controlled 6-DOF arms. Includes **collaborative robots (cobots)**, **parallel robots (Delta)**, **flexible/flexure-based robots**, and **cable-driven robots**.

**Analogy:** A **classical industrial robot** is like a **grandfather clock** — heavy, fixed, accurate only in its place. A **cobot** is like a **smartwatch** — light, mobile-friendly, safe to share space with, and adaptive.

## 1.5 Aerial Robot (UAV/Drone) — The "3D Worker"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:**
> An aerial robot is an **unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)** equipped with onboard intelligence (sensors, processors, actuators) capable of autonomous or semi-autonomous flight, perception, and task execution.

**Analogy:** A **helicopter** is piloted; an **aerial robot (drone)** is a *flying smartphone* — it carries cameras, GPS, IMU, and computing boards, and can be programmed to make decisions mid-air.

## 1.6 Collision Avoidance — The "Reflex Arc"

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A reactive or deliberative control strategy that enables a robot to **detect** an impending collision, **evaluate** alternatives, and **execute** a trajectory correction to maintain a *minimum safe distance* from obstacles or other agents.

**Analogy:** The **tap-root reflex** in humans — when your hand nears a hot surface, you withdraw *before* burning. Collision avoidance is the *robot's spinal reflex*: a *Sense → Think → Act* loop running in milliseconds.

---

## 1.7 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU-Boards Frequently Tested)

- **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** — independent motions of a mechanism
- **Payload (kg)** — maximum load the robot can carry at rated performance
- **Reach / Workspace (m)** — maximum extent of the end-effector
- **Repeatability (±mm)** — *precision of returning to a taught position*
- **Accuracy (mm)** — *deviation from a commanded absolute position*
- **Maximum Linear Speed (m/s)** — for mobile/aerial robots
- **Endurance (min)** — flight/operation time on single charge
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — reliability metric in **hours**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students confuse **Repeatability** and **Accuracy**. A robot can be **highly repeatable but inaccurate** (e.g., it returns to the *same wrong point* every time). Accuracy is about *hitting the target*; repeatability is about *hitting the same point*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 History of Service Robotics — Structured Timeline

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Depth:** Students should know *milestones, key inventors, and paradigm shifts* — not exhaustive dates.

### Evolution Phases

1. **Phase 1 — Proto-Service Era (1950s–1979): Birth of Mobile Platforms**
   - **1954:** George Devol files the first industrial robot patent (Unimate) — sets the conceptual foundation.
   - **1966:** Stanford Research Institute's **Shakey the Robot** — first mobile robot to reason about its actions (A*, STRIPS planner).
   - **1969:** *Mobot* (Stanford) — first field-deployable mobile manipulator.
   - **1979:** Stanford Cart — first autonomous road-following vehicle.

2. **Phase 2 — Specialization Era (1980s–1999): Field Robotics Emerges**
   - **1980s:** DARPA-funded **Autonomous Land Vehicle (ALV)** project.
   - **1986:** First **underwater ROV** used for Titanic wreck exploration.
   - **1990s:** NASA's **Mars Pathfinder / Sojourner Rover** (1997) — first successful planetary field robot.
   - **1996:** *Roomba* precursors (iRobot founded 1990) — vacuum cleaning domain matures.
   - **1999:** *Sony AIBO* — first mass-market **social/service entertainment robot**.

3. **Phase 3 — Commercialization Era (2000s–2014): Market Explosion**
   - **2002:** *iRobot Roomba* commercial launch — 1 million+ units sold by 2004.
   - **2005:** *PackBot* deployed in Iraq/Afghanistan — first combat EOD service robot.
   - **2010:** *Parrot AR.Drone* — first smartphone-controlled quadrotor for civilians.
   - **2012:** *Kiva Systems* (now Amazon Robotics) — warehouse mobile robots at scale.
   - **2014:** *DJI Phantom 3* brings professional cinematography to consumers.

4. **Phase 4 — Intelligence & Autonomy Era (2015–Present): AI-Driven Service Robots**
   - **2015:** SoftBank's *Pepper* — first robot to "read" human emotions.
   - **2016:** *Hanson Sophia* — advanced human-like social interaction.
   - **2017–2020:** Spot (Boston Dynamics), Digit (Agility), Stretch — logistics & inspection robots.
   - **2020–2024:** Generative-AI integration, foundation models for robot control, autonomous last-mile delivery (Starship, Nuro).
   - **2024:** Foundation-model-driven general-purpose service robots (e.g., Tesla Optimus, Figure 01, 1X Neo).

## 2.2 Present Status and Future Trends — KTU Board-Favored Sub-Topics

### 2.2.1 Present Status (as of 2024)

- **Market size:** Global service robotics market ≈ **USD 50+ billion** (2024), CAGR ~15%.
- **Service density:**
  - ~**30 million+** domestic service robots (vacuum, lawn, entertainment).
  - ~**1.5 million+** professional service robots (medical, logistics, agriculture).
- **Penetration leaders (geography):** USA, Japan, Germany, South Korea, China.
- **Penetration leaders (sector):** Logistics/warehousing, medical surgery, agriculture, defense, hospitality.

### 2.2.2 Future Trends

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Trends (memorize these for 7-mark questions):**

1. **Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC)** — cobots sharing workspace with humans.
2. **AI/Foundation Models in Robotics** — LLMs as high-level planners (e.g., RT-2, PaLM-E).
3. **Soft Robotics** — compliant, bio-inspired grippers for delicate manipulation.
4. **Swarm Robotics** — large-scale coordination (agriculture, search & rescue).
5. **Cloud Robotics** — offloading compute to edge/cloud; shared knowledge.
6. **Humanoid Robots** — Digit, Optimus, Figure 01 entering factories.
7. **Autonomous Last-Mile Delivery** — Nuro, Starship, sidewalk robots.
8. **Medical/Assistive Robotics** — exoskeletons, surgical micro-robots, elder care.
9. **Digital Twins + Simulation** — Isaac Sim, Gazebo for training.
10. **Sustainable/Agri-Robotics** — precision farming, pollination drones.

## 2.3 Need for Service Robots — The "4D" Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4D Tasks Framework (KTU-Board Favored):**

| D | Description | Example Service Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Dull** | Repetitive, monotonous | Floor cleaning (Roomba), warehouse picking (Kiva) |
| **Dirty** | Hazardous to human hygiene | Sewer cleaning, nuclear decontamination |
| **Dangerous** | Life-threatening to humans | EOD (PackBot), fire-fighting, bomb disposal |
| **Distant** | Beyond human reach (planetary, deep sea) | Mars Rover, ROVs at Mariana Trench |

**Additional Drivers (KTU Expected):**
- **Aging populations** (Japan, Europe) → elder-care robots.
- **Labor shortages** in agriculture and logistics.
- **Pandemic hygiene** → disinfection robots (UV-C), food delivery.
- **Precision agriculture** → increasing yield with fewer chemicals.
- **Precision medicine** → minimally invasive surgery with sub-mm accuracy.

## 2.4 Applications of Service and Field Robots

### 2.4.1 Service Robot Applications Matrix

| Domain | Application | Representative Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Domestic** | Vacuuming, mopping, lawn mowing | Roomba, Husqvarna Automower |
| **Medical** | Surgery, rehab, telepresence | Da Vinci, PARO (seal pup) |
| **Logistics** | Warehouse picking, last-mile | Kiva/Amazon, Starship |
| **Hospitality** | Concierge, room service | Relay, Pepper |
| **Defense/EOD** | Bomb disposal, recon | PackBot, Talon |
| **Entertainment** | Toys, companions | AIBO, Lovot |
| **Inspection** | Pipelines, power plants | ANYmal, Spot |
| **Agriculture** | Harvesting, weeding, milking | Naio Oz, Lely Astronaut |
| **Underwater** | Wreck, coral, pipeline | Jason ROV, Aqua |
| **Space** | Planetary exploration | Perseverance, VIPER |
| **Aerial** | Surveillance, mapping, delivery | DJI Matrice, Wing |
| **Disaster Response** | Search & rescue | Snake robots, Spot |

### 2.4.2 Field Robot Applications (Unstructured Outdoors)

1. **Agriculture** — autonomous tractors (John Deere 8R), weeding (Naio), fruit picking.
2. **Mining** — autonomous haul trucks (Caterpillar), drilling robots.
3. **Forestry** — automated log cutting, fire detection drones.
4. **Construction** — brick-laying (SAM100), 3D-printing buildings.
5. **Inspection of Infrastructure** — bridge cables, wind turbines, pipelines.
6. **Planetary Exploration** — Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance).
7. **Underwater/Subsea** — pipeline inspection, marine biology.
8. **Military/Defense** — perimeter patrol, route clearance.

## 2.5 Examples and Specifications of Service and Field Robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Specification Table** — memorize payload, reach, repeatability, sensors.

### 2.5.1 Specifications of Key Service Robots

| Robot | Type | DOF | Payload | Reach / Workspace | Sensors | Repeatability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Da Vinci Si** | Surgical | 7 DOF arms | — | Instrument tip reach ~30 cm | Endoscope, force | **± 0.1 mm** |
| **Roomba i7+** | Domestic | 2 drive wheels | — | Whole floor | IR, cliff, bumper | — |
| **Pepper** | Social | 20 DOF | — | 1.2 m height | Camera, sonar, IMU | — |
| **Spot (Boston Dyn.)** | Quadruped | 12 DOF | 14 kg | ~1.1 m height, 1.6 m reach | LIDAR, stereo cam | — |
| **Amazon Kiva** | Mobile base | 2 drive | 450 kg | Whole warehouse | Floor QR + cam | ± 25 mm |
| **Pepper** (service) | Humanoid | 20 | — | — | 3D cam, sonar | — |

### 2.5.2 Specifications of Key Field Robots

| Robot | Domain | DOF | Max Speed | Sensors | Endurance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mars Perseverance** | Planetary | 7-arm + 6-wheel | 152 m/h | Navcams, Hazcams, LIDAR, MOXIE | Multi-year |
| **Caterpillar 797F** | Mining truck | 4 wheels | 64 km/h (loaded) | GPS, RADAR, LIDAR | 24/7 |
| **John Deere 8R 410** | Agriculture | 4 wheels | 18 km/h | RTK-GPS, LIDAR, RGB cam | 14 h |
| **Naio Oz** | Agriculture (weeding) | 4 wheels | 1.5 km/h | GPS, RGB, ultrasonic | 8 h |
| **ANYmal C** | Inspection | 12 DOF legs | 1.3 m/s | Depth cam, LIDAR | 2 h |
| **DJI Matrice 350** | Aerial inspection | 6 rotors | 23 m/s | LIDAR, RGB, thermal, RTK | 55 min |

## 2.6 Non-Conventional Industrial Robots — Detailed Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Conventional"?** Classical industrial robots (PUMA, ABB IRB) are **serial, rigid, heavy, position-controlled, and fenced**. Non-conventional variants break *at least one* of these.

### 2.6.1 Classification

1. **Parallel Manipulators (Delta Robots)**
   - 3-DOF (translational) or 6-DOF versions.
   - *Use:* high-speed pick-and-place (packaging, pharma).
   - *Example:* ABB FlexPicker, Fanuc M-1.
   - *Speed:* up to **300 picks/min**.

2. **Collaborative Robots (Cobots)**
   - Designed to work *with* humans, not separated by cages.
   - *Safety mechanisms:* power/force limiting, skin sensors, speed monitoring.
   - *Examples:* Universal Robots UR5e (5 kg, ±0.03 mm), KUKA LBR iiwa (7 kg, 7 DOF).

3. **Cable-Driven Parallel Robots (CDPR)**
   - Cables replace rigid links — large workspace, low inertia.
   - *Examples:* SkyCam (stadium cameras), NIST RoboCrane.

4. **Soft/Flexible Robots**
   - Made of elastomers — compliant, safe, adaptive.
   - *Examples:* Festo BionicSoftHand, OctArm (soft continuum).

5. **Redundant Manipulators**
   - DOF > required for the task — better maneuverability in cluttered spaces.
   - *Examples:* 7-DOF arms (KUKA LWR, Franka Panda).

6. **Wearable/Exoskeleton Robots**
   - Worn by human — augment strength/endurance.
   - *Examples:* Sarcos Guardian XO, Ekso GT (medical).

### 2.6.2 Specifications of Non-Conventional Industrial Robots

| Robot | Type | DOF | Payload | Repeatability | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ABB FlexPicker IRB 360** | Delta parallel | 3–4 | 1–8 kg | **± 0.1 mm** | 150+ picks/min |
| **UR5e (Universal Robots)** | Cobot | 6 | 5 kg | **± 0.03 mm** | Power/force limited |
| **KUKA LBR iiwa 14 R820** | Cobot | 7 | 14 kg | **± 0.05 mm** | Joint torque sensing |
| **Franka Panda** | Cobot | 7 | 3 kg | **± 0.1 mm** | Torque at every joint |
| **Festo BionicSoftHand** | Soft | 12+ | — | — | Pneumatic, compliant |

## 2.7 Aerial Robots — Detailed Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Sub-Topic — High Weightage.**

### 2.7.1 Classification of Aerial Robots

| Type | Lift Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-wing UAV** | Forward airspeed → wing lift | Cessna-sized military drones (Predator) |
| **Multirotor** | Multiple rotors | Quadrotor, hexarotor (DJI Phantom) |
| **Single-rotor helicopter** | Main + tail rotor | Yamaha RMAX (agriculture) |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Tilting rotors + wings | WingtraOne, Quantum Trinity |
| **Flapping-wing / Ornithopter** | Bio-inspired | RoboBee (Harvard) |

### 2.7.2 Why Quadrotors Dominate

- **Simplicity** — 4 fixed-pitch propellers, no swashplate.
- **Maneuverability** — omnidirectional in hover.
- **Mechanical simplicity** — no variable-pitch mechanism.
- **Failure tolerance** — some hexa/octocopters tolerate motor loss.

### 2.7.3 Quadrotor Dynamics — Key Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Coordinate Frames:**
> - **Earth/Inertial frame (E):** $x_E, y_E, z_E$ (NED: North-East-Down)
> - **Body frame (B):** $x_B, y_B, z_B$ (forward-right-down)
> - Rotation from B to E: Rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ using **roll–pitch–yaw** Euler angles.

**Total Thrust Equation:**

$$T = b(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2)$$

where $b$ is the thrust coefficient and $\omega_i$ is the rotor speed of motor $i$.

**Body Torques (roll, pitch, yaw):**

$$\tau_\phi = b l (\omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

$$\tau_\theta = b l (\omega_3^2 - \omega_1^2)$$

$$\tau_\psi = d (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2)$$

where $l$ = arm length, $d$ = drag coefficient.

**Newton-Euler Translational Dynamics (E-frame):**

$$m \ddot{\mathbf{r}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{R} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{F}_{disturbance}$$

**Newton-Euler Rotational Dynamics (B-frame):**

$$\mathbf{I} \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$$

### 2.7.4 Hover Condition

> [!NOTE]
> For a quadrotor in steady hover, **total thrust equals weight:**
> $$T_{hover} = mg$$
> Each rotor contributes $T/4$ (for symmetric quadrotor).

## 2.8 Collision Avoidance — Detailed Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-weightage module topic for KTU Board Exams.**

### 2.8.1 Definitions

- **Obstacle Avoidance (Reactive):** Local, short-horizon, immediate response.
- **Path Planning (Deliberative):** Global, full-map, optimal path computation.
- **Collision Avoidance:** Hybrid — combines local sensing with global knowledge.

### 2.8.2 Classification of Collision Avoidance Techniques

| Approach | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bug Algorithms** | Walk along obstacle boundary | Simple, low compute | Path inefficiency |
| **Potential Field (PF)** | Attractive goal + repulsive obstacles | Smooth trajectories | Local minima |
| **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** | Polar histogram of obstacles | Real-time, robust | Tuning-intensive |
| **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** | Search in velocity space | Considers dynamics | Short horizon |
| **Velocity Obstacle (VO)** | Forbidden velocity region | Multi-agent capable | Reactive only |
| **RRT / RRT*** | Sampling-based planners | High-DOF capable | Stochastic |
| **A* / D* on grids** | Heuristic graph search | Optimal (A*) | Memory hungry |
| **Learning-based (RL/CNN)** | Learns from data | Handles unstructured | Black-box |

### 2.8.3 Potential Field Method — Detailed

**Artificial Potential Field (APF) Equation:**

$$U_{total}(\mathbf{q}) = U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + U_{rep}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Attractive Potential (goal-seeking):**

$$U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot \rho^2_{goal}(\mathbf{q})$$

where $\rho_{goal}(\mathbf{q}) = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \rVert$ is distance to goal.

**Attractive Force (negative gradient):**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = -k_{att} (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$$

**Repulsive Potential (obstacle-avoiding):**

$$
U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left( \frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{q}) \leq \rho_0 \\
0, & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0
\end{cases}
$$

where $\rho(\mathbf{q})$ is the *shortest distance to the obstacle* and $\rho_0$ is the *influence radius*.

**Repulsive Force:**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{cases}
k_{rep}\left( \frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2(\mathbf{q})} \hat{\mathbf{n}}, & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{q}) \leq \rho_0 \\
\mathbf{0}, & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0
\end{cases}
$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ points from obstacle to robot.

**Total Force (driving the robot):**

$$\mathbf{F}_{total}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_i \mathbf{F}_{rep,i}(\mathbf{q})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Local Minima Problem:** APF can trap the robot when attractive and repulsive forces *cancel* (e.g., in a U-shaped corridor). Solutions: random walks, virtual obstacles, navigation functions.

### 2.8.4 Velocity Obstacle (VO) Method

**Relative velocity concept:**
Given robot $A$ at position $\mathbf{p}_A$, velocity $\mathbf{v}_A$, and obstacle $B$ at $\mathbf{p}_B$, $\mathbf{v}_B$:

$$\mathbf{v}_{rel} = \mathbf{v}_A - \mathbf{v}_B$$

$$\mathbf{p}_{rel} = \mathbf{p}_A - \mathbf{p}_B$$

**Velocity Obstacle (VO):** The set of robot velocities $\mathbf{v}_A$ such that $A$ and $B$ will collide within a time horizon $\tau$:

$$VO = \left\{ \mathbf{v}_A \;\big|\; \exists t \in [0, \tau], \; \lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} + \mathbf{v}_{rel} t \rVert \leq r_A + r_B \right\}$$

**Collision Cone Geometry:** The forbidden velocities lie inside a cone with apex at $\mathbf{v}_B$ and axis along $\mathbf{p}_{rel}/t_{collision}$.

### 2.8.5 Time-to-Collision (TTC)

$$t_{collision} = \frac{-\mathbf{p}_{rel} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{rel} - \sqrt{(\mathbf{p}_{rel} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{rel})^2 - \lVert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \rVert^2 (\lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} \rVert^2 - r_{safe}^2)}}{\lVert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \rVert^2}$$

If $t_{collision} < t_{threshold}$, a *braking or avoidance maneuver* is triggered.

**Safety Distance (Bubble Boundary):**

$$d_{safe} = v \cdot t_{reaction} + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}} + d_{margin}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often miss the **second-order braking term** $v^2/(2a_{max})$ — that's the *stopping distance*, not just reaction distance.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Service Robot Definition** | ISO 8373: operates semi/fully autonomously for human use, *non-manufacturing* | — |
| 2 | **4D Tasks** | Dull, Dirty, Dangerous, Distant | — |
| 3 | **Repeatability vs Accuracy** | Repeat = same point; Accuracy = right point | ± mm |
| 4 | **Quadrotor Hover** | $T = mg$ | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | **Quadrotor Thrust** | $T = b \sum \omega_i^2$ | N |
| 6 | **Body Torques** | $\tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi$ depend on rotor speed asymmetry | N·m |
| 7 | **Newton-Euler Trans.** | $m\ddot{\mathbf{r}} = m\mathbf{g} + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{F}_{thrust}$ | m/s² |
| 8 | **Newton-Euler Rot.** | $\mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ | rad/s² |
| 9 | **APF Attractive Force** | $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -k_{att}(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$ | N |
| 10 | **APF Repulsive Force** | $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = k_{rep}(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0) \cdot \rho^{-2} \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ | N |
| 11 | **Velocity Obstacle Set** | Set of $\mathbf{v}_A$ causing collision within $\tau$ | m/s |
| 12 | **Time-to-Collision** | $t_c$ from relative kinematics | seconds |
| 13 | **Safe Stopping Distance** | $d_{safe} = v t_r + v^2/(2a_{max}) + d_{margin}$ | m |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** For 14-mark questions, derivations must be *exhaustive*, *self-contained*, and *physically meaningful*. Show **every algebraic transition**.

## 3.1 Derivation: Quadrotor Hover Thrust from Newton's Second Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** This derivation is the *foundation* of every aerial-robotics problem. KTU boards test it directly.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A quadrotor of mass $m = 1.5 \text{ kg}$ is hovering in steady state. Each rotor produces thrust $T_i$ proportional to the square of its angular velocity: $T_i = b \omega_i^2$, with $b = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$. Find the *required rotor speed* $\omega_i$ in hover.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the vertical force balance.**

In steady hover, linear acceleration $\ddot{z} = 0$. The forces on the quadrotor are gravity $-mg$ and total rotor thrust $T_{total}$ acting upward.

$$
\sum F_z = m \ddot{z} = 0
$$

$$
T_{total} - mg = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for total thrust.**

$$
T_{total} = mg
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute given values.**

$$
T_{total} = (1.5 \text{ kg})(9.81 \text{ m/s}^2) = 14.715 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Express total thrust as the sum of four rotor thrusts.**

By symmetry, all four rotors operate at the same hover speed $\omega_{hover}$ in steady hover:

$$
T_{total} = 4 \cdot T_i = 4 b \omega_{hover}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\omega_{hover}$.**

$$
\omega_{hover}^2 = \frac{T_{total}}{4b}
$$

$$
\omega_{hover}^2 = \frac{14.715}{4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{14.715}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\omega_{hover}^2 = 306,562.5 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
\omega_{hover} = \sqrt{306562.5} \approx 553.68 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to RPM for intuition.**

$$
\text{RPM} = \omega_{hover} \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 553.68 \times 9.5493 \approx 5287 \text{ RPM}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 1.5 kg quadrotor requires each rotor to spin at **~5287 RPM** in hover — consistent with small hobbyist drones.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Artificial Potential Field (APF) Forces from Energy Gradient

### 3.2.1 Goal: Derive the Total Force on a Mobile Robot in an APF

**Step 1 — Define the attractive potential.**

The attractive potential is quadratic in distance to goal, with gain $k_{att} > 0$:

$$
U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho_{goal}^2(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \rVert^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient (attractive force = negative gradient).**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = -k_{att}(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})
$$

> This is a **linear restoring force** pulling the robot toward the goal.

**Step 3 — Define the repulsive potential for a single obstacle.**

Let $\rho(\mathbf{q}) = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs} \rVert$ be the distance to the obstacle. The repulsive potential is *active* only within the influence radius $\rho_0$:

$$
U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) \leq \rho_0 \\[4pt] 0, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient of the repulsive potential.**

Apply the chain rule. Let $f(\mathbf{q}) = \dfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$. Then $U_{rep} = \dfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} f^2$.

$$
\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} f(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \nabla f(\mathbf{q})
$$

Since $\nabla \rho = \dfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs}}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} = \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ (unit outward normal):

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{q}) = -\frac{1}{\rho^2(\mathbf{q})} \hat{\mathbf{n}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\nabla U_{rep} = -k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2(\mathbf{q})} \hat{\mathbf{n}}
$$

**Step 5 — Repulsive force is the negative gradient.**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2(\mathbf{q})} \hat{\mathbf{n}}
$$

> The force grows as $1/\rho^3$ near the obstacle — sharply increasing as the robot approaches the obstacle.

**Step 6 — Total force for $n$ obstacles.**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{total}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{F}_{rep,i}(\mathbf{q})
$$

The robot's velocity update is then:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{robot}(\mathbf{q}) = \kappa \cdot \frac{\mathbf{F}_{total}}{\lVert \mathbf{F}_{total} \rVert + \epsilon}
$$

where $\kappa$ is the *speed gain* and $\epsilon$ is a small constant to avoid division by zero.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Safe Stopping Distance for a Mobile Robot

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A service robot moves at $v = 1.2$ m/s. Its maximum deceleration is $a_{max} = 2$ m/s². Its reaction time is $t_r = 0.2$ s. The required safety margin is $d_{margin} = 0.1$ m. Compute the **safe stopping distance** $d_{safe}$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Distance covered during reaction time (linear motion).**

$$
d_{reaction} = v \cdot t_r = (1.2)(0.2) = 0.24 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Braking distance (kinematics: $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d$).**

Final velocity $v_f = 0$, so:

$$
0 = v^2 - 2 a_{max} d_{brake}
$$

$$
d_{brake} = \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{2 \times 2} = \frac{1.44}{4} = 0.36 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Add safety margin.**

$$
d_{safe} = d_{reaction} + d_{brake} + d_{margin} = 0.24 + 0.36 + 0.1 = 0.70 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> The robot must keep **at least 70 cm** of clear space ahead. If a sensor detects an obstacle closer than 70 cm while moving at 1.2 m/s, the robot *cannot* stop in time — a *collision is unavoidable* without steering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why *velocity scaling* is fundamental — slow down when obstacles are near, fast when the path is clear.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Velocity Obstacle for Multi-Agent Collision Avoidance

### 3.4.1 Setup

Robot A: position $\mathbf{p}_A$, velocity $\mathbf{v}_A$, radius $r_A$.
Robot B: position $\mathbf{p}_B$, velocity $\mathbf{v}_B$, radius $r_B$.

**Step 1 — Reduce to a point-obstacle problem.**

Define a *virtual* point at $\mathbf{p}_A$ and a *virtual* obstacle of radius $r_{AB} = r_A + r_B$ at $\mathbf{p}_B$.

**Step 2 — Compute relative position and velocity.**

$$
\mathbf{p}_{rel} = \mathbf{p}_A - \mathbf{p}_B, \quad \mathbf{v}_{rel} = \mathbf{v}_A - \mathbf{v}_B
$$

**Step 3 — Time-to-collision formula.**

A collision occurs at time $t$ if $\lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} + \mathbf{v}_{rel} t \rVert = r_{AB}$. Solving the quadratic for the smallest positive $t$:

$$
t_{collision} = \frac{-\mathbf{p}_{rel} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{rel} - \sqrt{(\mathbf{p}_{rel} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{rel})^2 - \lVert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \rVert^2 (\lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} \rVert^2 - r_{AB}^2)}}{\lVert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \rVert^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric interpretation — Collision Cone.**

In the *velocity space* of robot A, the set of $\mathbf{v}_A$ leading to a collision is a *cone* with apex at $\mathbf{v}_B$ and angular extent $2\alpha$ where:

$$
\sin \alpha = \frac{r_{AB}}{\lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} \rVert}
$$

**Step 5 — Avoidance maneuver.**

Choose a velocity $\mathbf{v}_A$ *outside* the cone — typically on the *right* or *left* boundary for a *minimal deviation* maneuver. This is the basis of **ORCA (Optimal Reciprocal Collision Avoidance)** and **RVO (Reciprocal Velocity Obstacles)**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Collision Avoidance Methods

| Method | Computation Cost | Optimality | Handles Dynamics | Multi-Agent | Local Minima? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bug** | $O(n)$ | No | No | No | No |
| **APF** | $O(m)$ | No (local optima) | Limited | Limited | **Yes** |
| **VFH** | $O(m)$ | No | Yes (with DWA) | Limited | No |
| **DWA** | $O(k^2)$ | Local | **Yes** | Limited | No |
| **A*** | $O(b^d)$ | **Yes** | No | No | No |
| **RRT*** | $O(n \log n)$ | Asymptotic | No | Limited | No |
| **VO** | $O(m)$ | No | Yes | **Yes** | No |
| **ORCA** | $O(m)$ | **Yes** (reciprocal) | **Yes** | **Yes** | No |
| **Deep RL** | Train $O(?)$, Run $O(1)$ | Empirical | Yes | Yes | No (in principle) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** $m$ = number of obstacles, $n$ = path length, $k$ = velocity samples, $d$ = grid dimension, $b$ = branching factor.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — A Field Robot Crosses a Field

**Scenario:** An agricultural robot is weeding a 2-hectare field at 1.5 m/s. Row spacing is 0.5 m. Battery endurance is 6 hours.

**Step 1 — Compute row length traversed per hour.**

Speed: $1.5$ m/s → $1.5 \times 3600 = 5400$ m/h.

**Step 2 — Total row distance covered in 6 hours.**

$$
D = 5400 \times 6 = 32{,}400 \text{ m} = 32.4 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute area covered (assuming every row 0.5 m apart).**

$$
A = 32{,}400 \times 0.5 = 16{,}200 \text{ m}^2 \approx 1.62 \text{ hectares}
$$

**Step 4 — Recharging cycles needed.**

For 2 hectares: $\frac{2}{1.62} \approx 1.23$ → needs **2 battery cycles** (or a hot-swap).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students forget to account for *turning time at row ends*. In practice, this adds 5–15% to total mission time.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary — Selection of Aerial Robot Type

| Mission Profile | Best Aerial Robot Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Long-range surveillance (>100 km) | **Fixed-wing** | High endurance, aerodynamic efficiency |
| Indoor inspection / hovering payload work | **Quadrotor** | VTOL, precise hover |
| Heavy payload (>5 kg) over moderate distance | **Single-rotor heli** or **Octocopter** | Higher lift efficiency, redundancy |
| Surveying with both hover and long flight | **Hybrid VTOL** | Combines strengths |
| Micro-scale (insect-sized) reconnaissance | **Flapping-wing** | Bio-inspired stealth, low Reynolds |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Service Robot
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q:** Define a *service robot* as per ISO 8373. How does it differ from an *industrial robot*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **service robot** is a robot that operates *semi- or fully autonomously* to perform services useful to the well-being of humans and equipment, **excluding industrial automation applications** (ISO 8373:2021). **Difference from industrial robot:** Industrial robots perform *manufacturing* tasks (welding, painting, assembly) in *structured factory environments* behind safety fences, while service robots perform *non-manufacturing* tasks in *human-populated, often unstructured* environments. Industrial robots are typically *stationary* with high repeatability; service robots are often *mobile* with high autonomy and human-interaction capability.

### Question 2: 4D Tasks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q:** List and briefly explain the **4D tasks** that justify the deployment of service robots.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The 4D tasks are:
> - **Dull:** Repetitive, monotonous tasks (e.g., floor cleaning, warehouse picking).
> - **Dirty:** Tasks involving contaminants (sewer cleaning, nuclear decontamination).
> - **Dangerous:** Tasks posing life risks (EOD, fire-fighting, mining).
> - **Distant:** Tasks beyond human reach (deep-sea ROVs, planetary rovers).
> Robots are deployed for these tasks because they can perform *24/7* without fatigue, health risk, or environmental constraints.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Full Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the **history and evolution of service robotics** with reference to key milestones such as Shakey, Mars Pathfinder, Roomba, and the modern humanoid era. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **applications, examples, and specifications** of *field robots*. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Proto-Service Era (1960s–1980s):** Shakey the Robot (1966, SRI) was the first mobile robot to reason about its actions using STRIPS planner. Stanford Cart (1979) achieved autonomous road-following. These established the foundations of *perception-planning-action* loops. **(2 marks)**

**2. Field Robotics Emergence (1980s–1990s):** DARPA's ALV project (1984–) advanced autonomous navigation. ROVs explored the Titanic wreck (1985). NASA's Mars Pathfinder/Sojourner (1997) became the first successful *planetary* field robot, validating long-duration autonomous operation. **(2 marks)**

**3. Commercialization (2000s):** iRobot's Roomba (2002) brought domestic service robots to mass market — over 30 million sold cumulatively. PackBot (2005) deployed in Iraq for EOD. Kiva Systems (2012, acquired by Amazon) revolutionized warehouse logistics with hundreds of mobile units. **(1.5 marks)**

**4. AI-Driven Modern Era (2015–):** Spot (Boston Dynamics, 2017) advanced legged mobility. Humanoid robots like Digit (Agility, 2019), Optimus (Tesla, 2022), and Figure 01 (2023) mark the dawn of *general-purpose bipedal* service robots. Foundation models (RT-2, PaLM-E) integrate LLMs with robot policies, enabling natural-language task specification. **(1.5 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

A **field robot** is a service robot operating in *unstructured, outdoor, dynamic* environments. **(0.5 marks)**

**Major Applications:** **(3 marks)**
- **Agriculture:** autonomous tractors (John Deere 8R), precision weeding (Naio Oz, Ecorobotix), fruit picking.
- **Mining:** autonomous haul trucks (Caterpillar 797F), drilling rigs.
- **Forestry:** automated log cutting, fire-detection drones.
- **Construction:** brick-laying (SAM100), 3D printing of buildings.
- **Planetary exploration:** Mars Perseverance (NASA), VIPER (lunar).
- **Infrastructure inspection:** bridges, wind turbines, pipelines (ANYmal, Spot).
- **Subsea:** pipeline inspection, marine biology.
- **Defense:** route clearance, perimeter patrol (PackBot, Talon).

**Examples and Specifications Table:** **(3.5 marks)**

| Robot | Domain | DOF | Speed | Key Sensors | Payload/Reach | Endurance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mars Perseverance** | Planetary | 7-arm + 6-wheel | 152 m/h | Navcams, Hazcams, MOXIE | 7-arm reach ~2.1 m | Multi-year |
| **John Deere 8R 410** | Agriculture | 4 wheels | 18 km/h | RTK-GPS, LIDAR, RGB | 450 hp engine | 14 h |
| **Naio Oz** | Weeding | 4 wheels | 1.5 km/h | GPS, RGB, US | 80 kg | 8 h |
| **ANYmal C** | Inspection | 12 DOF legs | 1.3 m/s | Depth cam, LIDAR | 10 kg | 2 h |
| **Caterpillar 797F** | Mining | 4 wheels | 64 km/h | GPS, RADAR, LIDAR | 400 t payload | 24/7 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often (i) confuse **reach** with **workspace** (reach is a *scalar* max distance; workspace is the *set* of all reachable points), (ii) forget to mention the *terrain capability* (slope, step height) of field robots, (iii) miss the *sensor fusion* architecture which is the heart of field autonomy.

---

### Question B — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With neat schematics and equations, explain the **dynamic model of a quadrotor** in hover. Derive the **hover thrust condition**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **Velocity Obstacle (VO) method** for collision avoidance in detail, including the **time-to-collision** derivation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** A quadrotor is a 4-rotor VTOL aircraft with fixed-pitch propellers. Thrust and torques are controlled by varying the *rotor angular speeds* $\omega_i$.

**2. Coordinate Frames (1 mark):** Earth frame $\{E\}$ (NED) and body frame $\{B\}$ (front-right-down) related by a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ where $\phi$ = roll, $\theta$ = pitch, $\psi$ = yaw.

**3. Translational Dynamics (2 marks):** Using Newton's second law in the earth frame:

$$
m\ddot{\mathbf{r}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{R} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $T$ is the total rotor thrust.

**4. Rotational Dynamics (1.5 marks):** Using the Newton–Euler equation in the body frame:

$$
\mathbf{I} \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})
$$

where $\mathbf{I}$ is the inertia matrix and $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [p, q, r]^T$ is the body angular velocity.

**5. Thrust and Torque from Rotors (1 mark):**

$$
T = b \sum_{i=1}^{4} \omega_i^2
$$

$$
\tau_\phi = bl(\omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2), \quad \tau_\theta = bl(\omega_3^2 - \omega_1^2), \quad \tau_\psi = d(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2)
$$

**6. Hover Condition (0.5 marks):** In steady hover, $\ddot{\mathbf{r}} = 0$ and $\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = 0$, so $T = mg$.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**1. Problem Setup (1 mark):** Two agents A (robot) and B (obstacle) move with positions $\mathbf{p}_A, \mathbf{p}_B$ and velocities $\mathbf{v}_A, \mathbf{v}_B$. Radii $r_A$ and $r_B$.

**2. Relative Coordinates (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{p}_{rel} = \mathbf{p}_A - \mathbf{p}_B, \quad \mathbf{v}_{rel} = \mathbf{v}_A - \mathbf{v}_B
$$

**3. Collision Condition (1.5 marks):** A collision occurs at time $t > 0$ if $\lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} + \mathbf{v}_{rel} t \rVert \leq r_A + r_B = r_{AB}$.

**4. Velocity Obstacle Set (1.5 marks):**

$$
VO_{A|B}^{\tau} = \left\{ \mathbf{v}_A \;\big|\; \exists t \in [0,\tau], \lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} + \mathbf{v}_{rel} t \rVert \leq r_{AB} \right\}
$$

**5. Time-to-Collision Derivation (2 marks):** Solving the quadratic $\lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} + \mathbf{v}_{rel} t \rVert^2 = r_{AB}^2$ for the smallest positive root:

$$
t_{collision} = \frac{-\mathbf{p}_{rel} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{rel} - \sqrt{(\mathbf{p}_{rel} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{rel})^2 - \lVert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \rVert^2 (\lVert \mathbf{p}_{rel} \rVert^2 - r_{AB}^2)}}{\lVert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \rVert^2}
$$

If the discriminant is negative, no collision occurs.

**6. Avoidance Strategy (1 mark):** Choose $\mathbf{v}_A$ *outside* the VO cone. The optimal choice minimizes deviation from the preferred velocity, yielding **RVO/ORCA** algorithms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing **VO with the polar histogram (VFH)** — VO is a *velocity-space* method, VFH is *position-space*.
> 2. Forgetting the **time horizon $\tau$** — without $\tau$, the agent may avoid *all future* collisions unnecessarily.
> 3. Not handling the **no-collision case** (negative discriminant).
> 4. Ignoring **reciprocity** — pure VO causes oscillations; ORCA solves this by sharing avoidance responsibility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Quick-Reference Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Marks-Loss Traps in Module 2:**
> 1. **Confusing Repeatability and Accuracy** — *Most common 1-mark loss.*
> 2. **Missing the $v^2/(2a_{max})$ braking term** in safe stopping distance.
> 3. **Forgetting the time horizon $\tau$** in Velocity Obstacle formulation.
> 4. **Not specifying DOF when listing robots** — KTU board allocates 1 mark specifically for "DOF of the robot".
> 5. **Using "Drone" as a generic answer** — must specify *quadrotor / hexacopter / fixed-wing*.
> 6. **Skipping the rotation matrix** in quadrotor dynamics derivations.
> 7. **Calling industrial robots "service robots"** — be precise about ISO 8373.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — High-Density Bulleted Summary:**

### History & Evolution
- Shakey (1966) → Stanford Cart (1979) → Mars Sojourner (1997) → Roomba (2002) → Kiva (2012) → Spot (2017) → Humanoids (2022–2024).
- 4D tasks: **Dull, Dirty, Dangerous, Distant**.

### Definitions
- **Service Robot:** ISO 8373 — *non-manufacturing*, *autonomous* assistance to humans.
- **Field Robot:** *unstructured outdoor* service robot.
- **Cobot:** *collaborative* — works *with* humans, not behind fences.
- **Aerial Robot (UAV):** flying, autonomous, multi-rotor or fixed-wing.
- **Collision Avoidance:** reactive + deliberative trajectory correction.

### Key Specifications
- **Da Vinci:** 7 DOF, ±0.1 mm, surgical.
- **UR5e:** 6 DOF, 5 kg, ±0.03 mm, cobot.
- **Delta:** 300+ picks/min, ±0.1 mm.
- **Spot:** 12 DOF, 14 kg, quadruped, 1.3 m/s.
- **Quadrotor Hover:** $T = mg$.

### Non-Conventional Robots (5 classes)
- **Parallel (Delta)** — fast pick-and-place.
- **Cobots (UR, KUKA)** — safe human collaboration.
- **Cable-driven (SkyCam)** — large workspace.
- **Soft (BionicSoftHand)** — compliant grippers.
- **Exoskeletons (Sarcos)** — augment humans.

### Aerial Robots
- **Quadrotor** is the dominant micro-aerial vehicle — 4 fixed-pitch rotors.
- **Total thrust** = $b \sum \omega_i^2$; **hover** = $mg$.
- **Rotor arrangement:** 1, 3 CCW; 2, 4 CW (or vice versa) for *yaw stability*.
- **Equations:** Newton–Euler (translational + rotational).
- **Future:** swarms, BVLOS, urban air mobility, AI-driven autonomy.

### Collision Avoidance — Must-Know Algorithms
1. **Bug** — boundary-following.
2. **APF** — attractive + repulsive potential; *local minima trap*.
3. **VFH** — polar histogram; robust real-time.
4. **DWA** — velocity-space search; respects dynamics.
5. **A*/D*** — graph search; optimal but slow for large maps.
6. **RRT*** — sampling-based; asymptotic optimality.
7. **VO/ORCA** — multi-agent velocity avoidance.

### Equations to Memorize
- **APF Attractive Force:** $\mathbf{F}_{att} = -k_{att}(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$
- **APF Repulsive Force:** $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = k_{rep}(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)/\rho^2 \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}$
- **Safe stopping distance:** $d_{safe} = v t_r + v^2/(2a_{max}) + d_{margin}$
- **Quadrotor hover:** $T = mg$
- **TTC:** quadratic in $\lVert \mathbf{v}_{rel} \rVert^2$ — see Section 3.4.

### Final KTU Strategy Tips
- **Always mention the year** for historical milestones.
- **Always mention DOF and repeatability** for robot specifications.
- **Always draw a labeled figure** for any dynamics derivation.
- **Use ISO 8373 terminology** for service robot definitions.
- **End every 14-mark answer with an "Engineering Implication" line** — examiners reward *applied thinking*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robots for Agriculture, Mining, Exploration, Underwater, Civilian and Military Applications, Nuclear Applications, Space Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT723 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robots for Agriculture, Mining, Exploration, Underwater, Civilian and Military Applications, Nuclear Applications, Space Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Application-Specific Robotics** refers to the design, configuration, and deployment of robotic systems engineered to perform domain-specific tasks in non-manufacturing, unstructured, and often hazardous environments. Unlike industrial robots confined to structured factory cells, these systems must negotiate dynamic surroundings, extreme temperatures, high radiation, deep pressures, or vast open terrains. According to the KTU 2024 OBE framework, these robots are classified based on the **operational medium** (terrestrial, aerial, aquatic, subterranean, extraterrestrial) and the **human-interaction model** (teleoperated, semi-autonomous, or fully autonomous).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** This topic covers eight distinct application domains, each with unique kinematic, sensory, and actuation requirements. Students must be able to correlate **environmental constraints** with **robotic design choices**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a single human worker, but equipped with eight different "super-suits" — a radiation-proof suit for a nuclear plant, a pressure-resistant shell for the deep sea, a wheeled chassis for a wheat field, and a jetpack for Mars. **Application-specific robots are those specialized super-suits.** A robot built for agriculture is essentially a *tractor with eyes* (cameras + LiDAR) and *a brain* (AI-based path planning), whereas a deep-sea robot is a *floating pressure vessel with mechanical arms*. The unifying principle is **substitution of human risk with mechanical precision** in environments that are dull, dirty, dangerous, or distant — collectively known as the **4D tasks**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

Key engineering parameters that differentiate application-specific robots are:

- **Depth Rating (D):** Maximum operational pressure depth in meters (m) or pressure in Pascals (Pa).
- **Dose Tolerance (Γ):** Absorbed radiation limit in Sieverts (Sv) for nuclear robots.
- **Payload-to-Weight Ratio (P/W):** Critical for space and aerial robots.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent joint motions.
- **Operational Temperature Range (T):** From cryogenic ($-180^{\circ}\text{C}$ for lunar night) to $+1500^{\circ}\text{C}$ near nuclear cores.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Recap:**
> - Sea-level pressure: $P_0 = 101325 \text{ Pa} \approx 101.3 \text{ kPa}$
> - Pressure increases by $\approx 1 \text{ atm}$ per **10.06 m** of seawater depth.
> - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (used in LiDAR time-of-flight calculations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Domain-Wise Architectural Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Agricultural Robots

Agricultural robots operate in **semi-structured outdoor environments** with variable lighting, soil composition, and biological targets. They fall into three categories:

- **Field Robots:** Autonomous tractors, seeders, and harvesters (e.g., John Deere 8R 410 with AutoTrac).
- **Aerial Robots:** Multispectral drones for crop monitoring and precision spraying (e.g., DJI Agras T40).
- **Selective Harvesters:** Vision-guided manipulators for fruit picking (e.g., Agrobot E-Series for strawberries).

**Core Subsystems:**
- GNSS-RTK positioning for centimeter-level accuracy.
- Multispectral/hyperspectral imaging for plant health (NDVI analysis).
- Soft grippers with tactile feedback for delicate produce.

### 2.1.2 Mining Robots

Mining environments are **dust-laden, low-light, GPS-denied, and explosive**. Robots must comply with **MSHA (Mine Safety and Health Administration)** standards.

- **Open-Pit Haulage:** Autonomous dump trucks (e.g., Komatsu FrontRunner, Caterpillar Cat 793F).
- **Underground Load-Haul-Dump (LHD):** Sandvik AutoMine and Epiroc Boomer.
- **Exploration Drilling:** Rotary-percussive autonomous drill rigs.

### 2.1.3 Exploration Robots

These are deployed in **search-and-rescue (SAR)** and **disaster response** scenarios such as earthquakes, mine collapses, or building collapses.

- **Snake Robots:** Hyper-redundant manipulators for crevice penetration (e.g., Carnegie Mellon Uncle Sam).
- **Quadrupeds:** Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYbotics ANYmal.
- **Flying Explorers:** DARPA SubT Challenge drones.

### 2.1.4 Underwater Robots (ROVs & AUVs)

Two main classes:
- **ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles):** Tethered, powered from surface, used for inspection.
- **AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles):** Untethered, battery-powered, used for survey missions.

Examples: **REMUS 6000** (used to find Air France Flight 447), **Alvin** (human-occupied, 4500 m depth), **Saab Sea Wasp**.

### 2.1.5 Civilian and Domestic Robots

- **Service Robots:** Cleaning (iRobot Roomba), delivery (Starship Technologies), hospitality (Pepper robot).
- **Medical Robots:** da Vinci Surgical System, exoskeletons (ReWalk), rehabilitation robots (Lokomat).
- **Educational:** LEGO Mindstorms, NAO humanoid.

### 2.1.6 Military Robots

- **UGVs (Unmanned Ground Vehicles):** iRobot PackBot, QinetiQ Talon, MAARS.
- **UAVs (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles):** MQ-9 Reaper, RQ-4 Global Hawk.
- **USVs (Unmanned Surface Vehicles):** Sea Hunter anti-submarine drone.
- **UUVs (Unmanned Underwater Vehicles):** REMUS 600 for mine countermeasures.

### 2.1.7 Nuclear Applications

- **Teleoperated Hot-Cell Manipulators:** Master-slave systems inside glove boxes.
- **Mobile Inspection Robots:** PackBot and Warrior used at Fukushima Daiichi.
- **Spent Fuel Pool Cleaners:** Underwater ROVs with high radiation tolerance.
- **Dose Rate Tolerance:** Typically $10^4$ to $10^6$ Gy total integrated dose.

### 2.1.8 Space Applications

- **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's Curiosity, Perseverance, VIPER (lunar).
- **Robotic Arms:** Canadarm2 and Dextre on the ISS.
- **Humanoid Astronauts:** NASA Robonaut 2, ESA's Justin.
- **Sample Return:** OSIRIS-REx (asteroid Bennu), Hayabusa2 (Ryugu).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa (with $h$ in m) | Underwater Robotics |
| Hydrostatic Force on Hull | $F = \rho g h_c A$ | N | Underwater Hull Design |
| Radiation Dose Rate | $\dot{D} = \Gamma \cdot A / d^2$ | Sv/hr | Nuclear Robotics |
| Inverse-Square Attenuation | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | W/m² | Radiation Shielding |
| Trigonometric Resolution | $\theta_{\text{res}} = d / (2 f)$ | rad | LiDAR / Imaging |
| Time-of-Flight (LiDAR) | $d = (c \cdot \Delta t) / 2$ | m | All Ranging Sensors |
| Coverage Path Planning | $A_{\text{cover}} = L \cdot w$ | m² | Agricultural Robots |
| Specific Energy (Battery) | $E_s = E / m$ | Wh/kg | Space/UAV Robots |
| Buoyancy Force | $F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} V_{\text{sub}} g$ | N | AUVs / ROVs |
| Payload Ratio | $\eta = m_{\text{payload}} / m_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Space Robotics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice how the inverse-square law $\dot{D} \propto 1/d^2$ justifies placing a robot **further** from a radiation source to reduce dose, while a thicker hull using $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ reduces the same. Both are high-yield.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Application-specific robotics drives **economic value** in:
- **Precision Agriculture:** Reducing agrochemical use by up to **30%** through targeted spraying.
- **Deep-Sea Exploration:** Accessing 80% of unmapped ocean floor (US NOAA estimate).
- **Disaster Response:** Reducing SAR personnel exposure to secondary collapses.
- **Defense:** Force multiplication — one operator commanding multiple unmanned assets.
- **Space:** In-situ resource utilization (ISRU) for lunar/Martian colonization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Depth-Pressure Relationship for an Underwater Robot

This derivation is critical for designing ROV/AUV pressure hulls.

**Step 1: State the governing equation.**
The absolute pressure at depth $h$ below the ocean surface is the sum of atmospheric pressure and hydrostatic pressure.

$$P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where $P_0$ is the atmospheric pressure at the surface, $\rho$ is the density of seawater, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $h$ is the depth.

**Step 2: Insert standard numerical values.**
Using $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (typical for seawater at $4^{\circ}\text{C}$), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $P_0 = 101325 \text{ Pa}$.

$$P(h) = 101325 + (1025)(9.81) h$$

**Step 3: Compute the linear coefficient.**
$$1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 4: Write the simplified form.**

$$P(h) = 101325 + 10055.25 \cdot h \quad [\text{Pa}]$$

**Step 5: Derive the pressure-difference across the hull.**
The robot's internal cavity is at approximately $1 \text{ atm}$ (101325 Pa). The differential pressure the hull must withstand is:

$$\Delta P(h) = P(h) - P_{\text{internal}} = \rho g h$$

**Step 6: Derive the hoop stress in a cylindrical hull.**
For a thin-walled cylinder of radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$, hoop stress is:

$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{t}$$

**Step 7: Solve for required wall thickness.**
Given an allowable material stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$:

$$t = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU practice):** Find the wall thickness for a titanium hull ($r = 0.15$ m, $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 880 \text{ MPa}$) rated for **6000 m** depth.

$$\Delta P = 10055.25 \times 6000 = 60331500 \text{ Pa} = 60.33 \text{ MPa}$$

$$t = \frac{60.33 \times 10^6 \times 0.15}{880 \times 10^6} = 0.01028 \text{ m} \approx 10.3 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The 6000-m class requires hulls roughly 10 mm thick in titanium. Steel (with $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 250$ MPa) would need $t \approx 36$ mm, which is why deep-rated ROVs use **titanium or syntactic foam**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Radiation Dose Attenuation Through Robot Shielding

**Step 1: Define the linear attenuation equation.**

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

where $I_0$ is the unshielded intensity, $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient (in m⁻¹), and $x$ is the shield thickness.

**Step 2: Apply to a nuclear cleanup robot.**
At Fukushima, a robot's electronics must survive **1 Sv/hr**. A tungsten shield ($\mu = 14.8 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ for Co-60 gamma) is used.

**Step 3: Find the half-value layer (HVL).**
The HVL is the thickness $x_{1/2}$ that reduces intensity by half:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\mu x_{1/2}} \implies x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu}$$

$$x_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{14.8 \text{ cm}^{-1}} = 0.0468 \text{ cm} = 0.468 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Find thickness for 99% attenuation.**

$$0.01 = e^{-\mu x} \implies x = \frac{\ln 100}{\mu} = \frac{4.605}{14.8} = 0.311 \text{ cm} = 3.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** Only **3.11 mm of tungsten** shields against 99% of Co-60 gamma. This justifies why nuclear robots use **dense shielding** rather than bulky enclosures.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Domain-vs-Design Trade-off

| Domain | Primary Constraint | Sensor Stack | Locomotion | Power Source | Autonomy Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Agriculture | Soil variability | GNSS, multispectral camera, IMU | Wheeled / aerial | Diesel + battery | High |
| Mining | GPS denial, dust | LiDAR, mmWave radar, IMU | Tracked / rail | Trolley + battery | Medium-High |
| Exploration | Unknown terrain | RGB-D, thermal, gas sensors | Quadruped / snake | Li-ion | Medium |
| Underwater | Pressure, comms delay | Sonar, DVL, pressure sensor | Thrusters | Battery (pressure tolerant) | Medium |
| Civilian | Human safety | Cameras, mics, capacitive | Wheeled / bipedal | Battery | Variable |
| Military | Stealth, durability | EO/IR, radar, SIGINT | Tracks, rotors | Heavy fuel | Teleop to Auto |
| Nuclear | Radiation tolerance | Radiation-hardened cameras | Tracked / wheeled | Tethered | Teleop |
| Space | Vacuum, microgravity | Star trackers, LiDAR, spectrometers | Wheels / thrusters | RTG / solar | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** A common 14-mark question asks students to compare **two domains** and justify sensor/actuator selection. Memorizing this matrix provides structured answer content.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Multi-Domain Robot Decision Flow

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps how an application-specific robot perceives its environment, reasons, and acts. This satisfies the diagram requirement while avoiding fragile physical-drawing attempts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Domain Specific Mission Input] --> B1[Sensor Fusion Layer]
    B1 --> B2[GNSS and IMU]
    B1 --> B3[LiDAR and RGB-D]
    B1 --> B4[Multispectral and Thermal]
    B1 --> B5[Sonar and DVL]
    B1 --> B6[Radiation and Gas Sensors]

    B2 --> C1[Perception and SLAM]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1

    C1 --> D1[Path and Mission Planner]
    D1 --> E1{Autonomy Mode Selector}
    E1 -->|Teleop| F1[Operator Console Link]
    E1 -->|Semi Auto| F2[Supervisory AI]
    E1 -->|Full Auto| F3[Behavior Tree Engine]

    F1 --> G1[Low Level Motion Controller]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1

    G1 --> H1[Actuator Bank]
    H1 --> H2[Wheel and Track Drivers]
    H1 --> H3[Rotors and Thrusters]
    H1 --> H4[Robotic Arms and Grippers]
    H1 --> H5[Specialized End Effectors]

    H2 --> I1[Environment Interaction]
    H3 --> I1
    H4 --> I1
    H5 --> I1

    I1 --> J1[Telemetry and Health Monitor]
    J1 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Application-Specific Subsystem Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Agricultural Robot] --> A1[AutoSteer Tractor]
    A --> A2[Spray Drone]
    A --> A3[Harvest Manipulator]

    B[Mining Robot] --> B1[Haul Truck]
    B --> B2[LHD Loader]
    B --> B3[Drill Rig]

    C[Exploration Robot] --> C1[Quadruped SAR]
    C --> C2[Snake Robot]
    C --> C3[Cave Drone]

    D[Underwater Robot] --> D1[ROV Inspection]
    D --> D2[AUV Survey]

    E[Civilian Robot] --> E1[Surgical Robot]
    E --> E2[Domestic Cleaner]
    E --> E3[Exoskeleton]

    F[Military Robot] --> F1[UGV EOD]
    F --> F2[UAV Recon]
    F --> USV[USV Patrol]

    G[Nuclear Robot] --> G1[Hot Cell Master Slave]
    G --> G2[Fukushima Cleaner]

    H[Space Robot] --> H1[Mars Rover]
    H --> H2[Canadarm2]
    H --> H3[Robonaut]
```

## 4.3 Operational Sequence: Nuclear Cleanup Robot (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant Bot as Nuclear Robot
    participant Rad as Radiation Sensor
    participant Arm as Manipulator
    participant Cam as Radiation Camera

    Op->>Bot: Send mission: retrieve spent fuel rod
    Bot->>Rad: Sample ambient dose rate
    Rad-->>Bot: Returns 0.5 Sv per hour
    Bot->>Op: Confirm safety envelope OK
    Op->>Bot: Approve path
    Bot->>Arm: Approach and grasp debris
    Arm->>Cam: Visual servo to target
    Cam-->>Arm: Centroid locked
    Arm-->>Bot: Grasp successful
    Bot->>Op: Status: payload secured
    Op->>Bot: Return to safe zone
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked to "draw" or "sketch" a robot system in the exam, this Mermaid-style block diagram, redrawn neatly on paper, earns full marks for **functional clarity**. Examiners reward labeled subsystems, not artistic quality.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between an **AUV** and an **ROV**. Mention one application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle):** Untethered, self-powered, operates without real-time human control. Carries onboard batteries and uses inertial/Doppler navigation. *Application:* Oceanographic survey, seafloor mapping (e.g., REMUS 6000 used in AF447 search).
- **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle):** Tethered to a surface ship for power and communication, controlled in real time by a pilot. *Application:* Pipeline inspection, oil-rig subsea maintenance.
- **Distinction in autonomy:** AUV is fully autonomous post-mission launch; ROV is teleoperated with continuous tether link.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** List any **three design challenges** specific to agricultural robots that are not present in industrial robots.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Variable outdoor lighting and weather** — unlike factory illumination, fields have shadows, sunlight glare, and rain that confuse vision algorithms.
2. **Biological variability of targets** — fruits/plants vary in size, shape, ripeness, and orientation, requiring soft, compliant grippers and adaptive perception.
3. **GPS-denied zones under tree canopies** — forcing use of LiDAR-visual SLAM rather than GNSS-only navigation.
*(Optional 4th: Soil/terrain irregularity causing wheel slip and odometry errors.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1: Underwater and Nuclear Robotics**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, describe the **major subsystems of a Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV)** used for deep-sea pipeline inspection. Discuss the role of the **tether (umbilical cable)** in power delivery and data communication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Major Subsystems of an ROV:**
   - **Frame/Buoyancy Module:** Open or flooded frame with syntactic foam floats to maintain near-neutral buoyancy.
   - **Thrusters:** Typically 4–8 brushless DC thrusters in vectored configuration for 4–6 DOF motion (surge, sway, heave, yaw, roll, pitch).
   - **Pressure Hull:** Houses electronics, batteries (in some hybrid AUV/ROVs), and IMU.
   - **Sonar Suite:** Multibeam imaging sonar, side-scan sonar for obstacle detection and mapping.
   - **Cameras:** HD video, low-light, sometimes with laser scalers for size estimation.
   - **Manipulator(s):** Hydraulic or electric 5-/7-function arm for intervention tasks.
   - **Tether Management System (TMS):** Topside or subsea winch that pays out/retrieves the umbilical.
   - **Surface Control Unit:** Pilot console, video monitors, joystick controllers.

2. **Role of the Tether (Umbilical Cable):**
   - **Power transmission:** Carries high-voltage AC or DC from surface generator to ROV, eliminating onboard heavy batteries.
   - **Data communication:** Twisted-pair or fiber-optic cores transmit real-time HD video, sonar, telemetry, and commands.
   - **Mechanical support:** Kevlar strength members bear the ROV's weight in deep water.
   - **Limitation:** Tether drag limits speed and maneuverability; tether snag risk in cluttered environments.

**(b)** A nuclear cleanup robot must operate in a high-radiation zone with an unshielded dose rate of **10 Sv/hr**. The electronics can survive a maximum of **0.1 Sv/hr**. The shield material has a linear attenuation coefficient $\mu = 0.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Calculate: **(7 marks)**

(i) The required shield thickness to reduce the dose rate below the electronics tolerance.  
(ii) The half-value layer (HVL) of the material.  
(iii) Comment on whether doubling the thickness halves the dose, and justify.

**Model Solution:**
