# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Module 3 Common Loss Points**
> 1. **Mixing up pose update equations:** Students often write $\Delta s = r\Delta\phi$ for *both* wheels separately and forget the **average**. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Confusing hybrid matrix indices:** $H_{11}$ is *not* the transparency coefficient — it is the master's own impedance. Marks lost: 2.
> 3. **Skipping the *admissible* check on $A^*$ heuristic:** Admissibility is the difference between 7/7 and 4/7. Marks lost: 3.
> 4. **Writing unrectified stereo equations:** Triangulation requires rectified stereo. Marks lost: 2.
> 5. **Forgetting the $Z_c$ scale factor in pinhole projection:** Always include $\lambda$ on the LHS. Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **Treating prosthetics and rehabilitation as synonyms:** They differ in *control intent and mechanical attachment*. Marks lost: 1–2.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between unilateral and bilateral teleoperation systems. State one application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks total):**

- **Unilateral teleoperation (1 mark):** Control signals flow in *only one direction* — from the master to the slave. There is *no* force or tactile feedback from the slave to the master operator. The operator commands motion blindly with respect to contact forces. Example: simple bomb-disposal robots where only camera + joystick control is provided.
- **Bilateral teleoperation (1 mark):** Two-way information exchange — position/velocity flows forward from master to slave, and force/tactile information flows back from slave to master. This enables the operator to *feel* the environment.
- **Application example (1 mark):** Bilateral teleoperation is essential in *tele-surgery* (da Vinci Surgical System) where the surgeon must sense tissue stiffness to avoid cutting too deep; unilateral is acceptable in *planetary rover arm control* where round-trip delay makes force feedback destabilizing.

### Question A2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What are the three essential time-domain features commonly extracted from an EMG signal for prosthetic hand control? Briefly state their physical meaning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks total):**

1. **Mean Absolute Value (MAV) — 1 mark:**
$$
\text{MAV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert s_i \vert
$$
It is the average rectified amplitude of the EMG burst; it correlates with the *level of muscle contraction*.

2. **Waveform Length (WL) — 1 mark:**
$$
\text{WL} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert s_{i+1} - s_i \vert
$$
It accumulates the cumulative variation between successive samples; it captures the *complexity* of the muscle firing pattern and is robust to baseline drift.

3. **Zero Crossings (ZC) — 1 mark:** Number of times the EMG signal crosses zero within a window. It is a coarse indicator of the *dominant frequency content* of the muscle activation and is useful for distinguishing fatigue states.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Question (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Both Question Choice A and Question Choice B are complete, alternative 14-mark problems. A KTU student answers **only one** as per the ESE Module internal-choice pattern.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With the necessary block diagram, explain the architecture of a Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) system for a mobile robot. Discuss the role of the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF-SLAM) and identify its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot with wheel radii $r_L = r_R = 0.05$ m and axle length $L = 0.40$ m receives encoder counts corresponding to $\Delta\phi_L = 0.8$ rad and $\Delta\phi_R = 1.6$ rad over one sampling interval. The robot's previous pose was $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t) = (1.0, 2.0, 0.0)$ rad. Compute the updated pose $(x_{t+1}, y_{t+1}, \theta_{t+1})$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) EKF-SLAM Architecture & Limitations — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Sensors LiDAR or Camera] --> p1[Perception Feature Extraction]
    p1 --> da1[Data Association Nearest Neighbor]
    da1 --> pr1[Prediction Step using Motion Model]
    pr1 --> up1[Update Step using Measurement Model]
    up1 --> st1[State Vector x y theta plus Landmarks]
    st1 --> mp1[Map Maintenance]
    mp1 --> pr1
```

**Textual explanation (3 marks):**
- **Prediction (Time Update):** Using the odometry/control input $u_t$, the EKF predicts the new pose via the nonlinear motion model $g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$. The state covariance $P_{t \mid t-1} = G_t P_{t-1 \mid t-1} G_t^\top + Q_t$ propagates uncertainty, where $G_t = \partial g / \partial \mathbf{x}$ is the motion Jacobian.
- **Measurement Update (Correction):** For each observed landmark, compute the innovation $\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \mid t-1})$, the Kalman gain $K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top (H_t P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}$, and update the state $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \mid t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t$. The Jacobian $H_t = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{x}$ linearizes the measurement model about the current estimate.
- **State vector structure:** $\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{x}_v^\top, m_1^\top, m_2^\top, \dots, m_N^\top]^\top$ — vehicle pose plus $N$ landmark positions. The covariance $P$ is therefore $((3+2N) \times (3+2N))$.

**Limitations of EKF-SLAM (1 mark):**
1. Computational complexity $O(N^2)$ per update — does not scale to large maps.
2. Gaussian assumption fails in highly nonlinear environments (kidnapped-robot scenarios, large feature outliers).
3. Sensitive to data association errors — incorrect feature matching corrupts the map.
4. Cannot close large loops reliably because the linearization in the update step introduces inconsistent estimates.

**(b) Pose Update Calculation — 7 marks**

Given: $r_L = r_R = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.40$ m, $\Delta\phi_L = 0.8$ rad, $\Delta\phi_R = 1.6$ rad, $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t) = (1.0, 2.0, 0.0)$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Arc lengths
$\Delta s_L = r_L \Delta\phi_L = 0.05 \times 0.8 = 0.040$ m.
$\Delta s_R = r_R \Delta\phi_R = 0.05 \times 1.6 = 0.080$ m.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Average forward displacement
$\Delta s = (0.040 + 0.080)/2 = 0.060$ m.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Rotation
$\Delta\theta = (0.080 - 0.040)/0.40 = 0.040 / 0.40 = 0.10$ rad.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Midpoint heading
$\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2 = 0.0 + 0.05 = 0.05$ rad.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** $x$-update
$x_{t+1} = 1.0 + 0.060 \cos(0.05) = 1.0 + 0.060 \times 0.99875 = 1.0 + 0.05993 = 1.0599$ m.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** $y$-update
$y_{t+1} = 2.0 + 0.060 \sin(0.05) = 2.0 + 0.060 \times 0.04998 = 2.0 + 0.00300 = 2.0030$ m.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Heading update
$\theta_{t+1} = 0.0 + 0.10 = 0.10$ rad.

**Final answer:** $(x_{t+1}, y_{t+1}, \theta_{t+1}) = (1.060 \text{ m}, 2.003 \text{ m}, 0.100 \text{ rad})$.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, derive the pinhole camera projection equations. Show how the camera intrinsics matrix $K$ is constructed and define every element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectified stereo camera pair has focal length $f = 1200$ pixels and baseline $B = 0.20$ m. A feature is observed at horizontal pixel coordinates $u_L = 412$ and $u_R = 348$. Compute the depth $Z$ of the corresponding 3D point and its $X$-coordinate in the left-camera frame. If the sub-pixel disparity error is $\sigma_d = 0.4$ px, compute the depth uncertainty $\sigma_Z$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Pinhole Camera Derivation — 7 marks**

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wp1[World Point Xw Yw Zw] --> tf1[World to Camera Transform R and t]
    tf1 --> cp1[Camera Frame Point Xc Yc Zc]
    cp1 --> pp1[Pinhole Projection through Optical Centre]
    pp1 --> ip1[Image Plane Point xim yim in metres]
    ip1 --> pn1[Pixel Sampling by Sensor with Pixel Size sx sy]
    pn1 --> pix1[Pixel Coordinates u v]
```

**Derivation (4 marks):**

By similar triangles, with focal length $f$:

$$
x_{im} = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_{im} = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}
$$

Convert metres to pixel units using $s_x, s_y$ pixel size:

$$
u = \frac{x_{im}}{s_x} + c_x = \frac{f}{s_x}\frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x
$$

$$
v = \frac{y_{im}}{s_y} + c_y = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
$$

In homogeneous form:

$$
\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Definition of $K$ elements (1 mark):**
- $f_x, f_y$: focal length in pixel units ($f/s_x$, $f/s_y$).
- $(c_x, c_y)$: principal point — pixel where the optical axis pierces the sensor.
- $s$: skew factor — 0 for modern rectangular pixels.

When including the world-to-camera rigid transform, the full projection matrix is:

$$
P = K [R \mid \mathbf{t}]
$$

**(b) Stereo Depth Calculation — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Disparity
$d = u_L - u_R = 412 - 348 = 64$ px.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Depth
$Z = fB / d = (1200 \times 0.20) / 64 = 240 / 64 = 3.75$ m.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** $X$-coordinate in left-camera frame
For a rectified pair, $X_c = (u_L - c_x) Z / f$. Assuming principal point at image centre $c_x \approx 640$ (typical 1280-px-wide sensor):
$X_c = (412 - 640) \times 3.75 / 1200 = (-228) \times 3.75 / 1200 = -0.7125$ m.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Depth uncertainty
$\sigma_Z = (Z^2 / (fB)) \sigma_d = (3.75^2 / (1200 \times 0.20)) \times 0.4 = (14.0625 / 240) \times 0.4 = 0.0586 \times 0.4 = 0.0234$ m $\approx$ 23.4 mm.

**Final answers:** $Z = 3.75$ m, $X_c = -0.7125$ m, $\sigma_Z = 0.0234$ m $\approx 23.4$ mm.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3**

**Navigation**
- Differential-drive odometry requires *three* updates: linear $\Delta s$, angular $\Delta\theta$, and pose using *midpoint* heading.
- SLAM = Localization + Mapping done *simultaneously*. EKF-SLAM is $O(N^2)$ and fails for large maps; consider Graph-SLAM or FastSLAM.
- A* is admissible iff $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for every node — this guarantee yields optimal paths.
- Unicycle model: $\dot{x}=v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y}=v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta}=\omega$.

**Motion Replication**
- Unilateral = one-way; Bilateral = two-way force feedback.
- Hybrid matrix $H$ has four entries: $H_{11}$ (master impedance), $H_{22}$ (slave impedance), $H_{12}, H_{21}$ (transparency).
- Ideal transparency: $H_{12} = -1$, $H_{21} = 1$.
- Time delay $T$ destabilizes bilateral systems; wave-variable scattering is one robust fix.

**Imaging**
- Pinhole projection: $\lambda \mathbf{x} = K[R \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{X}_w$.
- Intrinsics: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, s$. Extrinsics: $R, \mathbf{t}$.
- Stereo depth: $Z = fB / d$. Depth uncertainty grows *quadratically* with range.
- Visual servoing: IBVS uses image errors directly; PBVS uses pose errors.

**Rehabilitation**
- Impedance control: $F = M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e$.
- Admittance control: opposite direction, safer when patient initiates.
- Two classes: *assistive* (ADL) vs *therapeutic* (recovery).
- Clinical metric: Fugl-Meyer Assessment score for upper-limb recovery.

**Prosthetics**
- EMG band: 20–500 Hz, sampled at $\ge$ 1 kHz.
- Features: MAV, WL, ZC, SSC; classifier: LDA, SVM, CNN.
- Socket pressure must remain $< \mathbf{200}$ **kPa** to prevent tissue breakdown.
- Distinguish prosthesis (replaces missing limb) from orthosis/rehabilitation robot (assists existing limb).

**Cross-Cutting**
- Imaging is the *enabling perception* layer for navigation, replication, rehabilitation, and prosthetics.
- All five subdomains share a real-time constraint envelope (1–100 ms typical) and a *safety-first* design philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State of Art of Robotics in the Field of Healthcare, Minimally Invasive Surgery and Robotic Integration, Surgical Robotic Sub-systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT723 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** State of Art of Robotics in the Field of Healthcare, Minimally Invasive Surgery and Robotic Integration, Surgical Robotic Sub-systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Surgical Robotics** is the application of mechatronic, computer-controlled manipulator systems designed to assist surgeons in performing operative procedures with enhanced precision, dexterity, and minimally invasive access. The state-of-the-art in this field is dominated by **teleoperated master-slave systems** such as the **da Vinci Surgical System (Intuitive Surgical)**, **Mako (Stryker)** for orthopaedics, **ROSA (Zimmer Biomet)** for neurosurgery, and **Hugo (Medtronic)** as a recent laparoscopic competitor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surgical Robot (KTU Definition):** A multi-degree-of-freedom, computer-assisted manipulator that operates under the real-time supervisory control of a clinician, executing surgical tasks (cutting, suturing, cauterising, retracting) either through teleoperation or image-guided automation.

**Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS)** refers to operative procedures performed through small incisions (typically **5–12 mm**) using specialised instruments and an endoscope, rather than the large open incisions of traditional surgery. Robotic integration enhances MIS by eliminating the physiological limitations of the human wrist at the fulcrum point of a long, rigid laparoscopic instrument.

**Surgical Robotic Sub-system** refers to the modular functional blocks of a surgical robotic platform, typically classified into the **Surgeon Console (Master)**, the **Patient-Side Manipulator Cart (Slave)**, the **Vision & Imaging Subsystem**, the **Instrument Drive & End-Effector Subsystem**, and the **Communication & Control Subsystem**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Puppeteer and the Marionette:**
> Imagine a skilled puppeteer controlling a marionette across a thin curtain. The puppeteer (the *surgeon*) sees the puppet through the curtain (the *3D HD endoscope*), pulls strings above (the *master controllers*), and the marionette below (the *patient-side slave arms*) copies every motion — but with smoother, scaled-down, tremor-free movements. The curtain hides the patient's body cavity, the strings filter out shaky hand motion, and the puppet's joints (EndoWrist instruments) can bend in ways the human wrist cannot inside a tiny incision.
>
> This is exactly how surgical robotics works: the surgeon remains *intellectually* in control, while the robot provides *mechanical* super-human precision, dexterity, and stability at the operative site.

### 1.3 Standard Clinical & Engineering Metrics

The following metrics are used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus and standard texts to benchmark surgical robotic systems:

- **Workspace Reach:** **≤ 1.5 m** radius around the patient cart (typical for da Vinci Xi).
- **Degrees of Freedom:** **7 DOF** per instrument arm (pitch, yaw, insertion, roll + 3 wrist joints) — exceeds the **4 DOF** of conventional laparoscopy.
- **Motion Scaling Ratio:** typically **3:1 to 5:1** (a 3 cm surgeon hand movement becomes a 1 cm instrument tip movement).
- **Tremor Filtering Cut-off:** typically **≤ 8 Hz** (physiological hand tremor band).
- **Positioning Accuracy:** sub-millimetre (**< 1 mm** repeatability).
- **3D HD Endoscopic Resolution:** **1080p** standard, with **4K / 8K 3D** in next-gen systems.
- **Latency Budget (Master-to-Slave):** typically **< 80 ms** to feel "transparent".

> [!TIP]
> **Why 7 DOF?** The human arm has 7 DOF in the shoulder-elbow-wrist chain. To replicate the natural dexterity of a surgeon's hand at the end of a long shaft inside the body, 7 DOF is the engineering minimum — 6 for spatial positioning + 1 for orienting the gripper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of State-of-the-Art Surgical Robots

The contemporary landscape can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Level of Autonomy** (Scale: 0 → 5)
2. **Mechanical Architecture** (Serial / Parallel / Continuum)
3. **Clinical Application Domain** (Soft tissue / Hard tissue / Endoluminal / Microsurgery)

#### 2.1.1 Level of Autonomy Scale

| Level | Designation | Description | Clinical Example |
|:------|:------------|:------------|:-----------------|
| **0** | No Autonomy | Pure manual instrument | Conventional laparoscopy |
| **1** | Robot Assistance | Robot holds retractor / scope | AESOP / ViKY voice-controlled scope holder |
| **2** | Task Autonomy | Robot performs a defined sub-task | Mako bone-shaping in knee arthroplasty |
| **3** | Conditional Autonomy | Robot executes suture / tumour resection under surgeon approval | Smart Tissue Autonomous Robot (STAR) bowel anastomosis |
| **4** | High Autonomy | Surgeon supervises; robot plans + executes full procedure | Experimental only (no commercial system) |
| **5** | Full Autonomy | No human intervention | Not ethically / legally approved |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All commercial systems cleared by the US FDA as of 2024 operate at **Level 1 or Level 2**. Level 3 systems exist only in research settings (e.g., Johns Hopkins STAR).

---

### 2.2 Master-Slave Teleoperation: The Operating Principle

The dominant architecture in modern surgical robotics is **bilateral teleoperation with force / position mapping**. The mathematical core is captured in the following relationships.

#### 2.2.1 Forward and Inverse Kinematics Mapping

Let the surgeon hand position in the master console be denoted by the vector $\mathbf{x}_m \in \mathbb{R}^6$ (3 translation + 3 orientation). The slave instrument tip position in the patient-side coordinate frame is $\mathbf{x}_s$.

The **position mapping equation** is:

$$\mathbf{x}_s = \mathbf{x}_{incision} + k_s \cdot \mathbf{R}_{base}\,(\mathbf{x}_m - \mathbf{x}_{m,0})$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}_{incision}$ = the trocar (incision) location (remote centre of motion)
- $k_s$ = the **motion scaling factor** (typically $k_s \in [0.1, 0.5]$)
- $\mathbf{R}_{base}$ = rotation aligning the master frame with the slave base frame
- $\mathbf{x}_{m,0}$ = the master handle home position

The **orientation mapping** preserves roll, pitch, yaw of the wrist:

$$\mathbf{R}_s = \mathbf{R}_{instrument}\,\mathbf{R}_m\,\mathbf{R}_{instrument}^{\,-1}$$

Where $\mathbf{R}_{instrument}$ is the fixed orientation offset due to the instrument shaft passing through the trocar.

#### 2.2.2 The Remote Centre of Motion (RCM) Constraint

The defining kinematic constraint of every surgical robot is the **Remote Centre of Motion (RCM)**. The instrument shaft must pivot about a virtual point coinciding with the trocar incision, so that the abdominal wall is neither compressed nor torn.

Mathematically:

$$\|\mathbf{x}_{pivot} - \mathbf{x}_{trocar}\| \le \epsilon$$

Where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (typically $\epsilon < 0.5\,\text{mm}$). Two engineering realisations exist:

- **Software RCM (sRCM):** Achieved purely through inverse kinematics + Jacobian null-space projection.
- **Hardware RCM (hRCM):** Achieved through a passive mechanical pivot joint (e.g., the da Vinci "Set-up Joint" array).

#### 2.2.3 Force Feedback (Haptics) Equation

The force reflected to the surgeon's hand in a bilateral controller is given by:

$$\mathbf{F}_{master} = k_f \cdot \mathbf{F}_{slave} + B_m \dot{\mathbf{x}}_m$$

Where:
- $k_f$ = **force scaling factor** (often $k_f = 1$ for direct reflection)
- $\mathbf{F}_{slave}$ = force measured by the slave's instrument-tip sensor
- $B_m$ = damping coefficient at the master handle
- $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_m$ = master handle velocity

> [!WARNING]
> The da Vinci system historically **does not provide force feedback** to the surgeon — it relies on **visual force cues** (tissue deformation on the 3D HD screen). Newer entrants (e.g., **Versius by CMR Surgical**, **Hugo by Medtronic**, **Senhance by Asensus**) are adding or planning haptic feedback as a differentiator.

---

### 2.3 Minimally Invasive Surgery vs. Open Surgery vs. Robotic MIS

| Parameter | Open Surgery | Conventional MIS | Robotic MIS |
|:----------|:-------------|:------------------|:-------------|
| Incision size | 10–25 cm | 5–12 mm × 3–5 ports | 5–12 mm × 4–6 ports |
| Surgeon dexterity at site | Full hand | Reduced (fulcrum effect) | Restored (7 DOF wrist) |
| 3D visualisation | Direct eye | 2D monitor | 3D HD / 4K magnified |
| Tremor filtering | None | None | Active (digital filter) |
| Surgeon fatigue | High | High (poor ergonomics) | Low (seated console) |
| Blood loss | High | Moderate | Low |
| Hospital stay | 5–10 days | 3–5 days | 1–3 days |
| Cost (USD) | Base | $1k–3k disposables | $3M robot + $2k/disposable |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|:--------------|:--------|:---------------------|
| $k_s$ | Motion scaling factor (hand-to-tip) | $0.2 - 0.5$ (dimensionless) |
| $k_f$ | Force scaling factor (tissue-to-hand) | $0.5 - 2.0$ (dimensionless) |
| $\tau$ | Master-to-slave latency | $30 - 80$ ms |
| $\epsilon$ | RCM positioning error | $< 0.5$ mm |
| $f_c$ | Tremor-filter cut-off frequency | $6 - 8$ Hz |
| $f_{update}$ | Control loop update rate | $1$ kHz typical |
| $N_{DOF}$ | Instrument degrees of freedom | $7$ |
| $N_{arms}$ | Patient-side manipulator arms | $3$ or $4$ |
| $F_{max}$ | Maximum instrument grip force | $10$ N typical |
| $\rho_{camera}$ | Endoscope resolution | $1080p$ / $4K$ / $8K$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These parameters directly drive the design of manipulator kinematics, choice of motors (harmonic drives for backlash $< 1$ arc-min), and the control sampling rate. Master-to-slave latency $\tau$ is the **single most critical parameter** — above $100$ ms, surgeons experience motion sickness and degraded task performance.

---

### 2.5 The "Why" and "How" of Robotic Integration in the Operating Room

**WHY integrate?**
1. **Dexterity preservation** beyond the trocar fulcrum.
2. **Ergonomic relief** for the surgeon (seated console).
3. **Motion scaling and tremor cancellation** for sub-millimetre accuracy.
4. **Telementoring and tele-surgery** for rural / remote access.
5. **Data capture** for AI-based intra-operative decision support (e.g., Intuitive's "Ion" lung biopsy robot uses AI for catheter navigation).

**HOW it is integrated?**
- **Layer 1 — Hardware:** Patient cart, vision cart, surgeon console, instrument tray.
- **Layer 2 — Networking:** DICOM-compliant imaging, hospital Ethernet for PACS integration.
- **Layer 3 — Software:** Real-time OS (e.g., QNX in some systems), machine vision, instrument telemetry.
- **Layer 4 — Clinical Workflow:** Pre-op planning (CT/MRI segmentation) → intra-op registration → post-op analytics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Design Trade-Off Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Master-Slave Position Mapping with RCM Constraint

We now derive the inverse-kinematics solution for a single patient-side manipulator arm constrained to pivot about the trocar point.

**Step 1 — Problem Setup.**
Define three coordinate frames:
- $\{B\}$: Base frame (fixed to the operating table / patient cart).
- $\{T\}$: Trocar frame, located at $\mathbf{p}_T \in \mathbb{R}^3$.
- $\{E\}$: End-effector (instrument tip) frame, located at $\mathbf{p}_E \in \mathbb{R}^3$.

The kinematic chain of the manipulator is a serial chain of revolute joints producing the forward map:

$$\mathbf{p}_E = f(\mathbf{q}), \quad \mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{DOF}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the RCM Constraint.**

The shaft of the instrument, modelled as a rigid line segment from the trocar $\mathbf{p}_T$ to the end-effector tip $\mathbf{p}_E$, must remain collinear with the trocar-to-tip axis. Equivalently, the displacement vector from the trocar must always remain aligned with the instrument shaft:

$$\mathbf{p}_E - \mathbf{p}_T = d \cdot \hat{\mathbf{u}}_{shaft}$$

where $d$ is the insertion depth and $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{shaft}$ is the unit vector of the instrument shaft.

**Step 3 — Choose a Parametrisation.**
Let the shaft orientation be described by two angles $(\theta, \phi)$, and the insertion depth by $d$. The 3-DOF end-effector pose is then:

$$\mathbf{p}_E = \mathbf{p}_T + d \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta \cos\phi \\ \sin\theta \sin\phi \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Add the Wrist DOF.**
The remaining $n - 3$ joints of the manipulator form the **instrument wrist**. These joints orient the gripper independently of the shaft direction. With a typical $n = 7$ DOF chain, the wrist contributes:

$$n_{wrist} = n_{DOF} - n_{position} - n_{RCM} = 7 - 3 - 1 = 3 \text{ DOF (yaw, pitch, grip)}$$

So the wrist realises the orientation:

$$\mathbf{R}_E = \mathbf{R}_{shaft}(\theta, \phi) \cdot \mathbf{R}_{wrist}(\psi_1, \psi_2, \psi_3)$$

**Step 5 — Combine into the Master Mapping.**
Given the surgeon hand pose $\mathbf{x}_m = (\mathbf{p}_m, \mathbf{R}_m)$, the slave pose is computed as:

$$\mathbf{x}_s = \begin{cases} \mathbf{p}_s = \mathbf{p}_T + k_s \cdot \mathbf{R}_{base}\,(\mathbf{p}_m - \mathbf{p}_{m,0}) \\[4pt] \mathbf{R}_s = \mathbf{R}_{shaft}(\theta_s, \phi_s) \cdot \mathbf{R}_{wrist}(\psi_1^s, \psi_2^s, \psi_3^s) \end{cases}$$

The angles $\theta_s, \phi_s$ are recovered from $\mathbf{p}_s$ via:

$$\theta_s = \arccos\!\left(\frac{p_{s,z} - p_{T,z}}{d_s}\right), \quad \phi_s = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(p_{s,y} - p_{T,y},\ p_{s,x} - p_{T,x}\right)$$

And $d_s = k_s \cdot \|\mathbf{p}_m - \mathbf{p}_{m,0}\|$.

**Step 6 — Verification of Constraint.**
Substituting back, the RCM constraint is automatically satisfied:

$$\|\mathbf{p}_E - \mathbf{p}_T\| = d_s$$

which equals the designed insertion depth, confirming the trocar is the true pivot. No tissue compression or stretching occurs at the incision. **End of derivation.**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choice of Surgical Robot Architecture

When a hospital evaluates a robotic platform, the following trade-off matrix guides the procurement decision:

| Design Criterion | da Vinci Xi | Hugo (Medtronic) | Versius (CMR) | Mako (Stryker) |
|:-----------------|:------------|:-----------------|:---------------|:----------------|
| Primary application | Multi-port soft tissue | Laparoscopic soft tissue | Laparoscopic soft tissue | Orthopaedic bone shaping |
| Autonomy level | 1 (assistive) | 1–2 (assistive + haptic) | 1 (assistive + haptic) | 2 (semi-autonomous bone cut) |
| Open vs proprietary instruments | Proprietary | Modular / open | Modular / open | Proprietary |
| Force feedback | No | Yes (planned) | Yes | No (visual haptic) |
| Setup time | 15–25 min | 10–15 min | 8–12 min | 5–10 min |
| Approx. system cost | \$2.0–2.5 M | \$1.5–2.0 M | \$1.0–1.5 M | \$1.0–1.2 M |
| Per-procedure disposables | \$1.5–3.0 k | \$1.0–2.0 k | \$0.8–1.5 k | \$0.5–1.0 k |
| Imaging integration | Fluorescence, TilePro | Fluorescence | Standard HD | CT-based 3D planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix above is a **KTU-style exam-ready comparison** — students must remember that the **da Vinci** is the *gold-standard reference*, while newer entrants compete on **cost, modularity, and haptic feedback**.

---

### 3.3 Sub-System Functional Block Analysis

A surgical robot is engineered as **five tightly-coupled sub-systems**. Each must be analysed for failure modes, redundancy, and certification.

| Sub-system | Core Function | Key Components | Critical Failure Mode |
|:-----------|:--------------|:----------------|:----------------------|
| **Surgeon Console (Master)** | Surgeon interface, 3D display, hand controls | Open-architecture stereoscopic viewer, master tool manipulators (MTMs), foot pedals, ergonomic seat | Display lag → motion sickness |
| **Patient-Side Cart (Slave)** | Holds and moves the instruments | 3–4 manipulator arms, set-up joints, instrument drives | Arm collision with patient anatomy |
| **Instrument Drive & End-Effector** | Holds and actuates EndoWrist tools | Sterile adapters, cable-driven pulleys, gripper actuators | Cable stretch → gripper position error |
| **Vision & Imaging Sub-system** | Provides magnified 3D view | HD / 4K endoscope, insufflator, light source, image processor | Endoscope fogging, illumination failure |
| **Communication & Control Sub-system** | Real-time data exchange & safety | Real-time OS, redundant Ethernet, watchdog timers, FDA-class lockouts | Latency > 100 ms → unsafe teleoperation |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Workflow of a Robotic Surgical Procedure

**Phase 1 — Pre-operative Planning.**
- Step 1.1: Acquire patient CT / MRI scans.
- Step 1.2: Segment anatomy and define surgical targets.
- Step 1.3: Plan port (trocar) placement to maximise reach and avoid collisions.

**Phase 2 — Patient-side Setup.**
- Step 2.1: Position the patient (Trendelenburg for pelvic, supine for abdominal).
- Step 2.2: Insufflate $\text{CO}_2$ to **12 mmHg** pneumoperitoneum.
- Step 2.3: Place trocars and dock the patient cart.
- Step 2.4: Insert instruments and endoscope, verify RCM at each port.

**Phase 3 — Console-side Operation.**
- Step 3.1: Surgeon sits at console, grips MTMs.
- Step 3.2: Engages master controllers → slave arms follow at scaling $k_s$.
- Step 3.3: Uses foot pedals to switch between camera control, energy, and instrument clutches.
- Step 3.4: Performs dissection, retraction, suturing under tremor-filtered, motion-scaled control.

**Phase 4 — Post-operative Recovery.**
- Step 4.1: Undock robot, close ports.
- Step 4.2: Log telemetry (instrument forces, motion paths) for AI training datasets.
- Step 4.3: Patient recovery: typical hospital stay **1–3 days** vs **5–10 days** open.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master-Slave Surgical Robotic Architecture

The following block diagram captures the complete functional architecture of a state-of-the-art teleoperated surgical robotic system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Surgeon Hand Input] --> A2[Master Tool Manipulator MTM]
    A2 --> A3[Master Encoder and Force Sensor]
    A3 --> A4[Real Time Controller QNX RTOS]
    A4 --> A5[Communication Link Redundant Ethernet]
    A5 --> A6[Slave Motor Drivers Harmonic Drives]
    A6 --> A7[Patient Side Manipulator Arms]
    A7 --> A8[EndoWrist Instrument Tip]
    A8 --> A9[Patient Tissue Operative Site]
    A9 --> A10[Tissue Force Sensor if present]
    A10 --> A5
    A11[3D HD Endoscope Camera] --> A12[Vision Processing Unit]
    A12 --> A13[Stereoscopic Display at Console]
    A13 --> A1
    A14[Safety Watchdog and FDA Interlocks] -.monitors.-> A4
    A15[Foot Pedal Switches Energy Camera Clutch] --> A4
```

### 4.2 Surgical Robot Sub-system Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sys[Surgical Robotic Platform] --> S1[Surgeon Console Master]
    Sys --> S2[Patient Side Cart Slave]
    Sys --> S3[Vision and Imaging Subsystem]
    Sys --> S4[Instrument Drive Subsystem]
    Sys --> S5[Control and Communication Subsystem]
    S1 --> S1a[3D HD Display]
    S1 --> S1b[Master Tool Manipulators]
    S1 --> S1c[Foot Pedal Panel]
    S2 --> S2a[Setup Joints]
    S2 --> S2b[Manipulator Arms 3 to 4]
    S2 --> S2c[Instrument Cannulae]
    S3 --> S3a[Endoscope]
    S3 --> S3b[Light Source]
    S3 --> S3c[Image Processor]
    S4 --> S4a[Sterile Adapters]
    S4 --> S4b[Cable Driven Wrist Actuators]
    S4 --> S4c[Gripper End Effector]
    S5 --> S5a[Real Time OS]
    S5 --> S5b[Safety Interlocks]
    S5 --> S5c[Hospital PACS Interface]
```

### 4.3 Robotic MIS Workflow Integration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Preop CT MRI Scan] --> P2[Anatomy Segmentation and Planning]
    P2 --> P3[Trocar Placement Map]
    P3 --> O1[Patient Preparation and CO2 Insufflation]
    O1 --> O2[Docking Patient Cart and Setting RCM]
    O2 --> O3[Insert Endoscope and EndoWrist Instruments]
    O3 --> O4[Surgeon at Console Engages Master Controllers]
    O4 --> O5[Motion Scaled Tremor Filtered Slave Execution]
    O5 --> O6[Intraoperative Imaging and AI Decision Support]
    O6 --> O7[Suturing and Closure]
    O7 --> Q1[Undock and Patient Recovery]
    Q1 --> Q2[Postop Analytics and Telemetry Logging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture diagram matters for KTU 2024 exams:** A 14-mark question may ask you to "draw and explain the architecture of a master-slave surgical robot." The block flow above gives you the **exact modules, their labels, and the bidirectional feedback paths** you must include to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define minimally invasive surgery (MIS). How does robotic integration enhance MIS compared to conventional laparoscopy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS) is a surgical technique performed through small incisions (typically **5–12 mm**) using a camera (endoscope) and long slender instruments, avoiding the large incisions of open surgery.

Robotic integration enhances MIS in three measurable ways:

1. **Dexterity restoration:** The da Vinci-style 7-DOF EndoWrist instrument restores the **4 DOF lost at the trocar fulcrum** in conventional laparoscopy.
2. **Precision enhancement:** Motion scaling ($k_s \approx 0.2$–$0.5$) and digital tremor filtering ($f_c \approx 8$ Hz) deliver sub-millimetre accuracy.
3. **Ergonomics & visualisation:** The surgeon operates from a seated 3D HD console, eliminating the fatigue and inverted-hand motor skills of conventional MIS.

> **Marking key (KTU):** 1 mark for MIS definition + 2 marks for the three enhancement points (½ each). Full marks only if quantitative values (DOF, scaling factor) are cited.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List any four surgical robotic sub-systems and state the primary function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Surgeon Console (Master):** Provides the 3D HD stereoscopic display and master tool manipulators for the surgeon to issue motion commands.
2. **Patient-Side Manipulator Cart (Slave):** Holds and moves the EndoWrist instruments inside the patient under the surgeon's command.
3. **Vision & Imaging Sub-system:** Houses the endoscope, light source, and image processor to deliver magnified 3D view.
4. **Instrument Drive & End-Effector Sub-system:** Drives the cable-actuated wrist and gripper of the sterile instrument.
5. *(Optional 5th)* **Control & Communication Sub-system:** Real-time OS, redundant Ethernet, and safety interlocks managing master-slave data flow.

> **Marking key:** ½ mark for naming + ¼ mark for function of each of the four sub-systems. Adding a 5th sub-system can earn a bonus mark in some valuation schemes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **master-slave teleoperated surgical robotic system**. Label all sub-systems and describe the bidirectional data flow between surgeon and patient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **position mapping equation** between the master hand pose and the slave instrument tip pose for a 7-DOF surgical manipulator constrained by the **Remote Centre of Motion (RCM)**. Clearly state all symbols and units. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

The architecture consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Surgeon Console (Master)** — Houses the stereoscopic 3D HD viewer, two master tool manipulators (MTMs), and a foot-pedal panel. The surgeon grips the MTMs and views the operative site in magnified 3D.

2. **Patient-Side Cart (Slave)** — A tower holding 3–4 manipulator arms. Each arm has a **set-up joint** (passive positioning) and a **manipulator joint chain** (active, motors drive the instrument through the trocar).

3. **Vision Cart** — Houses the endoscope controller, light source, insufflator, and image processing unit. The endoscope provides dual-channel stereo video.

4. **Control System** — A real-time operating system (e.g., QNX, VxWorks) running at **1 kHz** control loops. Receives master encoder signals, applies motion scaling and tremor filtering, computes slave joint commands, and forwards them via redundant Ethernet.

5. **Bidirectional Flow** —
   - **Forward path:** Hand pose → MTM encoders → controller → slave motor drivers → instrument tip.
   - **Feedback path:** Endoscope → vision processing → console display → surgeon's eyes.
   - **Optional force path:** Tip force sensor (if present) → controller → master actuators → haptic handle.

> **Marking key:** 3 marks for diagram (must include master, slave, vision, control, bidirectional arrows) + 4 marks for the flow explanation.

**(b) Position Mapping Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.1, **Steps 1–6** of the derivation in this note.

Concise version expected in exam:

**Step 1:** Define frames $\{B\}, \{T\}, \{E\}$ for base, trocar, end-effector.

**Step 2:** RCM constraint states $\mathbf{p}_E = \mathbf{p}_T + d\,\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{shaft}$.

**Step 3:** Parametrise using $(\theta, \phi)$ for shaft direction and $d$ for insertion depth.

**Step 4:** Wrist contributes the remaining $7 - 3 - 1 = 3$ DOF.

**Step 5:** Position map:

$$\mathbf{p}_s = \mathbf{p}_T + k_s \cdot \mathbf{R}_{base}(\mathbf{p}_m - \mathbf{p}_{m,0})$$

with $k_s \in [0.2, 0.5]$ dimensionless.

**Step 6:** Orientation map:

$$\mathbf{R}_s = \mathbf{R}_{shaft}(\theta_s, \phi_s) \cdot \mathbf{R}_{wrist}(\psi_1^s, \psi_2^s, \psi_3^s)$$

**Step 7:** Recovery of insertion parameters:

$$d_s = k_s \cdot \|\mathbf{p}_m - \mathbf{p}_{m,0}\|, \quad \theta_s = \arccos\!\left(\frac{p_{s,z} - p_{T,z}}{d_s}\right), \quad \phi_s = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(p_{s,y} - p_{T,y},\ p_{s,x} - p_{T,x}\right)$$

**Step 8:** RCM verification:

$$\|\mathbf{p}_E - \mathbf{p}_T\| = d_s$$

confirming the trocar is the true pivot.

> **Marking key:** 1 mark per step, 1 mark for units / symbol definitions. Diagram of trocar-pivot geometry earns 1 bonus mark.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS)**, **Open Surgery**, and **Robotic MIS** across any **seven engineering / clinical parameters**. Present your answer in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **state-of-the-art commercial surgical robots** — da Vinci, Mako, Hugo, and Versius — in terms of their **level of autonomy**, **clinical application**, **kinematic architecture**, and **haptic feedback capability**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

(Refer to Section 2.3 table in this note — reproduce with 7 chosen parameters. Typical parameters: incision size, dexterity, visualisation, tremor filter, ergonomics, blood loss, hospital stay, cost.)

Sample answer snippet:

| Parameter | Open Surgery | Conventional MIS | Robotic MIS |
|:----------|:-------------|:------------------|:-------------|
| Incision | 10–25 cm | 5–12 mm × 3 ports | 5–12 mm × 4–6 ports |
| DOF at tip | 7 (hand) | 4 (rigid laparoscope) | 7 (EndoWrist) |
| 3D view | Yes (direct) | No (2D monitor) | Yes (3D HD / 4K) |
| Tremor filter | No | No | Yes (<8 Hz) |
| Surgeon ergonomics | Poor (standing) | Poor (stooped) | Excellent (seated) |
| Hospital stay | 5–10 days | 3–5 days | 1–3 days |
| Blood loss | High | Moderate | Low |

> **Marking key:** 1 mark per row, ½ mark for correct quantitative entry, ½ mark for correct unit / category.

**(b) State-of-the-Art Comparison (7 marks):**

| Robot | Autonomy | Application | Architecture | Haptics |
|:------|:---------|:-------------|:--------------|:---------|
| **da Vinci Xi** (Intuitive) | Level 1 (assistive) | Multi-port soft tissue (urology, gynae, thoracic) | 4 slave arms + console, 7-DOF EndoWrist | No native haptics |
| **Mako** (Stryker) | Level 2 (semi-autonomous bone shaping) | Orthopaedic knee / hip arthroplasty | Serial arm with CT-based 3D planning | Visual + auditory haptic cues |
| **Hugo** (Medtronic) | Level 1–2 (assistive + haptic planned) | Laparoscopic soft tissue | Modular open-console arms | Yes (planned) |
| **Versius** (CMR Surgical) | Level 1 (assistive) | Laparoscopic soft tissue | Independent cart-mounted arms | Yes (grip force feedback) |

Discussion highlights (any 4):
1. da Vinci remains the **clinical gold standard** with > 9,000 systems installed worldwide and > 12 million procedures performed.
2. Mako uniquely uses **CT-based pre-operative 3D planning** combined with intra-operative bone registration.
3. Hugo and Versius compete on **cost (30–50% lower)** and **modular open architecture**.
4. Versius and Hugo offer **haptic feedback**, addressing a key da Vinci limitation.
5. All commercial systems remain at **Level 1–2 autonomy** — full autonomy is blocked by regulatory and ethical considerations.

> **Marking key:** 4 marks for the table + 3 marks for the 4 discussion points (¾ mark each).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "degrees of freedom" with "axes"** — Students write "the da Vinci has 4 DOF" because it has 4 arms. The correct statement is "**7 DOF per instrument arm**."
> 2. **Omitting the RCM constraint** — Any answer about surgical robot kinematics that does not mention the **Remote Centre of Motion** is incomplete. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing vague autonomy statements** — Saying "the robot does surgery itself" without referring to the **Level 0–5 autonomy scale** (or equivalent formal metric) loses marks. Always quantify.
> 4. **Forgetting motion scaling and tremor filtering** — These are the two **headline engineering advantages** of robotic MIS. Any answer that omits $k_s$ and $f_c$ is incomplete.
> 5. **Treating surgical robots as "fully autonomous"** — The KTU 2024 syllabus and current regulatory environment require you to state explicitly that **all FDA-cleared systems are Level 1–2**. Examiners deduct heavily for the misconception that robots operate autonomously.
> 6. **Missing the trocars in diagrams** — Any architecture diagram that does not show the **trocar / RCM point** between the slave arm and the patient is marked incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- **Surgical Robot Definition:** Multi-DOF, computer-controlled manipulator supervised by a clinician, operating under teleoperation or image-guided automation.
- **MIS Definition:** Surgery through **5–12 mm incisions** using an endoscope and long instruments.
- **7 DOF Per Arm:** The engineering minimum for restoring wrist dexterity inside the body cavity.
- **RCM (Remote Centre of Motion):** The instrument pivots about the trocar; implemented via **software RCM (sRCM)** or **hardware RCM (hRCM)**.
- **Motion Scaling Factor** $k_s$: typically **0.2–0.5** (hand-to-tip ratio).
- **Tremor Filter Cut-off** $f_c$: typically **< 8 Hz**.
- **Master-Slave Latency** $\tau$: must be **< 80–100 ms** for transparent teleoperation.
- **Control Loop Update Rate:** typically **1 kHz**.
- **Autonomy Levels:** **Level 0** (none) to **Level 5** (full); all commercial systems are **Level 1–2**.
- **State-of-the-Art Robots:** **da Vinci Xi** (gold standard), **Mako** (orthopaedic, Level 2), **Hugo** (Medtronic, haptic), **Versius** (CMR, modular haptic).
- **Five Sub-systems:** Surgeon Console, Patient-Side Cart, Vision & Imaging, Instrument Drive & End-Effector, Control & Communication.
- **Clinical advantages over open:** smaller incision, less blood loss, lower hospital stay (1–3 vs 5–10 days), faster recovery, ergonomic surgeon posture.
- **Key regulatory fact:** US FDA, EU MDR, and India's CDSCO all currently restrict surgical robots to **supervised autonomy** (Level 0–2).
- **Emerging trend:** AI-based intra-operative decision support, image-guided autonomous suturing (STAR robot, Johns Hopkins), and single-port robots (da Vinci SP) are the next frontier.
- **Engineering equation to memorise:**

$$\mathbf{x}_s = \mathbf{p}_T + k_s \cdot \mathbf{R}_{base}(\mathbf{x}_m - \mathbf{x}_{m,0})$$

- **RCM constraint to memorise:**

$$\|\mathbf{p}_E - \mathbf{p}_T\| = d, \quad \text{with } d = k_s \|\mathbf{x}_m - \mathbf{x}_{m,0}\|$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Assistive Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT723 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Assistive Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Assistive Robot (KTU 2024 Definition)**: An **Assistive Robot** is a mechatronic or robotic device — either autonomous, semi-autonomous, or teleoperated — that is purposefully engineered to aid, supplement, or augment the functional capabilities of individuals with physical, sensory, cognitive, or age-related impairments. These robots operate in close physical, social, or task-level cooperation with a human user (the operator/beneficiary) to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADL), Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL), rehabilitation, mobility, manipulation, or medical intervention tasks.

According to the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO) ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** framework, assistive robots are classified as a sub-category of **Personal Care Robots** and form the technological bridge between the medical (prosthetic/rehabilitative) and social (companion/service) robotics domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The 2024 Scheme OERAT723 syllabus explicitly categorizes assistive robots based on the **user-function they support** (mobility, manipulation, rehabilitation, companionship) AND on the **physical coupling** between robot and human (wearable, mobile, fixed-base, or socially embedded). Both classifications must be remembered for Part A and Part B questions.

## 1.2 Classification Taxonomies

Assistive robots are classified along three orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — Functional Purpose:**
- **Mobility Assistance Robots**
- **Manipulation Assistance Robots**
- **Rehabilitation & Therapy Robots**
- **Companion & Socially Assistive Robots (SAR)**
- **Prosthetic & Orthotic Robots**
- **Surgical & Medical Assistance Robots**

**Axis 2 — Physical Coupling Mode:**
- **Wearable / Body-coupled** (e.g., exoskeletons, powered prosthetics)
- **Mobile-coupled** (e.g., intelligent wheelchairs, mobile manipulators)
- **Fixed-base / Environment-coupled** (e.g., robotic kitchen, smart home manipulators)
- **Socially-coupled / Embodied** (e.g., Paro seal, Pepper)

**Axis 3 — Autonomy Level (per ISO 13482):**
- **Level 0** — No autonomy (teleoperated)
- **Level 1** — Robot-assisted (shared control)
- **Level 2** — Task-level autonomy
- **Level 3** — Full autonomy with safety guarantees

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Car of Human Abilities"**
> 
> Imagine if a person temporarily lost the ability to lift, walk, or see. Just as a car compensates for the human inability to run at 100 km/h, an **assistive robot compensates for a lost or weakened human capability**. The "type" of assistive robot is simply the answer to the question: *"Which human ability is being restored or amplified?"*
> 
> - **Cannot walk?** → Mobility assistive robot (wheelchair / exoskeleton)
> - **Cannot grip?** → Manipulation assistive robot (arm / prosthetic hand)
> - **Recovering from stroke?** → Rehabilitation robot (therapy device)
> - **Lonely / dementia?** → Companion robot (Paro, Pepper)
> 
> Think of assistive robots as **"specialized tools"** — each type is a custom tool designed for one specific human limitation, much like a screwdriver cannot replace a hammer.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **bold** constants and metrics are standard in assistive robotics and frequently appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions:

- **Standard Human Body Dimensions (Anthropometry):**
  - Standing height: **1.70 m (male) / 1.60 m (female)**
  - Arm reach (functional): **~0.60–0.75 m**
  - Body weight: **70 kg (male) / 60 kg (female)**
  - Maximum safe payload for arm exoskeleton: **~5–10 kg** per limb

- **Standard Performance Metrics:**
  - **DOF** (Degrees of Freedom) — typically **6–14 DOF** for upper-limb, **12 DOF** for lower-limb exoskeletons
  - **Repeatability** of surgical robots: **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**
  - **Wheelchair speed** (safe indoor): **≤ 0.6 m/s**
  - **Battery autonomy**: **4–8 hours** for mobile assistive platforms
  - **Interaction force** (safe upper limit, pHRI): **≤ 10 N** continuous, **≤ 50 N** transient

- **Standard Communication Protocols:**
  - **ROS (Robot Operating System)** — middleware
  - **CAN bus / EtherCAT** — real-time control bus
  - **Bluetooth LE / ZigBee** — wearable sensor networks

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Functional Analysis of Each Type

### 2.1.1 Mobility Assistance Robots (MARs)

These robots substitute or augment **locomotion**. They include **Intelligent Wheelchairs**, **Autonomous Mobility Aids (AMAs)**, and **Walking Assistants** (e.g., **Cody**, **Care-O-bot**). The robot must solve three coupled problems:
- **Localization** (where am I?)
- **Path planning** (how to reach the goal?)
- **Obstacle avoidance** (what is safe to traverse?)

Operating Principle — uses a **2D LiDAR + depth camera + IMU** sensor stack. Control loops run at **50–100 Hz** for safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: Wheelchair robotics questions frequently test **shared control** — the mathematical blending of user intent (joystick/BCI) with robot autonomy.

### 2.1.2 Robotic Wheelchairs & Smart Walkers

A **robotic wheelchair** adds:
- **Active obstacle detection** (ultrasonic + vision)
- **Shared-control navigation** (user steers intent → robot resolves path)
- **Voice/BCI command interface** for users with hand impairment

**Shared Control Law (KTU high-yield):**

$$
u(t) = \alpha \cdot u_{\text{user}}(t) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot u_{\text{robot}}(t)
$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = final control command
- $u_{\text{user}}(t)$ = user input (joystick / EEG)
- $u_{\text{robot}}(t)$ = autonomous controller output
- $\alpha$ = blending factor ($\alpha \in [0, 1]$)

When $\alpha = 1$ → **full user control**; when $\alpha = 0$ → **full autonomy**; intermediate values yield **shared control** — the gold standard for safety.

### 2.1.3 Rehabilitation & Therapy Robots

Used for **post-stroke**, **spinal cord injury (SCI)**, and **cerebral palsy** therapy. Operate on three control paradigms:

- **Assist-as-Needed (AAN)**: Robot provides force only when patient cannot complete the motion
- **Impedance Control**: Robot behaves as a programmable spring-damper
- **Haptic Guidance**: Robot physically leads the limb along a desired trajectory

**Impedance Control Equation (mass-spring-damper model):**

$$
M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}
$$

where:
- $M$ = virtual mass (kg)
- $B$ = virtual damping coefficient (Ns/m)
- $K$ = virtual stiffness (N/m)
- $x_d$ = desired trajectory
- $F_{\text{ext}}$ = external force from patient

**Examples**: **InMotion ARM (Bionik)**, **Lokomat (Hocoma)**, **Armeo Power (Hocoma)**.

### 2.1.4 Exoskeletons (Wearable Robots)

**Exoskeletons** are rigid, joint-aligned, externally worn robotic structures that act in **parallel with the human limb** to amplify strength or restore mobility. They are classified by body region:

| Body Region | Example Systems | Primary Application |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Lower-limb full | ReWalk, Ekso GT, HAL (Cyberdyne) | SCI, paraplegia |
| Lower-limb single | AnkleBot, LOPES | Stroke gait rehab |
| Upper-limb | MyoPro, Armeo Spring | Stroke, MS, weakness |
| Full-body | BLEEX (Berkeley), Human Universal Load Carrier | Military, industrial lift assist |
| Hand | CyberGlove, Festo ExoHand | Grasp restoration |

**Actuation Equation for Powered Joint (KTU high-yield):**

$$
\tau_m = J^T(\theta) \cdot F_{\text{ext}} + (I + M)\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + G(\theta)
$$

where:
- $\tau_m$ = motor torque (Nm)
- $J(\theta)$ = manipulator Jacobian
- $F_{\text{ext}}$ = external force from environment/load
- $I$ = link inertia
- $M$ = motor inertia
- $B$ = damping
- $G(\theta)$ = gravity compensation term

### 2.1.5 Prosthetic Robots

Unlike exoskeletons (parallel), **prosthetic robots are in series with the residual limb** — they REPLACE a missing segment. Modern prosthetics use:
- **Myoelectric control** — surface EMG (sEMG) signals drive motor
- **Targeted Muscle Reinnervation (TMR)** — rerouted nerves amplify EMG
- **Pattern recognition** — machine learning decodes muscle intent

**EMG-to-Force Mapping (linear model):**

$$
F_{\text{motor}} = k \cdot \text{RMS}(\text{EMG}(t)) + F_0
$$

where $k$ = gain (N/V), $F_0$ = offset. Practical example: **DEKA Arm (DARPA)**, **LUKE Arm (Mobius Bionics)**, **i-Limb (Össur)**.

### 2.1.6 Socially Assistive Robots (SAR) & Companion Robots

SARs provide **cognitive, emotional, and social support** without physical contact. They assist users with **dementia, autism, or loneliness**.

- **Paro** (Japan) — therapeutic baby seal robot
- **Pepper / NAO** (SoftBank) — humanoid companion
- **AIBO** (Sony) — robotic dog
- **KASPAR** (Hertfordshire) — autism therapy

Key sensing modalities: **speech recognition, facial expression, gaze tracking**.

### 2.1.7 Surgical Assistance Robots

These are **teleoperated or shared-control manipulators** used in minimally invasive surgery:
- **Da Vinci Surgical System (Intuitive Surgical)** — gold standard
- **Mako (Stryker)** — orthopedic
- **Rosa (Zimmer Biomet)** — neurosurgery
- **Hugo (Medtronic)** — laparoscopic

They employ **master-slave teleoperation** with **scaling factor** $k$ (typically 1:3 to 1:10), meaning the surgeon moves 3 cm and the slave moves 1 cm — translating gross hand motion into fine surgical motion.

**Position Scaling Equation:**

$$
x_{\text{slave}} = k \cdot x_{\text{master}}, \quad k \in [0.1, 0.3]
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — it covers 80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Shared control blending | $u(t) = \alpha \cdot u_{\text{user}}(t) + (1-\alpha) \cdot u_{\text{robot}}(t)$ | dimensionless | Wheelchair & BCI control |
| 2 | Impedance control | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x-x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}$ | N, m, s | Rehab robots |
| 3 | Exoskeleton torque | $\tau_m = J^T F_{\text{ext}} + (I+M)\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + G(\theta)$ | Nm | Exoskeleton design |
| 4 | EMG-to-force | $F = k \cdot \text{RMS}(\text{EMG}) + F_0$ | N | Prosthetic control |
| 5 | Teleoperation scaling | $x_{\text{slave}} = k \cdot x_{\text{master}}$ | m | Surgical robots |
| 6 | Master-slave force reflection | $F_{\text{master}} = -k_f \cdot F_{\text{slave}}$ | N | Haptic feedback |
| 7 | Safe interaction force | $F_{\max} \le 50$ N transient, $10$ N continuous | N | ISO/TS 15066 safety |
| 8 | DOF count (human arm) | $n_{\text{DOF}} = 7$ (3 shoulder + 1 elbow + 3 wrist) | — | Manipulator design |
| 9 | Bounded autonomy level | $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ | — | Wheelchair/cobot |
| 10 | Repeatability (surgical) | $R \le \pm 0.1$ mm | mm | Da Vinci class |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Fields

- **Medical Industry**: ~**$11 billion** global market for surgical and rehabilitation robots (2024 estimate)
- **Aging Population**: WHO projects **1.4 billion** people aged 60+ by 2030 → massive SAR demand
- **Disability Inclusion**: UN reports **~1.3 billion** people (16% of world) experience significant disability — direct target population
- **Defense/Industrial**: Exoskeletons reduce metabolic cost of load carriage by **~15–30%**
- **Space & Remote Surgery**: Telerobotics enables intercontinental surgery (Lindbergh Operation, 2001)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shared Control Blending Law for Robotic Wheelchair

**Given scenario**: A quadriplegic user provides joystick input scaled to $[-1, +1]$. The autonomous controller (driven by a SLAM algorithm) produces a collision-avoidance velocity vector. We must derive the optimal blending that guarantees the user **never collides** with an obstacle.

### Step 1 — Define control space

Let the **linear velocity** $v(t)$ and **angular velocity** $\omega(t)$ be the wheelchair state variables. Define:

$$
v_{\text{user}}(t) = v_{\max} \cdot u_v(t), \quad \omega_{\text{user}}(t) = \omega_{\max} \cdot u_\omega(t)
$$

where $u_v, u_\omega \in [-1, +1]$ are the user's normalized joystick commands, and $v_{\max} = 0.6$ m/s, $\omega_{\max} = 1.0$ rad/s are saturation limits.

**Conversion logic**: The user input is mapped from a percentage (0–100% joystick deflection) to physical velocities in m/s and rad/s. This linear mapping is the simplest, but in practice a dead-zone + cubic mapping is used to compensate for tremor.

### Step 2 — Define the robot's autonomous contribution

The autonomous controller (running a Dynamic Window Approach — DWA) outputs:

$$
v_{\text{robot}}(t) = v_{\text{DWA}}(t), \quad \omega_{\text{robot}}(t) = \omega_{\text{DWA}}(t)
$$

These are *safe* velocities guaranteed not to collide in the current planning window $\Delta t = 0.5$ s.

### Step 3 — Apply the blending law

$$
v(t) = \alpha \cdot v_{\text{user}}(t) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot v_{\text{robot}}(t)
$$

$$
\omega(t) = \alpha \cdot \omega_{\text{user}}(t) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \omega_{\text{robot}}(t)
$$

**Conversion logic**: The wheelchair's actual velocity is a weighted sum of the user's intent and the robot's safe override. The weight $\alpha$ determines the balance.

### Step 4 — Compute time-to-collision (TTC) and adapt $\alpha$

To guarantee safety, we adapt $\alpha$ as a function of the time-to-collision $\tau_{\text{TTC}}$:

$$
\alpha(\tau_{\text{TTC}}) = \begin{cases} 1, & \tau_{\text{TTC}} > \tau_{\text{safe}} \quad (\text{free space — full user control}) \\ \dfrac{\tau_{\text{TTC}}}{\tau_{\text{safe}}}, & 0 < \tau_{\text{TTC}} \le \tau_{\text{safe}} \quad (\text{gradual handover}) \\ 0, & \tau_{\text{TTC}} \le 0 \quad (\text{imminent collision — full robot takeover}) \end{cases}
$$

where $\tau_{\text{safe}} = 1.5$ s is the safety threshold (a tunable engineering parameter).

**Conversion logic**: When far from obstacles, the user is in full control. As an obstacle approaches, the robot gradually takes over to prevent collision. The smooth linear handover prevents jerky transitions.

### Step 5 — Saturation guard

The blended command must respect the actuator limits:

$$
v(t) = \text{clip}\bigl(v(t),\, -v_{\max},\, +v_{\max}\bigr)
$$

$$
\omega(t) = \text{clip}\bigl(\omega(t),\, -\omega_{\max},\, +\omega_{\max}\bigr)
$$

**Final resulting behavior**: A safe, responsive wheelchair that respects the user's autonomy *and* prevents collisions — this is the engineering essence of shared control.

## 3.2 Derivation: Impedance Control for a Rehabilitation Robot

**Goal**: Derive the control law such that the robot behaves as a programmable spring-damper to a patient's limb.

### Step 1 — Define the target impedance model

In the **Cartesian task space**, the desired interaction dynamics are:

$$
M_d (\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d) + B_d (\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K_d (x - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}}
$$

where $x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}$ are the actual end-effector position, velocity, acceleration, and $F_{\text{ext}}$ is the force exerted by the patient on the robot handle.

**Conversion logic**: This is a virtual mass-spring-damper; the "error" $(x - x_d)$ is how far the patient has strayed from the ideal trajectory, and the robot applies a restoring force proportional to it.

### Step 2 — Solve for the desired acceleration

Rearrange to isolate the desired acceleration:

$$
\ddot{x} = \ddot{x}_d + \frac{1}{M_d}\Bigl[F_{\text{ext}} - B_d (\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) - K_d (x - x_d)\Bigr]
$$

**Conversion logic**: The robot's "target acceleration" combines (a) the trajectory's nominal acceleration, (b) a term that responds to patient-applied force, and (c) spring-damper restoring forces toward the trajectory.

### Step 3 — Convert to joint space via inverse dynamics

Using the **task-space formulation**:

$$
\tau = J^T(q)\bigl[M_x(\ddot{x}) + C_x(\dot{x}, x) + G_x - F_{\text{ext}}\bigr]
$$

**Conversion logic**: To track the desired Cartesian acceleration in joint space, the controller computes the joint torques needed using the Jacobian $J(q)$, the task-space inertia matrix $M_x$, Coriolis/centrifugal term $C_x$, and gravity term $G_x$.

### Step 4 — Assist-as-Needed (AAN) modification

For rehabilitation, we modify $K_d$ dynamically:

$$
K_d = K_{\max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\vert e_{\text{voluntary}} \vert}{\vert e_{\text{total}} \vert}\right)
$$

where $e_{\text{voluntary}}$ is the patient's own motion contribution and $e_{\text{total}}$ is the total error.

**Conversion logic**: If the patient is doing most of the work ($e_{\text{voluntary}} \approx e_{\text{total}}$), the stiffness drops, and the robot provides minimal assistance. If the patient is passive ($e_{\text{voluntary}} \approx 0$), stiffness is maximal, and the robot completes the motion. This embodies the **"Assist-as-Needed"** principle — essential for motor relearning.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Wheelchair | Exoskeleton | Prosthetic | Surgical | Companion |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|------------|----------|-----------|
| Coupling | Mobile | Wearable | Wearable | Teleoperated | Socially-coupled |
| DOF typical | 2–4 | 6–14 | 5–7 | 6–10 | 5–12 |
| Power source | Battery | Battery | Battery | Mains | Battery |
| Latency requirement | 50–100 ms | 10–20 ms | 5–10 ms | < 1 ms | 100–500 ms |
| Safety standard | ISO 13482 | ISO 13482 | ISO 13482 | IEC 60601 | ISO 13482 |
| Autonomy | Shared | High | High | Tele + shared | High |
| Cost (USD) | 5K–50K | 30K–150K | 20K–100K | 0.5M–2.5M | 5K–20K |
| Control freq. | 50 Hz | 1 kHz | 1 kHz | 1–3 kHz | 10–30 Hz |
| Sensor set | LiDAR, cam | IMU, EMG, encoders | EMG, force | Force, vision | Cam, mic, IR |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Wheelchair Shared Control

**Problem**: A user with $u_v = 0.8$, $u_\omega = -0.5$. The DWA controller outputs $v_{\text{DWA}} = 0.3$ m/s, $\omega_{\text{DWA}} = 0.2$ rad/s. The current $\tau_{\text{TTC}} = 1.0$ s, and $\tau_{\text{safe}} = 1.5$ s. Find the wheelchair's actual velocities.

**Solution**:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\tau_{\text{TTC}}}{\tau_{\text{safe}}} = \frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.667
$$

$$
v_{\text{user}} = 0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.48 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{\text{user}} = 1.0 \times (-0.5) = -0.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
v(t) = 0.667 \times 0.48 + (1 - 0.667) \times 0.3 = 0.320 + 0.100 = 0.420 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega(t) = 0.667 \times (-0.5) + (1 - 0.667) \times 0.2 = -0.334 + 0.067 = -0.267 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Interpretation**: Because an obstacle is 1 second away, the robot reduces the user's forward speed (from 0.48 to 0.42 m/s) and dampens the turn, balancing safety and user intent.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Assistive Robot

Below is the generic **functional architecture flow** that is common to almost all assistive robot types. It maps **Perception → Decision → Action → Feedback** with safety interlocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B1 --> C1[Trajectory Planner\nDWA / RRT / A-Star]
    C1 --> D1[Safety Supervisor\nISO 13482 Compliant]
    D1 --> E1[Low-Level Motion Controller\nPID / Impedance / Computed Torque]
    E1 --> F1[Actuator Stage\nDC Motor / Servo / Hydraulic]
    F1 --> G1[Physical Interaction\nWheelchair / Exosuit / Manipulator]
    G1 --> H1[Environment Feedback\nForce / Vision / LiDAR]
    H1 --> I1[State Estimator\nEKF / Particle Filter]
    I1 --> C1
    H1 --> D1
    A2[Companion Robot Subsystem\nEmotion Detection] -.-> B1
    A2 -.-> D1
```
